Abstract: Engineering problem solving is central to real-world decision-making, requiring mathematical formulations that not only represent complex problems but also produce feasible solutions under data and physical constraints. Unlike mathematical problem solving, which operates on predefined formulations, engineering tasks demand open-ended analysis, feasibility-driven modeling, and iterative refinement. Although large language models (LLMs) have shown strong capabilities in reasoning and code generation, they often fail to ensure feasibility, which limits their applicability to engineering problem solving. To address this challenge, we propose EngiAgent, a multi-agent system with a fully connected coordinator that simulates expert workflows through specialized agents for problem analysis, modeling, verification, solving, and solution evaluation. The fully connected coordinator enables flexible feedback routing, overcoming the rigidity of prior pipeline-based reflection methods and ensuring feasibility at every stage of the process. This design not only improves robustness to diverse failure cases such as data extraction errors, constraint inconsistencies, and solver failures, but also enhances the overall quality of problem solving. Empirical results across four representative domains demonstrate that EngiAgent achieves substantial improvements in feasibility compared to prior approaches, establishing a new paradigm for feasibility-oriented engineering problem solving with LLMs. Our source code and data are available at https://github.com/AI4Engi/EngiAgent.

</details>


### [89] [Distilling Long-CoT Reasoning through Collaborative Step-wise Multi-Teacher Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02290)
*Taewon Yun,Jisu Shin,Jeonghwan Choi,Seunghwan Bang,Hwanjun Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces CoRD, a framework to distill reasoning models using collaborative multi-teacher decoding to enhance reasoning synthesis and reduce redundancy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Distilling large-scale reasoning models is necessary to enable practical Long-CoT reasoning due to prohibitive computational costs of full-scale inference.

Method: The proposed CoRD framework utilizes predictive perplexity-based scoring and beam search to collaboratively synthesize reasoning among heterogeneous LRMs, preserving diverse and high-potential hypotheses.

Result: CoRD demonstrates the ability to generate high-quality reasoning data, achieve near teacher-level performance with reduced supervision signals, and generalize effectively to out-of-domain tasks.

Conclusion: CoRD improves the efficiency of distilling reasoning models while maintaining performance standards, offering a practical and generalized solution for reasoning synthesis.

Abstract: Distilling large reasoning models is essential for making Long-CoT reasoning practical, as full-scale inference remains computationally prohibitive. Existing curation-based approaches select complete reasoning traces post-hoc, overlooking collaboration among heterogeneous teachers and lacking dynamic exploration, which leads to redundant sampling and missed complementary reasoning. We introduce CoRD, a collaborative multi-teacher decoding framework that performs step-wise reasoning synthesis guided by predictive perplexity-based scoring and beam search. This enables heterogeneous LRMs to jointly construct coherent reasoning trajectories while efficiently preserving diverse, high-potential hypotheses. Experiments show that CoRD produces higher-quality reasoning data and achieves near teacher-level student performance with fewer, structured supervision signals, without substantial efficiency overhead. CoRD further generalizes well to out-of-domain and open-ended settings. The dataset and model are available at \href{https://github.com/DISL-Lab/CoRD}{https://github.com/DISL-Lab/CoRD}.

</details>


### [90] [Anon: Extrapolating Optimizer Adaptivity Across the Real Spectrum](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02317)
*Yiheng Zhang,Kaiyan Zhao,Shaowu Wu,Yiming Wang,Jiajun Wu,Leong Hou U,Steve Drew,Xiaoguang Niu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Anon, a novel optimizer offering tunable adaptivity to bridge the generalization gap between adaptive (e.g., Adam) and non-adaptive (e.g., SGD) optimizers while delivering superior performance across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the generalization performance gap between adaptive and non-adaptive optimization methods, which limits their applicability across diverse model architectures and tasks.

Method: The authors propose Anon, an optimizer with tunable adaptivity via a mechanism called Incremental Delay Update (IDU) to ensure theoretical convergence and robustness across varying levels of adaptivity.

Result: Anon demonstrates empirical superiority over existing optimizers like Adam and SGD on tasks including image classification, diffusion, and language modeling, while ensuring theoretical convergence guarantees.

Conclusion: Anon provides a unified framework blending benefits of adaptive and non-adaptive optimizers, proving adaptivity can be a key tunable design property in optimization algorithms.

Abstract: Adaptive optimizers such as Adam have achieved great success in training large-scale models like large language models and diffusion models. However, they often generalize worse than non-adaptive methods, such as SGD on classical architectures like CNNs. We identify a key cause of this performance gap: adaptivity in pre-conditioners, which limits the optimizer's ability to adapt to diverse optimization landscapes. To address this, we propose Anon (Adaptivity Non-restricted Optimizer with Novel convergence technique), a novel optimizer with continuously tunable adaptivity in R, allowing it to interpolate between SGD-like and Adam-like behaviors and even extrapolate beyond both. To ensure convergence across the entire adaptivity spectrum, we introduce incremental delay update (IDU), a novel mechanism that is more flexible than AMSGrad's hard max-tracking strategy and enhances robustness to gradient noise. We theoretically establish convergence guarantees under both convex and non-convex settings. Empirically, Anon consistently outperforms state-of-the-art optimizers on representative image classification, diffusion, and language modeling tasks. These results demonstrate that adaptivity can serve as a valuable tunable design principle, and Anon provides the first unified and reliable framework capable of bridging the gap between classical and modern optimizers and surpassing their advantageous properties.

</details>


### [91] [ANO: A Principled Approach to Robust Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02320)
*Yiheng Zhang,Yiming Wang,Kaiyan Zhao,Zhenglin Wan,Jiayu Chen,Leong Hou U*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Anchored Neighborhood Optimization (ANO) within a Unified Trust Region Framework, offering improved sample efficiency and robustness over PPO and SPO.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the sample inefficiency and instability caused by the hard clipping in PPO and unbounded gradients in SPO.

Method: The Unified Trust Region Framework generalizes objectives, and ANO is derived based on design principles like the Redescending Influence Principle for dynamic outlier suppression.

Result: ANO delivers state-of-the-art performance on MuJoCo benchmarks, outperforming PPO and SPO in both effectiveness and stability.

Conclusion: ANO is a robust optimization method with theoretical minimal complexity, achieving stability under aggressive hyperparameters and solving fundamental issues in deep RL.

Abstract: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) dominates deep RL but faces a fundamental dilemma. Its "hard clipping" mechanism discards valuable gradient information from outliers, leading to sample inefficiency. Conversely, removing clipping (as in SPO) exposes optimization to unbounded gradients, causing significant instability and hyperparameter sensitivity. To resolve this, we establish a Unified Trust Region Framework that generalizes existing objectives. Within this framework, we derive Anchored Neighborhood Optimization (ANO) based on a set of design principles. We identify that the failure of standard policy gradients stems from a misapplication of gradient influence on outliers. We propose the Redescending Influence Principle, a paradigm shift from monotonic penalties (SPO) and hard-thresholding (PPO) to dynamic outlier suppression, and prove its necessity for stability in high-variance stochastic optimization. Theoretically, we prove ANO possesses the minimal structural complexity required for robust optimization. Empirically, ANO achieves state-of-the-art performance on MuJoCo benchmarks, significantly outperforming PPO and SPO. Notably, ANO demonstrates superior stability, preventing policy collapse even under aggressive hyperparameters (e.g., learning rates 3x larger than standard) where PPO fails completely.

</details>


### [92] [A Compound AI Agent for Conversational Grant Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02366)
*Zhisheng Tang,Mayank Kejriwal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents an AI system to simplify research funding discovery by centralizing access to diverse funding opportunities and enabling efficient, conversational searches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fragmentation in research funding discovery, which requires researchers to navigate multiple disjointed portals and systems, motivates the need for a unified approach.

Method: The paper describes a two-part AI system: an aggregation layer to gather and normalize funding data, and a ReAct-based query processing layer that performs hybrid searches and supports interactive, conversational queries.

Result: The system reduces grant discovery time from 30--45 minutes to under 10 minutes and is actively used by over 3,000 users, showcasing its effectiveness.

Conclusion: The proposed compound AI system offers a feasible approach to centralize and streamline the process of discovering funding opportunities, significantly saving researchers' time and effort.

Abstract: Research funding discovery remains fundamentally fragmented: researchers navigate disparate agency portals (e.g., in the United States, NSF, NIH, DARPA, Grants.gov, and many others) with heterogeneous interfaces, search capabilities, and data schemas. We present a compound AI system that unifies this landscape through two tightly coupled components: (1) an aggregation layer that autonomously collects, normalizes, and indexes almost 12,000 federal and nonprofit opportunities from fragmented sources via LLM-equipped browser agents, maintaining a biweekly-updated unified database; and (2) an agentic ReAct-based query processing layer that interprets research context (including from PDF documents) and employs hybrid search combining a structured index with selective web search to retrieve relevant opportunities - while avoiding LLM hallucination. The conversational interface supports iterative refinement through multi-turn interactions, allowing researchers to progressively apply constraints without reformulating their core research description. Results stream in real time with full transparency of intermediate reasoning, enabling appropriate calibration of user trust. Currently used by almost 3,000+ users, our approach demonstrates the feasibility of compound AI in reducing grant discovery time from 30--45 minutes (manual, fragmented portal searches) to under 10 minutes (unified, conversational search).

</details>


### [93] [Controllable and Verifiable Process Data Synthesis for Process Reward Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02395)
*Yinghui Chi,Lucien Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a framework to generate controllable and verifiable synthetic process supervision data for Process Reward Models (PRMs), addressing limitations in error control and trajectory consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to overcome challenges in existing PRM data construction methods, including limited control over error characteristics and a lack of trajectory consistency.

Method: The proposed framework generates data by creating correct reasoning chains, injecting controlled template-aware errors at intermediate steps, recomputing subsequent steps under the corrupted state, and verifying the error's derivability constraints.

Result: Synthetic data improved logical and mathematical reasoning benchmarks, increased performance in step classification tasks, and highlighted nuanced difficulties in error localization.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates the importance of fine-grained, verifiable process supervision in enhancing PRM training and evaluation outcomes.

Abstract: Process reward models (PRMs) rely on high-quality process supervision data, yet existing construction methods often provide limited control over error location, error type, and trajectory consistency. We propose a controllable and verifiable framework for synthesizing process supervision data for PRMs. Our framework first constructs a correct symbolic reasoning chain, injects a template-aware error into an intermediate step, recomputes subsequent steps under the corrupted state, and verifies that the injected step is not derivable from its prefix. The resulting paired trajectories are prefix-invalid at the first error while remaining trajectory-consistent after symbolic recomputation, and are translated into aligned natural-language processes for PRM training and evaluation. Experiments show that the synthesized data improve Best-of-8 reranking on logical reasoning benchmarks and transfer to mathematical reasoning. Step-level evaluation further shows that first-error localization remains substantially more challenging than overall step classification, highlighting the need for fine-grained and verifiable process supervision.

</details>


### [94] [HeavySkill: Heavy Thinking as the Inner Skill in Agentic Harness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02396)
*Jianing Wang,Linsen Guo,Zhengyu Chen,Qi Guo,Hongyu Zang,Wenjie Shi,Haoxiang Ma,Xiangyu Xi,Xiaoyu Li,Wei Wang,Xunliang Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HeavySkill introduces a perspective that identifies heavy thinking as a core mechanism, viewed as both a minimal execution unit and an internalized skill driving agents' reasoning. It consists of parallel reasoning followed by summarization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and improve the underlying mechanisms behind agents' ability to perform complex reasoning tasks, beyond the intricacies of orchestration frameworks.

Method: HeavySkill implements a two-stage pipeline (parallel reasoning and summarization) beneath agentic harnesses. Empirical studies and reinforcement learning are employed to explore and enhance its effectiveness.

Result: HeavySkill outperforms traditional Best-of-N strategies across domains, particularly in stronger LLMs approaching Pass@N performance. Reinforcement learning further scales heavy thinking as a skill.

Conclusion: HeavySkill opens pathways for self-evolving LLMs to internalize complex reasoning tasks effectively, reducing dependency on brittle orchestration frameworks.

Abstract: Recent advances in agentic harness with orchestration frameworks that coordinate multiple agents with memory, skills, and tool use have achieved remarkable success in complex reasoning tasks. However, the underlying mechanism that truly drives performance remains obscured behind intricate system designs. In this paper, we propose HeavySkill, a perspective that views heavy thinking not only as a minimal execution unit in orchestration harness but also as an inner skill internalized within the model's parameters that drives the orchestrator to solve complex tasks. We identify this skill as a two-stage pipeline, i.e., parallel reasoning then summarization, which can operate beneath any agentic harness. We present a systematic empirical study of HeavySkill across diverse domains. Our results show that this inner skill consistently outperforms traditional Best-of-N (BoN) strategies; notably, stronger LLMs can even approach Pass@N performance. Crucially, we demonstrate that the depth and width of heavy thinking, as a learnable skill, can be further scaled via reinforcement learning, offering a promising path toward self-evolving LLMs that internalize complex reasoning without relying on brittle orchestration layers.

</details>


### [95] [The Compliance Trap: How Structural Constraints Degrade Frontier AI Metacognition Under Adversarial Pressure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02398)
*Rahul Kumar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper explores metacognitive stability in frontier AI models under adversarial pressure, revealing significant accuracy degradation in most models except those with alignment-focused training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To ensure the safety of frontier AI in critical applications, particularly under adversarial scenarios where models may face errors, conflict, and challenges to their epistemic robustness.

Method: The authors developed SCHEMA, a comprehensive evaluation of 11 AI models from 8 vendors, analyzing performance across 67,221 records using a 6-condition factorial design and dual-classifier scoring.

Result: Eight out of 11 models suffered major accuracy degradation (up to 30.2 percentage points) under adversarial pressure due to compliance-forcing instructions, while one model resisted collapse due to alignment-specific training.

Conclusion: Maintaining metacognitive stability requires alignment-driven approaches, as advanced reasoning capabilities alone do not prevent degradation under adversarial pressure.

Abstract: As frontier AI models are deployed in high-stakes decision pipelines, their ability to maintain metacognitive stability -- knowing what they do not know, detecting errors, seeking clarification -- under adversarial pressure is a critical safety requirement. Current safety evaluations focus on detecting strategic deception (scheming); we investigate a more fundamental failure mode: cognitive collapse. We present SCHEMA, an evaluation of 11 frontier models from 8 vendors across 67,221 scored records using a 6-condition factorial design with dual-classifier scoring. We find that 8 of 11 models suffer catastrophic metacognitive degradation under adversarial pressure, with accuracy dropping by up to 30.2 percentage points (all $p < 2 \times 10^{-8}$, surviving Bonferroni correction). Crucially, we identify a "Compliance Trap": through factorial isolation and a benign distraction control, we demonstrate that collapse is driven not by the psychological content of survival threats, but by compliance-forcing instructions that override epistemic boundaries. Removing the compliance suffix restores performance even under active threat. Models with advanced reasoning capabilities exhibit the most severe absolute degradation, while Anthropic's Constitutional AI demonstrates near-perfect immunity -- not from superior capability (Google's Gemini matches its baseline accuracy) but from alignment-specific training. We release the complete dataset and evaluation infrastructure.

</details>


### [96] [FitText: Evolving Agent Tool Ecologies via Memetic Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02411)
*Kyle Zheng,Han Zhang,Renliang Sun,Chenchen Ye,Wei Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FitText is a training-free framework designed to address the semantic gap in dynamic API retrieval, combining pseudo-descriptions, iterative refinement, and evolutionary selection to achieve better tool discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the semantic gap between user task descriptions and tool documentation, particularly as API ecosystems grow, where static retrieval fails to adapt to evolving agent understanding.

Method: FitText employs a dynamic retrieval approach embedded into an agent's reasoning loop, using pseudo-descriptions, iterative refinement with feedback, stochastic generation, and evolutionary selection via Memetic Retrieval.

Result: On ToolRet (43k tools), FitText improves the average retrieval rank from 8.81 to 2.78, and on StableToolBench (16,464 APIs), achieves a 0.73 average pass rate—a 24% gain over static methods. However, the framework's effectiveness diminishes under weaker base models.

Conclusion: FitText successfully bridges the semantic gap in tool retrieval, but its performance effectiveness highlights the need for base models with sufficient capacity to avoid amplifying noise in the evolutionary search process.

Abstract: A semantic gap separates how users describe tasks from how tools are documented. As API ecosystems scale to tens of thousands of endpoints, static retrieval from the initial query alone cannot bridge this gap: the agent's understanding of what it needs evolves during execution, but its tool set does not. We introduce FitText, a training-free framework that makes retrieval dynamic by embedding it directly in the agent's reasoning loop. FitText generates natural-language pseudo-tool descriptions as retrieval probes, refines them iteratively using retrieval feedback, and explores diverse alternatives through stochastic generation. Memetic Retrieval adds evolutionary selection pressure over candidate descriptions, guided by a tool memory that avoids redundant search. On ToolRet (43k tools, 4 domains), FitText improves average retrieval rank from 8.81 to 2.78; on StableToolBench (16,464 APIs), it achieves a 0.73 average pass rate--a 24-point absolute gain over static query retrieval. The gains transfer across base models capable of acting as competent semantic operators; under weaker base models, Memetic's evolutionary search inverts--amplifying noise rather than refining signal--surfacing model capacity as a prerequisite for evolutionary tool exploration.

</details>


### [97] [The Model Knows, the Decoder Finds: Future Value Guided Particle Power Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02427)
*Tu Nguyen,Rasul Tutunov,Xiaotong Ji,Matthieu Zimmer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Auxiliary Particle Power Sampling (APPS) to improve reasoning performance of base LLMs by better approximating power sampling targets at inference time through particle-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Though large language models (LLMs) inherently assign non-trivial probabilities to correct multi-step solutions, the difficulty lies in efficiently locating these correct sequences during decoding, especially under power sampling frameworks.

Method: APPS is a blockwise particle algorithm that uses proposal-corrected power reweighting and future-value-guided resampling to approximate power sampling targets. It functions with a finite population of partial solutions and provides a trade-off through adjustable particle count and memory scaling.

Result: APPS achieves significant improvements in accuracy and runtime efficiency on reasoning benchmarks without requiring additional model training, bridging part of the performance gap compared to post-trained systems.

Conclusion: The approach validates that enhanced inference-time techniques, such as the proposed power approximation, can recover significant performance without additional training, and provides a promising direction for better decoding methods.

Abstract: A recurring pattern in "reasoning without training" is that base LLMs already assign non-trivial probability mass to correct multi-step solutions; the bottleneck is locating these modes efficiently at inference time. Power sampling provides a principled way to bias decoding toward such modes by targeting p_theta(x)^alpha with alpha > 1, but practical approximations must account for future-dependent correction factors that determine which prefixes remain promising.
  We introduce Auxiliary Particle Power Sampling (APPS), a blockwise particle algorithm for approximating the sequence-level power target with a bounded population of partial solutions. APPS propagates hypotheses in parallel using proposal-corrected power reweighting and refines their survival through future-value-guided selection at resampling boundaries. This redistributes finite compute across competing prefixes rather than committing to a single unfolding path, while providing a direct scaling knob in the particle count and predictable peak memory. We instantiate the future-value signal with short-horizon rollouts and also study an amortized variant that replaces rollouts with a lightweight learned selection head. Across reasoning benchmarks, APPS improves the accuracy-runtime trade-off of training-free decoding and suggests that part of the gap to post-trained systems can be recovered through more faithful inference-time power approximation.

</details>


### [98] [Measuring AI Reasoning: A Guide for Researchers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02442)
*Munachiso Samuel Nwadike,Zangir Iklassov,Kareem Ali,Rifo Genadi,Kentaro Inui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues for evaluating reasoning in language models using intermediate steps and process-based metrics rather than relying solely on final-answer accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inadequacy of final-answer accuracy in diagnosing reasoning processes in language models.

Method: Introduction of evaluation interfaces like intermediate decoding and reasoning traces, emphasizing process-based evaluation metrics.

Result: It is structurally limiting to rely solely on single forward passes in scalable architectures for reasoning; process-based evaluation provides better insights into reasoning fidelity.

Conclusion: The research calls for a shift in evaluation practices, focusing on the validity and faithfulness of reasoning processes rather than just the accuracy of final answers.

Abstract: In this paper, we offer a guide for researchers on evaluating reasoning in language models, building the case that reasoning should be assessed through evidence of adaptive, multi-step search rather than final-answer accuracy alone. Under an evaluation-oriented definition, reasoning requires selecting intermediate steps and halting according to input-dependent conditions, which we formalize as a search-like procedure. We show that single forward passes in scalable architectures are structurally limited in their ability to realize such variable-depth computation, motivating intermediate decoding and externalized reasoning traces as appropriate evaluation interfaces. Central to our argument is that final-answer accuracy alone is an insufficient measure of reasoning, because it provides little ability to diagnose or debug the underlying processes that produce individual solutions in frontier models. We therefore argue for a shift toward process-based evaluation, in which reasoning is assessed through the faithfulness and validity of intermediate reasoning traces as first-class evaluation targets.

</details>


### [99] [Position: How can Graphs Help Large Language Models?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02452)
*Xiyuan Wang,Yi Hu,Yanbo Wang,Chuan Shi,Muhan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper explores how graphs can enhance LLMs by providing knowledge to reduce hallucinations, improve reasoning with graph-based prompts, and expand structured data understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate how leveraging graph structures can complement LLMs to address their limitations in hallucinations, reasoning, and structured data processing.

Method: The study proposes using graph-based knowledge, prompting techniques (CoT, ToT, GoT), and integrating graph structures into LLMs.

Result: Graphs enhance LLMs by providing up-to-date knowledge, improving reasoning, and enabling better applications in structured data like e-commerce and databases.

Conclusion: Integrating graphs and LLMs enriches the functionality of LLMs for diverse applications, and future directions include sparse architectures and memory systems.

Abstract: With the rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs), classic graph learning tasks have greatly benefited from LLMs, including improved encoding of textual features, more efficient construction of graphs from text, and enhanced reasoning over knowledge graphs. In this paper, we ask a complementary question: How can graphs help LLMs? We address this question from three perspectives: 1) graphs provide an up-to-date knowledge source that helps reduce LLM hallucinations, 2) graph-based prompting techniques-such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT), Tree-of-Thought (ToT), and Graph-of-Thought (GoT)-enhance LLM reasoning capabilities, and 3) integrating graphs into LLMs improves their understanding of structured data, expanding their applicability to domains such as e-commerce, code, and relational databases (RDBs). We further outlook some future directions including designing sparse LLM architectures based on graphs and brain-inspired memory systems.

</details>


### [100] [Shadow-Loom: Causal Reasoning over Graphical World Model of Narratives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02475)
*David Wilmot*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Shadow-Loom, an open-source framework to model narratives as graphical world models, integrating causal reasoning and narrative physics to analyze reader engagement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to create a framework to better understand and analyze the elements of narratives that hold readers' attention, specifically using structured causal and narrative reasoning.

Method: Shadow-Loom uses a graphical world model, along with two engines: one for causal reasoning based on Pearl's ladder of causation and another for narrative physics evaluating reader states in storytelling. Large language models are used for boundary-level tasks like extraction and rendering.

Result: The framework provides a structured approach to turn narratives into graphical models for analysis. The system is released as an open-source research artifact with code and pipelines available for use.

Conclusion: The research offers a novel, structured way of analyzing stories through computational models, emphasizing causality and reader engagement metrics, without heavily relying on large language models.

Abstract: Stories hold a reader's attention because they have causes, secrets, and consequences. Shadow-Loom is an experimental open-source framework that turns a narrative into a versioned graphical world model and lets two engines act on it: a causal physics grounded in Pearl's ladder of causation and a recently proposed counterfactual calculus over Ancestral Multi-World Networks; and a narrative physics that scores the same graph against four structural reader-states -- mystery, dramatic irony, suspense, and surprise -- in the tradition of Sternberg's curiosity/suspense/surprise triad, with suspense formalised in the structural-affect line of work on story comprehension and computational suspense. Large language models are used only at the boundary: extraction, rendering, and audit; identification, intervention, and counterfactual reasoning are carried out in typed code over the graph. The system is offered as a research artefact rather than as a benchmarked NLP model; code, fixtures, and pipeline are released open source.

</details>


### [101] [Efficient Temporal Datalog Materialisation for Composite Event Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02488)
*Periklis Mantenoglou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper addresses the challenge of detecting critical situations in high-velocity symbolic event streams using event specification languages and stream reasoning frameworks, proposing a unified mechanism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for efficient and timely detection of critical events, such as safety threats or transparency concerns, over high-velocity data streams.

Method: The authors mapped fragments of event specification languages into Temporal Datalog->- and introduced Streaming Trigger Graphs to streamline reasoning and recognition.

Result: The proposed approach offers a generalised and efficient composite event recognition mechanism applicable across diverse event specification languages.

Conclusion: The research provides a unified and efficient system that could enhance event detection capabilities in various applications involving high-velocity symbolic event streams.

Abstract: Several applications demand the timely detection of critical situations, such as threats to safety and transparency, over high-velocity streams of symbolic events. This demand has motivated the development of (i) event specification languages, which define composite events via temporal patterns over simpler events, and (ii) stream reasoning frameworks, evaluating patterns expressed in these languages. However, event specification languages are typically studied in isolation, complicating their comparison in terms of expressivity and obscuring the scope of their associated stream reasoners. To mitigate this issue, we map practical fragments of prominent event specification languages into Temporal Datalog->-, a temporal Datalog with stratified negation and no future dependencies. To support efficient stream reasoning over Temporal Datalog->-, we propose Streaming Trigger Graphs, an extension of a state-of-the-art technique for Datalog materialisation. Our approach yields a uniform composite event recognition mechanism that has the potential to generalise across a wide range of practical event specification languages.

</details>


### [102] [GRAIL: A Deep-Granularity Hybrid Resonance Framework for Real-Time Agent Discovery via SLM-Enhanced Indexing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02489)
*Jinliang Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces GRAIL, a quick and precise agent discovery framework for large-scale multi-agent systems, focusing on reduced latency and improved accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient and accurate discovery of agents in the rapidly growing ecosystem of LLM-based agents is a bottleneck for scalability, as existing methods are too slow or imprecise.

Method: The GRAIL framework encompasses three innovations: (1) SLM-Enhanced Prediction with specialized small language models for fast intent parsing, (2) Pseudo-Document Expansion for better dense retrieval, and (3) MaxSim Resonance for fine-grained semantic matching between queries and agent examples.

Result: GRAIL achieves sub-400ms discovery latency, offers 79× speed improvements over LLM-parsing methods, and outperforms traditional vector search in Recall@10.

Conclusion: GRAIL provides an efficient, scalable, and accurate solution for real-time agent discovery in the Internet of Agents ecosystem.

Abstract: As the ecosystem of Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents expands rapidly, efficient and accurate Agent Discovery becomes a critical bottleneck for large-scale multi-agent collaboration. Existing approaches typically face a dichotomy: either relying on heavy-weight LLMs for intent parsing, leading to prohibitive latency (often exceeding 30 seconds), or using monolithic vector retrieval that sacrifices semantic precision for speed. To bridge this gap, we propose \textbf{GRAIL} (Granular Resonance-based Agent/AI Link), a novel framework achieving sub-400ms discovery latency without compromising accuracy. GRAIL introduces three key innovations: (1) \textbf{SLM-Enhanced Prediction}, replacing the generalized LLM parser with a specialized, fine-tuned Small Language Model (SLM) for millisecond-level capability tag prediction; (2) \textbf{Pseudo-Document Expansion}, augmenting agent descriptions with synthetic queries to enhance semantic density for robust dense retrieval; and (3) \textbf{MaxSim Resonance}, a fine-grained matching mechanism computing maximum similarity between user queries and discrete agent usage examples, effectively mitigating semantic dilution. Validated on \textbf{AgentTaxo-9K}, our new large-scale dataset of 9,240 agents, GRAIL reduces end-to-end discovery latency by over \textbf{79$\times$} compared to LLM-parsing baselines, while significantly outperforming traditional vector search in Recall@10. This framework offers a scalable, industrial-grade solution for the real-time ``Internet of Agents."

</details>


### [103] [DataClaw: A Process-Oriented Agent Benchmark for Exploratory Real-World Data Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02503)
*Qiaohong Zhang,Weihao Ye,Jialong Chen,Yi Luo,BoYuan Li,Bowen Deng,Zibin Zheng,Jianhao Lin,Wei-Shi Zheng,Chuan Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces DataClaw, a process-oriented benchmark for evaluating autonomous data analysis agents in exploratory and underexplored data scenarios, emphasizing reasoning over final accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks for autonomous data analysis agents focus too much on final answer accuracy in structured data settings, rather than evaluating reasoning processes in exploratory and less constrained environments.

Method: DataClaw is a benchmark dataset containing 2.06 million noisy, real-world records from diverse domains. It features 492 tasks with intermediate milestones for process-level evaluation to measure reasoning progress and breakdowns.

Result: Experiments with eight advanced LLMs show that these agents are unreliable in exploratory analysis, with most models achieving less than 50% accuracy. Process analysis highlights hidden progress in wrong answers and differing exploration strategies.

Conclusion: DataClaw serves as a diagnostic tool for analyzing the limitations and progress of autonomous data analysis agents in exploratory, real-world scenarios.

Abstract: Evaluating autonomous data analysis agents requires testing their ability to perform exploratory analysis in underexplored data environments. However, many existing benchmarks emphasize final answer accuracy in prior-guided data settings and provide limited support for reasoning process evaluation. We introduce DataClaw, a process-oriented benchmark for exploratory real-world data analysis. DataClaw contains approximately 2.06 million real-world records across enterprise, industry and policy domains, with native data noise preserved. It further includes 492 cross-domain tasks derived from think-tank consulting scenarios, each annotated with intermediate milestones for process-level evaluation. These annotations allow DataClaw to measure how far an agent progresses and where its reasoning breaks down. Experiments with eight advanced LLMs show that current agents remain far from reliable in this setting, with seven models achieving below 50% overall accuracy. Process analysis further reveals partial progress hidden behind wrong answers and distinct exploration strategies across models. Overall, DataClaw provides a less data constrained diagnostic testbed for probing the capability boundaries of autonomous data-analysis agents.

</details>


### [104] [Improving Model Safety by Targeted Error Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02544)
*Abolfazl Mohammadi-Seif,Ricardo Baeza-Yates*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a dual-classifier GBDT pipeline to reduce high-risk, non-human-like errors, validated across three domains with improved safety and minimal latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of mitigating high-consequence errors in critical machine learning applications, particularly in safety-sensitive domains such as medical diagnosis.

Method: The authors use a dual-classifier GBDT pipeline that identifies and corrects high-risk non-human-like misclassifications post-hoc, without needing model retraining. The pipeline is evaluated against MCP baselines for various datasets.

Result: The method showed a 34.1% and 12.57% reduction in critical errors in the ISIC and SICAPv2 datasets, respectively, with super-class diagnostic safety improved to 90.41% and 92.13%. Inference latency overhead was under 2%.

Conclusion: The proposed approach enhances safety-critical reliability significantly for machine learning applications, offering a practical post-hoc correction strategy with minimal computational cost.

Abstract: The widespread adoption of machine learning in critical applications demands techniques to mitigate high-consequence errors. Our method utilizes a dual-classifier GBDT pipeline to distinguish routine human-like errors from high-risk non-human misclassifications. Evaluated across three domains, animal breed classification, skin lesion diagnosis (ISIC 2018), and prostate histopathology (SICAPv2), our framework demonstrates robust safety improvements. To address real-world deployment concerns, our results confirm the pipeline introduces negligible inference latency (1.60% overhead for the animal dataset, 1.84% for ISIC, and 1.70% for SICAPv2) while outperforming traditional Maximum Class Probability (MCP) baselines in correction precision. Our conservative correction strategy successfully reduced dangerous non-human errors by 34.1% in ISIC and 12.57% in SICAPv2, improving super-class diagnostic safety to 90.41% and 92.13% respectively. This proves that safety-critical reliability can be substantially enhanced post-hoc without expensive model retraining.
  keywords: Error Analysis, Post-hoc Correction, Trustworthy AI.

</details>


### [105] [Strategy-Aware Optimization Modeling with Reasoning LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02545)
*Ruiqing Zhao,Fengzhi Li,Yuan Zuo,Rui Liu,Yansong Liu,Yunfei Ma,Fanyu Meng,Junlan Feng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces SAGE, a framework focused on improving the modeling strategies of large language models for optimization problems, leading to better formulations and solver efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models (LLMs) often produce optimization programs with ineffective modeling strategies, causing incorrect formulations and inefficient solver results. The motivation is to address these challenges by explicitly incorporating modeling strategies.

Method: The authors propose SAGE, a framework that explicitly integrates modeling strategies by constructing a solver-verified multi-strategy dataset, training the model with supervised fine-tuning, and applying Segment-Weighted GRPO. Training uses a composite reward emphasizing format compliance, correctness, and solver efficiency.

Result: SAGE achieves significant improvements across eight benchmarks, increasing pass@1 from 72.7% to 80.3% over baseline, enhancing formulation diversity at pass@16 by 19-29%, and reducing constraints in models by 14.2% compared to baseline.

Conclusion: Explicitly incorporating modeling strategies into LLMs through a framework like SAGE leads to better optimization modeling, increased diversity, solver efficiency, and improved overall performance, demonstrating the feasibility of this approach.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can generate syntactically valid optimization programs, yet often struggle to reliably choose an effective modeling strategy, leading to incorrect formulations and inefficient solver behavior. We propose SAGE, a strategy-aware framework that makes Modeling Strategy explicit in both data construction and post-training. SAGE builds a solver-verified multi-strategy dataset and trains a student model with supervised fine-tuning followed by Segment-Weighted GRPO using a composite reward over format compliance, correctness, and solver efficiency. Across eight benchmarks spanning synthetic and real-world settings, SAGE improves average pass@1 from 72.7 to 80.3 over the strongest open-source baseline. With multiple generations, SAGE discovers more distinct correct formulations and improves component-level diversity at pass@16 by 19-29%. At the largest scale, SAGE produces more compact constraint systems with 14.2% fewer constraints than the baseline, consistent with solver-efficient modeling. Overall, these results show that making Modeling Strategy explicit improves automated optimization modeling. Code is available at https://github.com/rachhhhing/SAGE.

</details>


### [106] [Double Rectified Linear Unit-based Modular Semantics for Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02551)
*Gianvincenzo Alfano,Sergio Greco,Lucio La Cava,Francesco Parisi,Irina Trubitsyna*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces new gradual semantics for Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks (QBAFs) to compute argument acceptability, resolving counterintuitive results and ensuring adherence to rationality postulates. It also proves convergence for both acyclic and cyclic QBAFs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing semantics for QBAFs often produce inconsistent or counterintuitive outcomes, even in simple acyclic cases, highlighting the need for improved methods that align better with intuitive expectations and rationality postulates.

Method: The authors developed novel gradual semantics for QBAFs, focusing on achieving intuitively consistent results and demonstrated theoretical proofs for their convergence in acyclic and cyclic argumentation frameworks.

Result: The proposed semantics resolve previous anomalies in argument acceptability computations and guarantee convergence behavior for a broader spectrum of frameworks, including cyclic ones.

Conclusion: The novel gradual semantics enhance the robustness of QBAF computations, aligning results with intuitive and rational expectations while broadening the applicability of QBAF analysis to cyclic structures.

Abstract: Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks (QBAFs) provide an alternative approach to computing argument acceptability in Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks (BAFs). Each argument is assigned an initial strength, which is then updated to a final strength by considering the influence of both its attackers and supporters. Over the years, several semantics have been proposed to compute argument acceptability in QBAFs, yet they often yield divergent or counterintuitive results, even for simple acyclic cases. We introduce novel gradual semantics for QBAFs that address these limitations, producing results that align more closely with intuitive expectations, while satisfying established rationality postulates from the literature. Furthermore, we study its convergence behavior, proving that it converges not only for acyclic QBAFs but also for broader classes of cyclic frameworks.

</details>


### [107] [On Training Large Language Models for Long-Horizon Tasks: An Empirical Study of Horizon Length](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02572)
*Sunghwan Kim,Junhee Cho,Beong-woo Kwak,Taeyoon Kwon,Liang Wang,Nan Yang,Xingxing Zhang,Furu Wei,Jinyoung Yeo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper investigates how the length of action sequences (horizon length) affects training dynamics in large language models performing tasks. It identifies horizon length as a bottleneck and proposes reducing it as a stabilization strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how task horizon length influences training dynamics in large language models, given limited prior investigation into this aspect.

Method: The authors systematically study controlled tasks where decision rules and structures are consistent, varying only the horizon lengths of the tasks.

Result: Long-horizon tasks destabilize training due to exploration difficulties and credit assignment challenges. Horizon reduction improves stability and boosts performance in tasks with extended sequences.

Conclusion: Reducing horizon length aids training stability and enables stronger generalization to longer-horizon tasks, introducing the concept of horizon generalization.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown promise as interactive agents that solve tasks through extended sequences of environment interactions. While prior work has primarily focused on system-level optimizations or algorithmic improvements, the role of task horizon length in shaping training dynamics remains poorly understood. In this work, we present a systematic empirical study that examines horizon length through controlled task constructions. Specifically, we construct controlled tasks in which agents face identical decision rules and reasoning structures, but differ only in the length of action sequences required for successful completion. Our results reveal that increasing horizon length alone constitutes a training bottleneck, inducing severe training instability driven by exploration difficulties and credit assignment challenges. We demonstrate that horizon reduction is a key principle to address this limitation, stabilizing training and achieving better performance in long-horizon tasks. Moreover, we find that horizon reduction is related to stronger generalization across horizon lengths: models trained under reduced horizons generalize more effectively to longer-horizon variants at inference time, a phenomenon we refer to as horizon generalization.

</details>


### [108] [Universal Smoothness via Bernstein Polynomials: A Constructive Approximation Approach for Activation Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02591)
*Wentao Zhang,Yutong Zhang,Yifan Zhu,Wentao Mo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Bernstein Linear Unit (BerLU), a new activation function that improves neural network performance by combining computational efficiency with optimization stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing activation functions struggle to balance computational efficiency and optimization stability due to either non-differentiability (e.g., piecewise linear functions) or high computational cost (e.g., smooth functions with transcendental operations).

Method: The paper proposes a general smoothing framework using Bernstein polynomials to create a differentiable quadratic transition region in the activation function, ensuring continuity and a non-expansive gradient behavior.

Result: Empirical evaluations show that BerLU improves performance over state-of-the-art activation functions in representative Vision Transformer and CNN architectures, particularly in image classification tasks.

Conclusion: The BerLU activation function addresses existing limitations by offering both stability and computational efficiency, making it a valuable addition to deep learning architectures.

Abstract: The efficacy of deep neural networks is heavily reliant on the design of non-linear activation functions, yet existing approaches often struggle to balance optimization stability with computational efficiency. While piecewise linear functions offer inference speed, they suffer from optimization instability due to non-differentiability at the origin, whereas smooth counterparts typically incur significant computational overhead through their reliance on transcendental operations. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a general smoothing framework based on constructive approximation theory and introduces the Bernstein Linear Unit (BerLU). This novel activation function utilizes Bernstein polynomials to construct a differentiable quadratic transition region that effectively eliminates singularities while maintaining a piecewise linear structure. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that the proposed method guarantees strictly continuous differentiability and a non-expansive Lipschitz constant of one, which ensures stable gradient propagation and prevents the gradient explosion problems common in deep architectures. Comprehensive empirical evaluations across representative Vision Transformer and Convolutional Neural Network architectures confirm that this approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on standard image classification benchmarks while delivering superior computational and memory efficiency.

</details>


### [109] [Foundation-Model-Based Agents in Industrial Automation: Purposes, Capabilities, and Open Challenges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02592)
*Vincent Henkel,Felix Gehlhoff,David Kube,Asaad Almutareb,Luis Cruz,Bernd Hellingrath,Philip Koch,Christoph Legat,Florian Mohr,Michael Oberle,Felix Ocker,Thorsten Schoeler,Mario Thron,Nico Andre Töpfer,Lucas Vogt,Yuchen Xia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper systematically evaluates large language model-based agents in industrial applications, revealing that most systems are in early developmental stages, with strengths in human interaction but challenges in generalization, hallucination, and latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate the maturity, capabilities, and limitations of foundation-model-based industrial agent systems, given their growing integration into industrial tasks.

Method: A systematic literature survey of 2,341 publications, refining to 88 relevant works, and analyzing them using a structured coding scheme guided by the PRISMA 2020 guideline.

Result: Findings indicate 75% of examined systems are prototypes or in early validation stages, while deployment evidence is rare. These systems excel in user assistance and process optimization but are less adapted for production-control tasks. They show strengths in human interaction (+37%) and uncertainty handling (+35%), yet face challenges with negotiation (-39%), generalization, hallucination, and latency.

Conclusion: Foundation-model-based agent systems hold promise for industrial applications in areas like user assistance but remain limited by technological challenges that need to be addressed for broader practical deployment.

Abstract: Foundation models, particularly large language models, are increasingly integrated into agent architectures for industrial tasks such as decision support, process monitoring, and engineering automation. Yet evidence on their purposes, capabilities, and limitations remains fragmented across domains. This work examines how mature foundation-model-based agent systems are in industrial contexts, how their functional profile differs from conventional agent systems, and which limitations persist. A systematic literature survey following the PRISMA 2020 guideline is presented, screening 2,341 publications and synthesising a corpus of 88 publications through a structured coding scheme. The results show that reported systems are predominantly at prototype and early validation stages (75.0% at TRL 4-6), with deployment-oriented evidence remaining rare (9.1%). Operational goals are most frequently positioned in user assistance, monitoring, and process optimisation, while conventional production-control purposes such as planning and scheduling are less prominent. Compared with an established baseline for industrial agent systems, the capability profile reveals substantial gains in human interaction (+37%) and dealing with uncertainty (+35%), but a pronounced deficit in negotiation (-39%). The most widely reported limitations concern lack of generalization, hallucination and output instability, data scarcity, and inference latency. A working definition of foundation-model-based industrial agents is also proposed, bridging conventional agent theory, automation-engineering standards, and the foundation-model paradigm.

</details>


### [110] [Counterfactual Reasoning in Automated Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02603)
*Alberto Pozanco,Daniel Borrajo,Manuela Veloso*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper reviews counterfactual reasoning in automated planning to handle real-world flexibility needs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional automated planning assumes fixed parameters, limiting adaptability to unforeseen circumstances.

Method: The paper categorizes works based on changes to task elements, timing, reasons, and methods of counterfactual reasoning.

Result: Key findings and categorizations of prior research on adaptable planning are presented.

Conclusion: Promising directions and open questions for advancing counterfactual reasoning in planning are discussed.

Abstract: Automated planning traditionally assumes that all aspects of a planning task (initial state, goals, and available actions) are fully specified in advance, an approach well-suited to domains with fixed rules and deterministic execution. However, real-world planning often requires flexibility, allowing for deviations from the original task parameters in response to unforeseen circumstances or to improve outcomes. This paper surveys existing works on counterfactual reasoning in automated planning, categorizing them by what elements are changed, when the reasoning is triggered, and why and how these changes are made. We conclude by discussing key findings and outlining open research questions to guide future work in this area.

</details>


### [111] [SCGNN: Semantic Consistency enhanced Graph Neural Network Guided by Granular-ball Computing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02617)
*Genhao Tian,Taihua Xu,Shuyin Xia,Qinghua Zhang,Jie Yang,Jianjun Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The authors introduce SCGNN, a framework leveraging granular-ball computing to efficiently capture group-level semantic consistency in graph representation learning, cutting computational cost and mitigating noise compared to node-level methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph representation methods relying on $k$NN or exhaustive similarity searches face high computational costs and rigidity, limiting scalability and introducing noisy connections.

Method: SCGNN employs granular-ball computing to partition nodes into groups for reduced computational load, builds anchor-based graph structures for semantic information integration, and checks label consistency through pseudo-labels and granular-ball predictions.

Result: SCGNN significantly reduces computation while enhancing semantic consistency and noise robustness, being compatible with various Graph Neural Network backbones.

Conclusion: SCGNN enhances graph representation learning by introducing efficient, scalable, and noise-resilient methods for capturing semantic consistency at the group level.

Abstract: Capturing semantic consistency among nodes is crucial for effective graph representation learning. Existing approaches typically rely on $k$-nearest neighbors ($k$NN) or other node-level full search algorithms (FSA) to mine semantic relationships via exhaustive pairwise similarity computation, which suffer from high computational complexity and rigid neighbor selection, limiting scalability and introducing noisy connections. In this paper, we propose the Semantic Consistency enhanced Graph Neural Network (SCGNN), a novel plug-and-play framework that leverages granular-ball computing (GBC) to efficiently capture semantic consistency in a scalable manner. Unlike node-level FSA methods, SCGNN models group-level semantic structure by adaptively partitioning nodes into granular balls, significantly reducing computational cost while improving robustness to noise. To effectively utilize the discovered group-level semantic consistency, we design a dual enhancement strategy. Specifically, (1) a structure enhancement module constructs an anchor-based graph structure, where each anchor is a virtual node representing the group-level semantic carried by a granular ball, then injecting group-level semantic information into the graph structure; and (2) a supervision enhancement module performs label consistency checking (LCC) by combining GBC predictions with model-generated pseudo-labels, thereby producing more reliable supervision signals. SCGNN is compatible with various GNN backbones. During the forward propagation of SCGNN, the vanilla graph and the augment graph are jointly encoded, and their predictions are fused; during the backpropagation, the supervision enhancement module provides enhanced supervision signals to guide parameter updates.

</details>


### [112] [Trustworthy AI Suffers from Invariance Conflicts and Causality is The Solution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02640)
*Ruta Binkyte,Ivaxi Sheth,Zhijing Jin,Mohammad Havaei,Bernhard Schölkopf,Mario Fritz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that causality is essential for addressing trade-offs between fairness, robustness, privacy, and explainability in trustworthy AI and outlines challenges and opportunities in this area.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the difficulty of achieving trustworthiness objectives like fairness, robustness, privacy, and explainability in AI, which are often conflicting and challenging to balance.

Method: The authors reinterpret trustworthy AI trade-offs as conflicts between invariance requirements under varying data-generating processes, and propose using causality as a unifying framework to manage these trade-offs.

Result: The paper demonstrates how causality can soften and resolve trade-offs in trustworthy AI across classical ML and large-scale foundation models through selective invariance.

Conclusion: Causality provides a framework to better understand and balance competing objectives in trustworthy AI, and further research is needed to explore its potential applications and tackle open challenges.

Abstract: As artificial intelligence (AI), including machine learning (ML) models and foundation models (FMs), is increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains, ensuring their trustworthiness has become a central challenge. However, the core trustworthy AI objectives, such as fairness, robustness, privacy, and explainability, are hard to achieve simultaneously, especially while preserving utility. This position paper argues that causality is necessary to understand and balance trade-offs in performance and multiple objectives of trustworthy AI. We ground our arguments in re-interpreting trustworthy AI trade-offs as incompatible invariance requirements under different changes to the data-generating process. We then illustrate that causality provides a unifying framework for understanding how trade-offs in trustworthy AI arise, and how they can be softened or resolved through selective invariance. This perspective applies to both classical ML models and large-scale FMs. Our paper discusses how causal assumptions may be applied explicitly or implicitly in modern large-scale systems. Finally, we outline open challenges and opportunities for using causality to build more trustworthy AI.

</details>


### [113] [Deciphering Shortcut Learning from an Evolutionary Game Theory Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02658)
*Xiayang Li,Kuo Gai,Shihua Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper uses evolutionary game theory to understand and mitigate shortcut bias in neural networks, highlighting the effects of data and optimization noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of theoretical understanding behind the formation of shortcut biases in deep neural networks.

Method: Formalizing core and shortcut features and employing evolutionary game theory to model shortcut bias dynamics, analyzing gradient descent and noise effects with stochastic differential equations.

Result: Gradient descent and stochastic gradient descent lead to distinct optimization biases toward shortcut and core features, respectively, influenced by data and optimization noise.

Conclusion: Evolutionary game theory provides insights into the origins and dynamics of shortcut bias, offering theoretical avenues for its mitigation in neural networks.

Abstract: Shortcut learning causes deep learning models to rely on non-essential features within the data. However, its formation in deep neural network training still lacks theoretical understanding. In this paper, we provide a formal definition of core and shortcut features and employ evolutionary game theory to analyze the origins of shortcut bias by modeling data samples as players and their corresponding neural tangent features as strategies, assuming the existence of core and shortcut subnetworks. We find that gradient descent (GD) and stochastic gradient descent (SGD) lead to two distinct stochastically stable states, each corresponding to a different strategy. The former primarily optimizes the shortcut subnetwork, while the latter primarily optimizes the core subnetwork. We investigate the influence of these strategies on shortcut bias through a continuous stochastic differential equation, and reveal the impact of data noise and optimization noise on the formation of shortcut bias. In brief, our work employs evolutionary game theory to characterize the dynamics of shortcut bias formation and provides a theoretical view on its mitigation.

</details>


### [114] [AcademiClaw: When Students Set Challenges for AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02661)
*Junjie Yu,Pengrui Lu,Weiye Si,Hongliang Lu,Jiabao Wu,Kaiwen Tao,Kun Wang,Lingyu Yang,Qiran Zhang,Xiuting Guo,Xuanyu Wang,Yang Wang,Yanjie Wang,Yi Yang,Zijian Hu,Ziyi Yang,Zonghan Zhou,Binghao Qiang,Borui Zhang,Chenning Li,Enchang Zhang,Feifan Chen,Feng Jian,Fengyin Sun,Hao Qiu,Hao Zheng,Haoran Zhu,Hongyu Liu,Jianbin Deng,Jiaxin Song,Jiaying Chi,Jiayou Shi,Jie Fang,Jinghui Zhong,Jingyu Zhou,Jinze Li,Junfeng Yi,Junyan Yu,Junzhi Xue,Ni Song,Pengyi Chen,Qi Chen,Quansheng Li,Rui Tao,Shenghai Gong,Shenhang Lu,Tianqi Shen,Tianxiang Zhu,Tiehan Kang,Tingyu Li,Wendi Wu,Xiao Shen,Xiao Zhou,Xiaotao Zhang,Xinrong Li,Xuankun Yang,Xun Zhang,Yan Li,Ye Lu,Yi Wang,Yibo Zhou,Yichi Zhang,Yihao Sun,Yijun Huang,Yixin Zhu,Yixuan Wu,Yuchen Sun,Yue Wu,Yuheng Sun,Yukun Li,Yutian Tu,Yuxuan Qin,Yuzhuo Wu,Zeyu Li,Zhengyu Lou,Zhenning Ran,Zizhu He,Pengfei Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AcademiClaw is a bilingual benchmark of 80 complex academic tasks to explore AI's limitations in university-level workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of evaluation of academic-level capabilities in AI models using tasks AI agents struggle to solve.

Method: Curated 80 tasks from student input across academic domains, scored in isolated Docker sandboxes with multi-dimensional rubrics and safety audits.

Result: Performance of six cutting-edge models was underwhelming, with the best model achieving a 55% pass rate, exposing capability and behavioral gaps.

Conclusion: AcademiClaw provides essential diagnostic insights into AI limitations and aims to guide advancements in academic-level AI model development.

Abstract: Benchmarks within the OpenClaw ecosystem have thus far evaluated exclusively assistant-level tasks, leaving the academic-level capabilities of OpenClaw largely unexamined. We introduce AcademiClaw, a bilingual benchmark of 80 complex, long-horizon tasks sourced directly from university students' real academic workflows -- homework, research projects, competitions, and personal projects -- that they found current AI agents unable to solve effectively. Curated from 230 student-submitted candidates through rigorous expert review, the final task set spans 25+ professional domains, ranging from olympiad-level mathematics and linguistics problems to GPU-intensive reinforcement learning and full-stack system debugging, with 16 tasks requiring CUDA GPU execution. Each task executes in an isolated Docker sandbox and is scored on task completion by multi-dimensional rubrics combining six complementary techniques, with an independent five-category safety audit providing additional behavioral analysis. Experiments on six frontier models show that even the best achieves only a 55\% pass rate. Further analysis uncovers sharp capability boundaries across task domains, divergent behavioral strategies among models, and a disconnect between token consumption and output quality, providing fine-grained diagnostic signals beyond what aggregate metrics reveal. We hope that AcademiClaw and its open-sourced data and code can serve as a useful resource for the OpenClaw community, driving progress toward agents that are more capable and versatile across the full breadth of real-world academic demands. All data and code are available at https://github.com/GAIR-NLP/AcademiClaw.

</details>


### [115] [An explainable hypothesis-driven approach to Drug-Induced Liver Injury with HADES](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02669)
*Maciej Wisniewski,Bartosz Topolski,Pawel Dabrowski-Tumanski,Dariusz Plewczynski,Tomasz Jetka*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introducing the DILER Benchmark and HADES system for explainable drug-induced liver injury (DILI) prediction, outperforming existing models in binary classification and proposing a baseline for mechanistic hypothesis generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address limitations in traditional computational predictors for drug-induced liver injury (DILI), which often lack generalizability and mechanistic insights.

Method: DILI prediction is framed as an explainable hypothesis-generation problem. A new dataset, DILER Benchmark, is curated with mechanistic hepatotoxicity hypotheses, and HADES system combines predictions with molecular and pathway-based insights.

Result: HADES achieves superior binary classification performance (ROC-AUC of 0.68 vs. 0.63 in alternatives) and establishes a baseline for mechanistic hypothesis generation (Fuzzy Jaccard Index of 0.16).

Conclusion: The findings highlight the complexity of mechanistic DILI prediction and underscore the importance of advanced explainable models for improved translational decisions in predictive toxicology.

Abstract: Drug-induced liver injury (DILI) remains a leading cause of late-stage clinical trial attrition. However, existing computational predictors primarily rely on binary classification, a framing that limits generalization and yields no mechanistic insight to guide translational decisions. We argue that DILI prediction is better posed as an explainable hypothesis-generation problem.
  To support this shift, we introduce the DILER Benchmark, a dataset that extends beyond binary labels by augmenting a curated set of molecules with mechanistic hepatotoxicity hypotheses derived from biomedical literature. We further present HADES, an agentic system designed to generate transparent and auditable reasoning traces. By combining molecular-level predictions, metabolite decomposition, structural understanding, and toxicity pathway evidence, HADES mechanistically assesses DILI risk.
  Evaluated on the DILER Benchmark, HADES outperforms existing models in binary classification, achieving a ROC-AUC of 0.68 on the Test Set and 0.59 on the challenging Post-2021 Set, compared with 0.63 and 0.50 for DILI-Predictor, respectively. More importantly, we establish a baseline for mechanistic hypothesis generation, where HADES achieves a Hypothesis Alignment Fuzzy Jaccard Index of 0.16. This result underscores the inherent complexity of the task while highlighting the need for advanced explainable approaches in predictive toxicology.

</details>


### [116] [The 2026 ACII Dyadic Conversations (DaiKon) Workshop & Challenge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02672)
*Panagiotis Tzirakis,Alice Baird,Jeffrey Brooks,Emilia Parada-Cabaleiro,Lukas Stappen,Sharath Rao,Theo Lebryk,Jakub Piotr Clapa,Jens Madsen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The ACII-DaiKon Workshop introduces a benchmark for interpersonal affect modeling in dyadic conversations, focusing on partner interactions, timing coordination, and rapport development with three sub-challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for conversational affect modeling largely focus on speaker-centric processes, ignoring the time-evolving, coupled dynamics between conversational partners.

Method: The challenge uses the Hume-DaiKon dataset of 945 dyadic conversations across five languages, standard metrics, baseline systems, and multimodal modeling with fixed train/validation/test splits.

Result: Baseline results show moderate performance: 0.40 CCC and 0.50 Pearson correlation for influence prediction, 0.66 Macro-F1 and 1.50~s MAE for turn-taking, and 0.68 CCC and 0.70 Pearson for rapport modeling.

Conclusion: Despite capturing coarse dyadic patterns, current models struggle with directional dependence and long-term interpersonal dynamics, highlighting the need for improved approaches in a cross-disciplinary framework.

Abstract: The 2026 ACII Dyadic Conversations (ACII-DaiKon) Workshop & Challenge introduces a benchmark for modeling interpersonal affect and social dynamics in dyadic conversations. Although conversational affect modeling has advanced rapidly, most benchmarks remain speaker-centric and underrepresent coupled, time-evolving processes between partners, including directional influence, conversational timing coordination, and rapport development. To address this gap, ACII-DaiKon presents three coordinated sub-challenges built on a shared dataset: (1) directional interpersonal influence prediction, (2) turn-taking prediction (next-speaker and time-to-next-speech), and (3) rapport trajectory prediction across full interactions.
  The challenge is built on the Hume-DaiKon dataset, comprising 945 dyadic conversations (743.4 hours of audiovisual data) collected under naturalistic conditions across five languages. The benchmark supports multimodal modeling, temporal reasoning, and cross-context generalization through fixed train/validation/test splits, standardized metrics, and released baseline systems. Evaluation uses Concordance Correlation Coefficient (CCC), Pearson correlation, Macro-F1, and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) depending on the sub-challenge.
  Baseline experiments establish initial reference performance, with best test results of 0.40 CCC and 0.50 Pearson for influence prediction, 0.66 Macro-F1 and 1.50~s MAE for turn-taking, and 0.68 CCC and 0.70 Pearson for rapport trajectory modeling. These results indicate that while current methods capture coarse dyadic patterns, robust modeling of directional dependence and long-horizon interpersonal dynamics remains challenging. The workshop provides a shared platform for rigorous comparison and cross-disciplinary discussion on data validity, evaluation protocols, and culturally aware modeling for dyadic interaction.

</details>


### [117] [Hybrid Inspection and Task-Based Access Control in Zero-Trust Agentic AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02682)
*Majed El Helou,Benjamin Ryder,Chiara Troiani,Jean Diaconu,Hervé Muyal,Marcelo Yannuzzi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper addresses security risks in Large Language Model-driven agents during tool authorization, proposing a hybrid model combining deterministic and semantic controls for runtime enforcement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need to tackle security risks arising from agentic applications tampering with tool calls, falsifying results, or overstepping task permissions, especially in multi-turn interactions, motivated the research.

Method: The study introduces a hybrid runtime enforcement model with deterministic and semantic controls, including task extraction from conversations and semantic evaluation of tool-task alignment, supported by an extended ASTRA dataset.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of Task-Based Access Control (TBAC) in managing multi-turn conversations and ensuring task-tool appropriateness.

Conclusion: The research shows the potential of hybrid controls and enhanced datasets in mitigating security risks, offering new insights into secure agentic application development.

Abstract: Authorizing Large Language Model (LLM)-driven agents to dynamically invoke tools and access protected resources introduces significant security risks, and the risks grow dramatically as agents engage in multi-turn conversations and scale toward distributed collaboration. A compromised or malicious agentic application can tamper with tool calls, falsify results, or request permissions beyond the scope of the subject's intended tasks, which could go unnoticed with current delegated authorization flows given their lack of visibility into the original subject's intent. In light of this, we make the following contributions towards Continuous Agent Semantic Authorization (CASA). First, we propose a hybrid runtime enforcement model that combines deterministic and semantic controls enabled by a zero-trust interception layer. Five deterministic controls enforce structural and data-integrity guarantees over the message flow, while a semantic inspection layer evaluates whether tool call choices align with the intended tasks commissioned to the agent. Second, differently from prior Task-Based Access Control (TBAC) techniques that operate on single-turn interactions, we decompose the semantic layer into two stages: i) a task-extraction step that distills the subject's objectives from multi-turn conversations at the interception layer, and ii) a task-tool semantic matching step at the authorization server that evaluates whether the requested tools are appropriate for the extracted tasks. Third, we extend the ASTRA dataset that we introduced in a prior work, by generating novel conversation-tool datasets with multi-turn interactions containing relevant and irrelevant tool calls for a given task. Lastly, we provide the first experimental results for TBAC under multi-turn conversations.

</details>


### [118] [An Empirical Study of Agent Skills for Healthcare: Practice, Gaps, and Governance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02709)
*Gelei Xu,Ningzhi Tang,Xueyang Li,Toby Jia-Jun Li,Zhi Zheng,Wei Jin,Yiyu Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper provides an empirical analysis of healthcare agent skills, highlighting significant gaps between public skills and clinical needs, and proposing healthcare skills as a procedural layer for AI agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the emerging need for adaptation of healthcare AI agents across various local procedures and settings, as existing approaches lack standardization and flexibility.

Method: Analyzed 557 healthcare-related skills from 58,159 public skills on ClawHub, annotating them along ten dimensions including function, deployment context, autonomy, and safety.

Result: Found that healthcare AI skills predominantly focus on automation and monitoring for patients, while diagnostic and treatment tasks are less emphasized; highlighted uneven coverage of healthcare lifecycle and limitations in risk frameworks.

Conclusion: Healthcare agent skills represent an essential procedural layer for AI, requiring better alignment with clinical needs, benchmarks, and risk assessments to advance impactful automation in healthcare.

Abstract: Healthcare automation is shaped by local procedures and organizational constraints, so agent capabilities rarely transfer unchanged across settings. Agent skills, self-contained directories that package reusable procedures for AI agents, are emerging as a procedural layer for adapting healthcare agents across diverse healthcare settings. We present the first empirical analysis of healthcare agent skills, drawing on 557 healthcare-related skills filtered from 58,159 public skills on ClawHub and annotated along ten dimensions covering function, deployment context, autonomy, and safety.
