Our findings provide industry insights into the fidelity degradation of reconstructions from multiple novel viewpoints, both lateral and longitudinal, enabling the integration of these methods into real-world industrial AD software development and testing pipelines.

</details>


### [383] [Cross-Language Learning within Arabic Script for Low-Resource HTR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02089)
*Sana Al-azzawi,Elisa Barney,Marcus Liwicki*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper explores Handwritten Text Recognition (HTR) for Arabic-script languages in low-resource settings by employing cross-script training and demonstrates its benefits over single-script baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of Handwritten Text Recognition (HTR) in low-resource settings, especially for Arabic-script languages, and investigate cross-script training strategies to enhance performance.

Method: Performed cross-script training on Arabic, Urdu, and Persian handwritten text data using a CRNN model. Joint training leveraged datasets with a substantial overlap of shared characters among these scripts.

Result: Achieved state-of-the-art results on Persian (CER: 9.99) and Urdu (CER reduced from 17.20 to 14.45) datasets. Results showcase script-level overlap and emphasize improvements on shared characters among the scripts.

Conclusion: Cross-script training can effectively leverage shared script overlaps in Arabic-script languages to mitigate data scarcity challenges in HTR, especially for low-resource linguistic settings.

Abstract: Handwritten Text Recognition (HTR) under limited labeled data remains a challenging problem, particularly for Arabic-script languages. Although modern sequence-based recognizers perform well in high-resource settings, their accuracy degrades sharply as training data becomes scarce. Arabic-script languages share a common writing system with substantial character overlap, motivating cross-script training as a strategy to mitigate data scarcity. We performed experiments on Arabic, Urdu, and Persian scripts and achieved improvements over single-script baselines (new SotA especially for low-resource settings). A key finding of our experiments is that cross-script transfer is largely driven by script-level overlap rather than uniform accuracy improvements. Through a statistical character-level analysis we show that gains are structurally concentrated on characters shared across scripts, while language-specific characters exhibit limited or negative transfer. These findings provide insight into transfer dynamics in low-resource script families. Detailed results include: We conduct a controlled line-level study of cross-script joint training for Arabic-script HTR under low-resource regimes (number of samples K \in 100, 500, 1000 labeled lines) on Arabic (KHATT), Urdu (NUST-UHWR), and Persian (PHTD). A CRNN model is trained on the union of multiple related Arabic-script datasets and evaluated on individual target languages. On Persian (PHTD), joint training achieves a Character Error Rate (CER) of 9.99, surpassing previously reported results despite not using the full available training data. On an Urdu dataset (UNHD), joint training reduces CER from 17.20 to 14.45. Code and data splits are released to ensure reproducibility.1

</details>


### [384] [SignMAE: Segmentation-Driven Self-Supervised Learning for Sign Language Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02094)
*Kunyuan Xie,Zhixi Cai,Kalin Stefanov*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A self-supervised pretraining method improves sign language recognition by using segmentation-based masking of body parts to better capture subtle hand and motion cues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods struggle with the fine-grained hand and motion differences in sign language recognition as they rely on generic action datasets.

Method: A segmentation-based masking and reconstruction pretraining strategy is used to optimize how an encoder learns representations for key body parts and motions.

Result: The proposed encoder achieves state-of-the-art performance on WLASL, NMFs-CSL, and Slovo datasets, with improved Top-1 accuracy and reduced frame/modality requirements.

Conclusion: The segmentation-based masking method effectively enhances representation learning for nuanced aspects of sign language, outperforming traditional methods on multiple datasets.

Abstract: Subtle hand differences make sign language recognition challenging, yet many existing methods rely on encoders pretrained on generic action datasets that poorly capture such fine-grained cues. We propose a self-supervised pretraining method for sign language recognition that uses segmentation-based masking to adapt to the presence and motion of key body parts, rather than treating hand poses as static visual tokens. The resulting mask-and-reconstruct objective improves fine-grained sign representation learning. On WLASL, NMFs-CSL, and Slovo, our encoder achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving per-instance and per-class Top-1 accuracy while using fewer input frames and modalities than comparable encoders.

</details>


### [385] [From Spherical to Gaussian: A Comparative Analysis of Point Cloud Cropping Strategies in Large-Scale 3D Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02098)
*Maximilian Kellner,Dominik Merkle,Michael Brunklaus,Alexander Reiterer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper addresses challenges in scaling large 3D point clouds for neural networks by proposing alternative subcloud cropping methods to improve model performance and context retention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the loss of geometric context caused by conventional spherical cropping in large-scale 3D point clouds, aiming to improve scene semantic understanding.

Method: The authors propose exponential, Gaussian, and linear cropping methods, compared with conventional spherical cropping, to create subclouds with larger crop sizes while maintaining similar point counts. These were tested using two 3D deep learning models across diverse datasets.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate enhancements in model performance with the proposed cropping methods, especially in large-scale outdoor scenes, achieving state-of-the-art results.

Conclusion: Alternative cropping methods can more effectively balance geometric context and model input size compared to spherical cropping, improving semantic understanding in 3D point clouds.

Abstract: Large-scale 3D point clouds can consist of billions of points. Even after downsampling, these point clouds are too large for modern 3D neural networks. In order to develop a semantic understanding of the scene, the point clouds are divided into smaller subclouds that can be processed. Typically, this division is done using spherical crops, resulting in a loss of surrounding geometric context. To address this issue, we propose alternative methods that produce subclouds with larger crop sizes while maintaining a similar number of points. Specifically, we compare exponential, Gaussian, and linear cropping methods with the spherical method. We evaluated two 3D deep learning model architectures using multiple indoor and outdoor environment datasets. Our results demonstrate that altering the cropping strategy can enhance model performance, especially for large-scale outdoor scenes, yielding new state-of-the-art results. Code is available at https://github.com/mvg-inatech/point_cloud_cropping

</details>


### [386] [Ultrasound Vision-Language Alignment via Contrastive Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02126)
*Zhuoyang Lyu,Yiyang Zhang,Tongxin Wang,Ruirui Lan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EchoCare-CLIP introduces a CLIP-style framework aligning ultrasound images with clinical text, achieving strong cross-modal alignment but revealing trade-offs for downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the lack of zero-shot and few-shot transfer capabilities in ultrasound foundation models by proposing a framework that aligns ultrasound images with text, enabling better cross-modal understanding.

Method: The study develops EchoCare-CLIP, a dual-encoder contrastive framework, using a curated dataset of over 16K image-text pairs and comparing various model configurations.

Result: EchoCare-CLIP improves cross-modal alignment over baselines, achieving a 0.682 paired alignment score; however, performance in downstream tasks is influenced by fine-tuning strategies, encoder choices, and captioning methods.

Conclusion: While ultrasound vision-language alignment is achievable using public data, effective clinical transfer necessitates balancing domain adaptation, encoder capacity, and quality of caption supervision.

Abstract: Ultrasound foundation models have achieved strong performance on structured prediction tasks but remain exclusively vision-based, limiting zero-shot and few-shot transfer to novel tasks where task-specific annotation is scarce. We address this gap with EchoCare-CLIP, a CLIP-style dual-encoder contrastive framework that aligns ultrasound images with clinical text in a shared embedding space. We curate a multi-organ corpus of over 16K image-text pairs spanning breast, liver, lung, and thyroid, with over 78% of captions derived from expert-annotated reports, and complement the remainder with a three-tier template-based and LLM-based caption generation pipeline. We evaluate model configurations spanning two text encoder families (CLIP, BioClinicalBERT) and two caption strategies (template-based, LLM-generated) against OpenAI CLIP and BiomedCLIP baselines. Our trained models consistently improve cross-modal alignment over baselines, with the best configuration achieving a paired alignment score of 0.682. However, stronger alignment does not guarantee better downstream performance: CLIP-based variants with partial fine-tuning achieve the strongest zero-shot classification on external held-out datasets (0.709 on BUSI; 0.626 on AULI), while full end-to-end fine-tuning degrades transfer due to overfitting. On linear probing and few-shot adaptation, model rankings are dataset-dependent, reflecting a trade-off between domain adaptation and representational generalizability. We further show that template-based captions match or outperform LLM-generated captions, suggesting lexical diversity is not a proxy for caption quality. Taken together, our results demonstrate that ultrasound vision-language alignment is achievable from public data alone, but robust clinical transfer requires careful balancing of domain adaptation, encoder capacity, and caption supervision quality.

</details>


### [387] [From Where Things Are to What They Are For: Benchmarking Spatial-Functional Intelligence in Multimodal LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02130)
*Le Zhang,Jihan Yang,Soundarya Krishnan,Jimit Majmudar,Xiou Ge,Prasoon Puri,Prathamesh Nandkishor Saraf,Shruti Bhargava,Dhivya Piraviperumal,Yinan Ling,Cindy Pan,Hong Yu,Aishwarya Agrawal,Bo-Hsiang Tseng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces SFI-Bench, a video-based benchmark with over 1,500 questions designed to evaluate high-level reasoning capabilities of multimodal models, focusing on spatial and functional intelligence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for multimodal large language models only assess low-level geometric perception, ignoring higher-order cognitive abilities essential for grounded intelligence.

Method: The benchmark systematically tests reasoning via two dimensions - structured spatial reasoning and functional reasoning - using tasks like conditional counting and knowledge-grounded troubleshooting.

Result: Current multimodal large language models struggle in integrating spatial memory with functional reasoning and external knowledge, exposing limitations in achieving grounded intelligence.

Conclusion: SFI-Bench serves as a diagnostic tool for gauging progress in developing cognitively advanced multimodal agents capable of grounded reasoning.

Abstract: Human-level agentic intelligence extends beyond low-level geometric perception, evolving from recognizing where things are to understanding what they are for. While existing benchmarks effectively evaluate the geometric perception capabilities of multimodal large language models (MLLMs), they fall short of probing the higher-order cognitive abilities required for grounded intelligence. To address this gap, we introduce the Spatial-Functional Intelligence Benchmark (SFI-Bench), a video-based benchmark with over 1,500 expert-annotated questions derived from diverse egocentric indoor video scans. SFI-Bench systematically evaluates two complementary dimensions of advanced reasoning: (1) Structured Spatial Reasoning, which requires understanding complex layouts and forming coherent spatial representations, and (2) Functional Reasoning, which involves inferring object affordances and their context-dependent utility. The benchmark includes tasks such as conditional counting, multi-hop relational reasoning, functional pairing, and knowledge-grounded troubleshooting, directly challenging models to integrate perception, memory, and inference. Our experiments reveal that current MLLMs consistently struggle to combine spatial memory with functional reasoning and external knowledge, highlighting a critical bottleneck in achieving grounded intelligence. SFI-Bench therefore provides a diagnostic tool for measuring progress toward more cognitively capable and truly grounded multimodal agents.

</details>


### [388] [Video Generation with Predictive Latents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02134)
*Yian Zhao,Feng Wang,Qiushan Guo,Chang Liu,Xiangyang Ji,Jian Zhang,Jie Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes Predictive Video VAE (PV-VAE), a model combining predictive learning with video reconstruction to improve video generation quality and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although video VAEs efficiently encode videos into latent spaces, existing models struggle to enhance generative performance despite good reconstruction quality. The motivation lies in addressing this gap by improving the diffusability of video latents.

Method: The authors introduce a predictive reconstruction objective, where partial past frames are encoded while simultaneously reconstructing past and predicting future frames. This encourages encoding temporally predictive structures into latent spaces.

Result: The PV-VAE model achieved a 52% faster convergence rate and a 34.42 FVD improvement over a baseline on UCF101. The method demonstrated enhanced scalability and consistent improvements in downstream video analysis tasks.

Conclusion: PV-VAE effectively combines predictive learning with reconstruction, resulting in better temporal coherence, motion understanding, and superior generative performance for videos.

Abstract: Video Variational Autoencoder (VAE) enables latent video generative modeling by mapping the visual world into compact spatiotemporal latent spaces, improving training efficiency and stability. While existing video VAEs achieve commendable reconstruction quality, continued optimization of reconstruction does not necessarily translate into improved generative performance. How to enhance the diffusability of video latents remains a critical and unresolved challenge. In this work, inspired by principles of predictive world modeling, we investigate the potential of predictive learning to improve the video generative modeling. To this end, we introduce a simple and effective predictive reconstruction objective that unifies predictive learning with video reconstruction. Specifically, we randomly discard future frames and encode only partial past observations, while training the decoder to reconstruct the observed frames and predict future ones simultaneously. This design encourages the latent space to encode temporally predictive structures and build a more coherent understanding of video dynamics, thereby improving generation quality. Our model, termed Predictive Video VAE (PV-VAE), achieves superior performance on video generation, with 52% faster convergence and a 34.42 FVD improvement over the Wan2.2 VAE on UCF101. Furthermore, comprehensive analyses demonstrate that PV-VAE not only exhibits favorable scalability, with generative performance improving alongside VAE training, but also yields consistent gains in downstream video understanding, underscoring a latent space that effectively captures temporal coherence and motion priors.

</details>


### [389] [FLoRA: Fusion-Latent for Optical Reconstruction and Flood Area Segmentation via Cross-Modal Multi-Task Distillation Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02137)
*Jagrati Talreja,Tewodros Syum Gebre,Leila Hashemi-Beni*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces FLoRA, a cross-modal multi-task framework that combines SAR and optical imagery for flood mapping, improving both reconstruction of optical-like images from SAR and accurate flood segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flood mapping is vital for disaster management, but existing methods fail to fully utilize spaceborne imagery due to the limitations of optical data under certain conditions and the lower interpretability of SAR data.

Method: FLoRA integrates SAR and optical data by using a lightweight optical teacher and advanced feature fusion techniques to enable SAR-to-optical translation and flood water segmentation. The framework employs dual decoders and multiple loss constraints to ensure optical reconstruction quality and precise flood delineation.

Result: FLoRA outperforms existing fusion baselines in PSNR, SSIM, and LPIPS on SEN1FLOODS11, DEEPFLOOD, and SEN12MS datasets, achieving better semantic and physical consistency.

Conclusion: FLoRA effectively fuses the strengths of optical and SAR data, offering a robust solution for accurate, semantically faithful flood mapping and hydrologic interpretation from satellite observations.

Abstract: Accurate flood water mapping is critical for disaster management, yet current methods struggle to fully exploit the potential of spaceborne imagery. Optical data offers high interpretability but is limited by environmental conditions, whereas SAR provides reliable all-weather coverage with reduced visual interpretability. FLoRA (Fusion Latent for Optical Reconstruction and Area Segmentation) is a cross-modal multi-task framework that jointly reconstructs high-fidelity optical imagery and segments flood water regions from Sentinel 1 SAR by fusing the complementary strengths of optical and SAR data. During training, a lightweight optical teacher (driven by RGB and NDVI priors) provides pyramidal features that guide SAR representations into a fusion latent space via multiscale windowed cross attention and FiLM conditioning, with gated residuals preventing overcorrection. This design enables multi-task learning across two complementary objectives: (a) SAR-to-optical translation for fine-grained RGB reconstruction and (b) flood water region segmentation for hydrologic interpretation. The dual decoders are optimized using Charbonnier SSIM for structural fidelity, edge FFT magnitude losses for spectral realism, and Dice BCE hydrology-aware edge alignment for precise flood water delineation. A feature distillation constraint further aligns fused SAR features with the optical teacher's manifold. Evaluations on SEN1FLOODS11, DEEPFLOOD, and SEN12MS demonstrate that FLoRA surpasses fusion baselines in PSNR, SSIM, and LPIPS, demonstrating that multi-modal fusion within a teacher-guided latent space yields semantically faithful and physically consistent flood-water intelligence from spaceborne observations.

</details>


### [390] [WindowQuant: Mixed-Precision KV Cache Quantization based on Window-Level Similarity for VLMs Inference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02262)
*Wei Tao,Xiaoyang Qu,Peiqiang Wang,Guokuan Li,Jiguang Wan,Kai Lu,Jianzong Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes WindowQuant, a method to optimize video language models' KV cache using window-adaptive mixed-precision quantization, improving inference latency and memory usage efficiently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The visual token sequence in video language models is too lengthy, leading to high inference latency and GPU memory usage. Existing solutions using token-based quantization are both computationally expensive and hardware inefficient.

Method: WindowQuant introduces window-adaptive mixed-precision quantization with two modules: (1) A window-level quantization search that identifies optimal bit-width configurations using similarity scores, and (2) a reordering-based window-level KV cache computation to address hardware inefficiency caused by mixed-precision operations.

Result: Experiments show that WindowQuant surpasses current video language models and key-value cache quantization techniques across various datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed WindowQuant improves the efficiency of VLMs by effectively addressing both computational and hardware challenges, maintaining accuracy while reducing inference latency and memory usage.

Abstract: Recently, video language models (VLMs) have been applied in various fields. However, the visual token sequence of the VLM is too long, which may cause intolerant inference latency and GPU memory usage. Existing methods propose mixed-precision quantization to the key-value (KV) cache in VLMs based on token granularity, which is time-consuming in the search process and hardware inefficient during computation. This paper introduces a novel approach called WindowQuant, which employs window-adaptive mixed-precision quantization to optimize the KV cache. WindowQuant consists of two modules: window-level quantization search and window-level KV cache computation. Window-level quantization search quickly determines the optimal bit-width configuration of the KV cache windows based on the similarity scores between the corresponding visual token windows and the text prompt, maintaining the model accuracy. Furthermore, window-level KV cache computation reorders the KV cache windows before quantization, avoiding the hardware inefficiency caused by mixed-precision quantization in inference computation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that WindowQuant outperforms state-of-the-art VLM models and KV cache quantization methods on various datasets.

</details>


### [391] [SpecEdit: Training-Free Acceleration for Diffusion based Image Editing via Semantic Locking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02152)
*Zhengan Yan,Shikang Zheng,Haoran Qin,Xiaobing Tu,Yinggui Wang,Jiacheng Liu,Jiaxuan Ren,Yuqi Lin,Peiliang Cai,Jinkui Ren,Xiantao Zhang,Linfeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces SpecEdit, a training-free framework for faster diffusion-based image editing by selectively refining only edit-relevant image regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based image editing offers flexibility but is computationally expensive, and current efficiency techniques often lack semantic alignment leading to redundant processing.

Method: SpecEdit uses a draft-and-verify approach, generating a low-resolution draft to estimate outcomes before refining select tokens for high-resolution editing, avoiding unnecessary computation.

Result: Experiments show up to 10x to 13x acceleration with strong quality preservation when combined with other acceleration techniques.

Conclusion: SpecEdit efficiently reduces computation for diffusion-based editing while maintaining high-quality results, representing a promising direction for semantic image editing.

Abstract: Diffusion-based image editing offers strong semantic controllability, but remains computationally expensive due to iterative high-resolution denoising over all spatial tokens. Dynamic-resolution sampling reduces this cost by performing early steps at reduced resolution. However, existing approaches prioritize upsampling using low-level heuristics such as edge detection or channel variance, which are weakly aligned with editing semantics and may lead to structural inconsistency. Moreover, spatial regions are often upsampled without verifying whether semantic modification is actually required, resulting in redundant high-resolution computation and accumulated errors. Therefore, we propose SpecEdit, a training-free dynamic-resolution framework tailored for diffusion-based image editing. SpecEdit follows a draft-and-verify scheme: a low-resolution draft first estimates the semantic outcome, after which token-level discrepancies are used to identify edit-relevant tokens for high-resolution denoising, while the remaining tokens stay at a coarse resolution. Experiments on Qwen-Image-Edit and FLUX.1-Kontext-dev demonstrate up to 10x and 7x acceleration, while maintaining strong quality. SpecEdit is complementary to step distillation and other acceleration techniques, achieving up to 13x speedup when combined with existing methods. Our code is in supplementary material and will be released on GitHub.

</details>


### [392] [Cross-Polarization Fusion of VV AND VH SAR Observations for Improved Flood Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02153)
*Jagrati Talreja,Tewodros Syum Gebre,Leila Hashemi Beni*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper explores combining cross-polarization SAR data (VV and VH) using deep learning for improved flood mapping under challenging environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flood mapping is critical for disaster monitoring, but single-polarization SAR data struggles in complex areas containing both surface and volume scattering.

Method: A deep learning-based segmentation framework is used to fuse VV and VH SAR polarizations. Three data configurations are compared: VV, VH, and fused VV-VH.

Result: The fused VV-VH model outperforms VV-only and VH-only approaches, especially in regions with vegetation and mixed complexity, achieving better metrics like IoU and F1-score.

Conclusion: Cross-polarization SAR fusion enhances flood mapping accuracy, proving valuable for disaster monitoring in challenging environments.

Abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery is widely used for flood monitoring due to its all-weather and day-night imaging capability. However, flood mapping using single-polarization SAR data remains challenging in complex environments where surface and volume scattering coexist. In this paper, we investigate the effectiveness of cross-polarization fusion of VV and VH SAR observations for improved flood mapping. A deep learning-based segmentation framework is employed to jointly exploit complementary information from VV and VH polarizations. To ensure a fair evaluation, three configurations are compared under identical training conditions: VV only, VH only, and fused VV-VH input. Performance is assessed using standard flood mapping metrics, including Intersection over Union (IoU) and F1-score, along with qualitative visual analysis. Experimental results demonstrate that VV-VH fusion consistently outperforms single-polarization models, particularly in vegetated and heterogeneous flood regions, leading to more accurate flood boundary delineation. The findings highlight the importance of cross-polarization SAR fusion for enhancing the reliability of SAR-based flood mapping in disaster monitoring applications.

</details>


### [393] [RAFNet: Region-Aware Fusion Network for Pansharpening](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02184)
*Jianing Zhang,Zijian Zhou,Kai Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposed RAFNet improves pansharpening by addressing computational and adaptability challenges using spatial-frequency adaptive techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome inefficiencies in pansharpening due to computational complexity and lack of adaptability in remote sensing images.

Method: Utilizes a Spatial Adaptive Refinement module (employing DWT and K-means) and Clustered Frequency Aggregation module (sparse attention strategy) within a progressive spatial-frequency network.

Result: RAFNet delivers superior image reconstruction performance, outperforming state-of-the-art pansharpening methods across multiple benchmarks.

Conclusion: RAFNet is effective and computationally efficient, addressing spatial and frequency challenges in pansharpening.

Abstract: Pansharpening aims to generate high-resolution multispectral (HRMS) images by fusing low-resolution multispectral (LRMS) and high-resolution panchromatic (PAN) images. Although deep learning has advanced this field, mainstream frequency-based methods relying on standard scaled dot-product attention suffer from quadratic computational complexity and fail to exploit the inherent regional sparsity of remote sensing imagery. Furthermore, existing spatial enhancement strategies typically employ static convolution kernels, which struggle to adapt to the complex frequency and regional variations of PAN and MS images. To address these bottlenecks, we propose a Region-Aware Fusion (RAFNet) Network that synergistically models spatial and frequency information. Specifically, we design a Spatial Adaptive Refinement (SAR) module that leverages the discrete wavelet transform (DWT) for directional frequency separation and K-means clustering for regional partitioning, which enables the dynamic construction of region-specific adaptive convolution kernels, achieving spatially and frequency-adaptive feature enhancement. Moreover, we introduce a Clustered Frequency Aggregation (CFA) module based on a sparse attention mechanism guided by the semantic clusters, which executes a region-aware sparse attention strategy that drastically reduces computational redundancy while ensuring high-quality frequency feature extraction. In addition we integrated these modules into a progressive, multi-level spatial-frequency network architecture to facilitate robust interaction and accurate image reconstruction. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed RAFNet significantly outperforms state-of-the-art pansharpening methods in both reduced- and full-resolution assessments. The code is available at https://github.com/PatrickNod/RAFNet.

</details>


### [394] [SlimDiffSR: Toward Lightweight and Efficient Remote Sensing Image Super-Resolution via Diffusion Model Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02198)
*Ce Wang,Zhenyu Hu,Wanjie Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SlimDiffSR is a lightweight framework designed for efficient remote sensing image super-resolution, achieving significant acceleration and parameter reduction while maintaining competitive quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models excel in image super-resolution but are computationally expensive, limiting their practical use in remote sensing applications.

Method: SlimDiffSR introduces an uncertainty-guided timestep assignment strategy, structured pruning methods, lightweight convolution designs, and MMD-based distillation to optimize diffusion models for remote sensing data.

Result: SlimDiffSR delivers up to 200× faster inference and 20× fewer parameters than multi-step diffusion models while preserving competitive perceptual quality.

Conclusion: SlimDiffSR successfully addresses computational inefficiency in diffusion-based remote sensing image super-resolution, offering an effective and efficient solution.

Abstract: Diffusion models have recently achieved remarkable performance in image super-resolution (SR), but their high computational cost limits practical deployment in remote sensing applications. To address this issue, we propose SlimDiffSR, a lightweight and efficient diffusion-based framework for real-world remote sensing image super-resolution. Unlike existing single-step diffusion methods that rely on fixed timesteps, we first introduce an uncertainty-guided timestep assignment strategy to construct a stronger single-step teacher model, where reconstruction difficulty is explicitly linked to diffusion timesteps, enabling adaptive generative strength. Building upon this teacher, we further present a structured pruning strategy tailored to remote sensing imagery, which systematically removes redundant semantic modules and replaces standard operations with lightweight designs, including frequency-separable convolution, direction-separable convolution, and a query-driven global aggregation module. These components explicitly exploit the unique characteristics of remote sensing data, such as sparse high-frequency details, strong directional patterns, and long-range spatial dependencies. To enhance knowledge transfer, we incorporate Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) into the distillation process to align feature distributions between the teacher and student models. Extensive experiments on multiple remote sensing benchmarks demonstrate that SlimDiffSR achieves a favorable balance between efficiency and reconstruction quality. In particular, it attains up to $200\times$ inference acceleration and a $20\times$ reduction in model parameters compared with multi-step diffusion models, while achieving competitive perceptual quality and clearly outperforming existing lightweight diffusion baselines in efficiency. The code is available at: https://github.com/wwangcece/SlimDiffSR.

</details>


### [395] [Super-resolution of airborne laser scanning point clouds for forest inventory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02201)
*Jinyuan Shao,Sangyoong Park,Chunxi Zhao,Ayman Habib,Songlin Fei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The study introduces 3DFSR, a deep learning model, to enhance the quality of ALS point clouds for forest inventory by improving point density and reducing noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ALS point clouds are sparse and noisy, which hinders accurate forest inventory tasks such as stem localization and size estimation.

Method: The authors propose 3DFSR, a voxel-based CNN with a U-Net architecture, to generate higher-density and less noisy point clouds. The model's performance is evaluated on ALS data from forests in the U.S. and Germany.

Result: 3DFSR achieves superior point cloud refinement compared to existing algorithms, significantly improving stem detection (F1: 0.97), DBH estimation (RMSE: 6.43 cm), and stem reconstruction metrics. The model is robust across varying point densities and multiple LiDAR platforms.

Conclusion: 3DFSR successfully enhances ALS point clouds for forest inventory applications, achieving superior results and generalizing well across platforms without requiring transfer learning.

Abstract: Airborne Laser Scanning (ALS) can collect point clouds across large areas, enabling large-scale forest inventory. However, ALS point clouds are sparse and noisy, resulting in inaccurate individual-tree-level forest inventory, such as stem localization and tree size estimation. To overcome this problem, we propose a deep learning model, 3D Forest Super Resolution (3DFSR), to simultaneously improve point density and reduce noise for ALS forest point cloud. 3DFSR is a voxel-based CNN with a U-Net architecture. The proposed 3DFSR is evaluated on ALS point clouds collected in both temperate forests in the U.S. and boreal forests in Germany. Experimental results demonstrate that 3DFSR can generate finer point clouds of tree structure than other state-of-the-art point cloud super-resolution algorithms, achieving 0.249 m Chamfer Distance and 2.711 m Hausdorff Distance. Furthermore, to verify the effectiveness of 3DFSR point clouds in forest inventory, we conduct stem detection, DBH measurements, and stem reconstruction on both original ALS point clouds and 3DFSR enhanced point clouds. We find that stem detection and reconstruction algorithms developed for TLS/MLS point clouds can directly work on our 3DFSR point clouds, and DBH can be derived with circle-fitting method. F1 score of stem detection is improved from 0.71 on original ALS point clouds to 0.97 on 3DFSR point clouds; DBH estimation improves from 13.45 cm RMSE using allometric equations to 6.43 cm using circle fitting; comparing to stems reconstruction from MLS point clouds, stem reconstructed from 3DFSR point clouds has 0.170 m of Chamfer Distance and 0.377 m of Hausdorff Distance, and 0.95 R2 volume estimation. Finally, we find that the proposed 3DFSR is applicable to process point densities from 10 to 1700 points/m2; it also can be generalized across data collected from different LiDAR platforms without transfer learning.

</details>


### [396] [Metric Unreliability in Multimodal Machine Unlearning: A Systematic Analysis and Principled Unified Score](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02206)
*Abdullah Ahmad Khan,Hamid Laga,Ferdous Sohel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper evaluates metric reliability for machine unlearning in Vision-Language Models (VLMs), finding inconsistencies among standard metrics and proposing a Unified Quality Score (UQS) for improved reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inconsistencies in evaluation metrics for machine unlearning in multimodal models, which is critical for regulatory compliance with GDPR.

Method: The study systematically compares five standard metrics using Kendall tau and Spearman correlation on 36 unlearned models across VQA benchmarks. It introduces UQS as a composite metric based on correlation with an oracle model.

Result: The research identifies inconsistencies between current metrics, with RA being the most reliable. UQS demonstrates stable rankings, providing a more consistent evaluation framework.

Conclusion: UQS offers a robust alternative for assessing machine unlearning in VLMs and addresses significant metric inconsistencies. The study also contributes benchmarks, checkpoints, and code to the community.

Abstract: Machine unlearning in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) is required for compliance with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), yet current evaluation practices are inconsistent. We present the first systematic study of metric reliability in multimodal unlearning. Five standard metrics, Forget Accuracy (FA), Retain Accuracy (RA), Membership Inference Attack (MIA), Activation Distance (AD), and JS divergence (JS), yield conflicting method rankings across three VQA benchmarks (MLLMU-Bench, UnLOK-VQA, MMUBench). Kendall tau analysis over 36 unlearned LLaVA-1.5-7B models reveals two opposing clusters, {FA, RA, MIA} and {AD, JS}, with tau_FA_AD = -0.26, reproduced on BLIP-2 OPT-2.7B. Agreement is lower in multimodal VQA (average tau = 0.086) than in unimodal classification (average tau = 0.158; difference = 0.072), indicating that dual image-and-text pathways amplify inconsistency. We introduce the Unified Quality Score (UQS), a composite metric with weights derived from each metric's Spearman correlation with the oracle distance d(M_hat, M_star), where M_star is the oracle model retrained only on the retain set. RA shows the strongest reliability (rho = 0.484, p = 0.003), while FA is negatively correlated (rho = -0.418, p = 0.011). UQS yields stable rankings under 100 random weight perturbations (tau = 0.647 +- 0.262). We release the benchmark, 36 checkpoints, and an interactive leaderboard. Code and pre-computed results are available at https://github.com/neurips26/UnifiedUnl.

</details>


### [397] [MultiSense-Pneumo: A Multimodal Learning Framework for Pneumonia Screening in Resource-Constrained Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02207)
*Dineth Jayakody,Pasindu Thenahandi,Chameli Dommanige*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces MultiSense-Pneumo, a multimodal framework for pneumonia screening that integrates various data types, operates on modest hardware, and supports real-world deployment in low-resource settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pneumonia causes significant morbidity and mortality, particularly in low-resource settings with limited access to healthcare resources. Current computational approaches are often unimodal and focus mainly on radiographs, limiting their effectiveness in these environments.

Method: The authors developed MultiSense-Pneumo, a multimodal framework that integrates symptom descriptors, cough audio, spoken language, and chest radiographs. Techniques used include deterministic triage, LightGBM for acoustic analysis, adversarial radiograph processing with ResNet 18, transformer-based speech recognition, and multimodal fusion for decision support.

Result: Experimental results showed that the framework is robust when handling radiograph domain shifts but has limitations in minority class recall for acoustic data. The system successfully operates on standard laptop hardware, making it viable for deployment in resource-limited settings.

Conclusion: MultiSense-Pneumo provides a transparent, modular, and multimodal approach to pneumonia screening and triage, suitable for low-resource environments. While it is not clinically validated, it serves as a promising research prototype for accessible and scalable healthcare solutions.

Abstract: Pneumonia remains a leading global cause of morbidity and mortality, particularly in low resource settings where access to imaging, laboratory testing, and specialist care is limited. Clinical assessment relies on heterogeneous evidence, including symptoms, respiratory patterns, and chest imaging, making screening inherently multimodal. However, many existing computational approaches remain unimodal and focus primarily on radiographs. In this work, we present MultiSense-Pneumo, a multimodal framework for pneumonia oriented screening and triage support that integrates structured symptom descriptors, cough audio, spoken language, and chest radiographs. The system combines deterministic symptom triage, LightGBM based acoustic classification, domain adversarial radiograph analysis using ResNet 18, transformer based speech recognition, and an interpretable multimodal fusion operator. Each modality is transformed into a normalized risk signal and aggregated into a unified screening estimate, enabling transparent and modular decision support. MultiSense-Pneumo is designed for real world deployment under modest computational constraints and can operate fully offline on standard laptop class hardware, making it suitable for community health workers, rural clinics, and emergency response settings. Experimental results demonstrate robustness of the radiograph pathway under domain shifts, while highlighting limitations in minority class recall for acoustic signals. MultiSense-Pneumo is intended as a research prototype for screening and triage support rather than a clinically validated diagnostic system.

</details>


### [398] [PubMed-Ophtha: An open resource for training ophthalmology vision-language models on scientific literature](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02720)
*Verena Jasmin Hallitschke,Carsten Eickhoff,Philipp Berens*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces PubMed-Ophtha, a new dataset containing over 102,000 ophthalmological image-caption pairs extracted from open-access articles to aid vision-language model development in ophthalmology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the scarcity of large-scale, high-quality image-text datasets in ophthalmology, which limits the development of vision-language models for this field.

Method: The authors extracted figures directly from PDFs, decomposing them into panels and individual images, while annotating imaging modalities and mark statuses. Panel-level subcaptions were generated using a two-step large language model (LLM) approach.

Result: The models demonstrated high performance with a BLEU score of 0.913 for captioning, mAP@0.50 of 0.909 and 0.892 for panel/image detection, and median IoU of 0.997 for figure extraction.

Conclusion: PubMed-Ophtha provides a robust resource with an accompanying pipeline, ground-truth annotations, models, and tools to enhance reproducibility and advance vision-language research in ophthalmology.

Abstract: Vision-language models hold considerable promise for ophthalmology, but their development depends on large-scale, high-quality image-text datasets that remain scarce. We present PubMed-Ophtha, a hierarchical dataset of 102,023 ophthalmological image-caption pairs extracted from 15,842 open-access articles in PubMed Central. Unlike existing datasets, figures are extracted directly from article PDFs at full resolution and decomposed into their constituent panels, panel identifiers, and individual images. Each image is annotated with its imaging modality -- color fundus photography, optical coherence tomography, retinal imaging, or other -- and a mark status indicating the presence of annotation marks such as arrows. Figure captions are split into panel-level subcaptions using a two-step LLM approach, achieving a mean average sentence BLEU score of 0.913 on human-annotated data. Panel and image detection models reach a mAP@0.50 of 0.909 and 0.892, respectively, and figure extraction achieves a median IoU of 0.997. To support reproducibility, we additionally release the human-annotated ground-truth data, all trained models, and the full dataset generation pipeline.

</details>


### [399] [NTIRE 2026 Challenge on Efficient Low Light Image Enhancement: Methods and Results](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02212)
*Jiebin Yan,Chenyu Tu,Weixia Zhang,Zhihua Wang,Peibei Cao,Qinghua Lin,Yuming Fang,Xiaoning Liu,Zongwei Wu,Zhuyun Zhou,Radu Timofte*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper reviews the NITRE 2026 E-LLIE Challenge, examining methods for mobile low-light image enhancement focused on balancing quality and computational efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of enhancing low-light images for mobile devices while ensuring lightweight and efficient solutions.

Method: The paper evaluates submissions from 27 teams, systematically assessing recent methods for efficient low-light image enhancement.

Result: The review highlights significant progress in state-of-the-art methods, showing improved performance and efficiency in low-light image enhancement.

Conclusion: Key insights into modern advancements in lightweight low-light enhancement approaches are provided, emphasizing their deployability under resource constraints.

Abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive review of the NITRE 2026 Efficient Low Light Image Enhancement (E-LLIE) Challenge, highlighting the proposed solutions and final outcomes. This challenge focuses on mobile image enhancement under low-light conditions, aiming to design lightweight networks that improve enhancement quality while ensuring practical deployability under limited computational resources. A total of 207 participants registered, 27 teams submitted valid entries, and 17 teams ultimately provided valid factsheet. Based on these submissions, this paper provides a systematic evaluation of recent methods for E-LLIE, offering a comprehensive overview of state-of-the-art progress and demonstrating significant improvements in both performance and efficiency.

</details>


### [400] [InfiltrNet: Dual-Branch CNN-Transformer Architecture for Brain Tumor Infiltration Risk Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02230)
*S M Asif Hossain,Shruti Kshirsagar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces InfiltrNet, a novel model that predicts infiltration risk maps of gliomas, beyond visible MRI boundaries, using a dual-branch CNN and Swin Transformer architecture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurately predicting the infiltration extent of gliomas in surrounding brain tissue is crucial for effective surgical and radiation treatments, but current methods only focus on visible tumor segmentation.

Method: The proposed InfiltrNet employs a dual-branch architecture with CNN and Swin Transformer encoders fused via cross-attention modules. It uses a newly designed label generation approach and a combined loss function to improve predictions.

Result: Extensive evaluations on BraTS 2020 and 2025 datasets indicate that InfiltrNet surpasses five baseline models in performance.

Conclusion: InfiltrNet effectively predicts clinically relevant infiltration risk regions and elucidates model decisions via explainability tools such as GradCAM++ and Occlusion sensitivity.

Abstract: Gliomas are aggressive brain tumors that infiltrate surrounding tissue beyond the visible tumor margins observed on Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI). Predicting the spatial extent of this infiltration is essential for surgical planning and radiation therapy, yet existing deep learning approaches focus on segmenting the visible tumor rather than estimating infiltration risk in the surrounding tissue. This paper presents InfiltrNet, a novel dual-branch architecture that combines a convolutional neural network (CNN) encoder with a Swin Transformer encoder through cross-attention fusion modules to predict three-zone infiltration risk maps from multimodal MRI. A label generation strategy based on distance transforms is proposed to derive reproducible infiltration risk zones from standard Brain Tumor Segmentation (BraTS) annotations. InfiltrNet is trained with a combined Dice-CrossEntropy and boundary-aware loss augmented by auxiliary supervision heads at intermediate decoder levels. Extensive experiments on BraTS 2020 and BraTS 2025 demonstrate that InfiltrNet outperforms five established baselines. Explainability analysis using GradCAM++ and Occlusion sensitivity confirms that the model attends to clinically relevant peritumoral regions.

</details>


### [401] [Fine-Tuning Impairs the Balancedness of Foundation Models in Long-tailed Personalized Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02247)
*Shihao Hou,Chikai Shang,Zhiheng Yang,Jiacheng Yang,Xinyi Shang,Junlong Gao,Yiqun Zhang,Yang Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces FedPuReL, a new method to tackle challenges in personalized federated learning (PFL) with long-tailed class distributions and non-IID data, outperforming existing approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of current PFL methods that degrade performance in long-tailed distributions due to erosion of class balance and biased personalization.

Method: FedPuReL uses gradient purification via zero-shot predictions to maintain a balanced global model and applies residual learning for unbiased local model personalization.

Result: Experiments show that FedPuReL achieves better results than state-of-the-art methods in both global and personalized settings under diverse scenarios.

Conclusion: The proposed method ensures class balance and unbiased personalization, making it highly effective for real-world PFL challenges, backed by its superior performance in experiments.

Abstract: Personalized federated learning (PFL) with foundation models has emerged as a promising paradigm enabling clients to adapt to heterogeneous data distributions. However, real-world scenarios often face the co-occurrence of non-IID data and long-tailed class distributions, presenting unique challenges that remain underexplored in PFL. In this paper, we investigate this long-tailed personalized federated learning and observe that current methods suffer from two limitations: (i) fine-tuning degrades performance below zero-shot baselines due to the erosion of inherent class balance in foundation models; (ii) conventional personalization techniques further transfer this bias to local models through parameter or feature-level fusion. To address these challenges, we propose Federated Learning via Gradient Purification and Residual Learning (FedPuReL), which preserves balanced knowledge in the global model while enabling unbiased personalization. Specifically, we purify local gradients using zero-shot predictions to maintain a class-balanced global model, and model personalization as residual correction atop the frozen global model. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FedPuReL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior performance on both global and personalized models across diverse long-tailed scenarios. The code is available at https://github.com/shihaohou/FedPuReL.

</details>


### [402] [SpectraDINO: Bridging the Spectral Gap in Vision Foundation Models via Lightweight Adapters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02258)
*Yagiz Nalcakan,Hyeongjin Ju,Incheol Park,Sanghyeop Yeo,Youngwan Jin,Shiho Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SpectraDINO develops a multispectral Vision Foundation Model extending RGB-centric representation to modalities like NIR, SWIR, and LWIR, achieving state-of-the-art performance in spectral tasks through novel training protocols.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multispectral imaging, including NIR, SWIR, and LWIR, offers enhanced perception capabilities in adverse conditions, but RGB-focused pretrained models struggle with these modalities due to domain gaps.

Method: SpectraDINO employs per-modality bottleneck adapters to integrate multispectral data into a frozen RGB backbone. It uses a multi-stage teacher-student training approach leveraging techniques such as cosine distillation, symmetric contrastive loss, and neighborhood-structure-preservation loss to ensure cross-modal alignment.

Result: SpectraDINO achieved state-of-the-art results in multispectral object detection and semantic segmentation tasks across NIR, SWIR, and LWIR benchmarks using fusion techniques.

Conclusion: SpectraDINO effectively bridges the gap between RGB-centric and multispectral modalities, offering robust multispectral perception capabilities without compromising existing RGB representations.

Abstract: Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) pretrained on large-scale RGB data have demonstrated remarkable representation quality, yet their applicability to multispectral imaging spanning Near-Infrared (NIR), Short-Wave Infrared (SWIR), and Long-Wave Infrared (LWIR) remains largely unexplored. These spectral modalities offer complementary sensing capabilities critical for robust perception in adverse conditions, but present a fundamental domain gap relative to RGB-centric pretrained models. We present SpectraDINO, a multispectral VFM that bridges this spectral gap by extending DINOv2 ViT backbones to beyond-visible modalities through lightweight, per-modality bottleneck adapters, while preserving the rich representations of the frozen RGB backbone. We introduce a multi-stage teacher-student training protocol in which a frozen DINOv2 teacher guides a spectral student via cosine distillation, symmetric contrastive loss, patch-level alignment, and a novel neighborhood-structure-preservation loss. This staged curriculum enables strong cross-modal alignment without catastrophic forgetting of RGB priors. We evaluate SpectraDINO on multispectral object detection and semantic segmentation across challenging NIR, SWIR, and LWIR benchmarks using widely adopted fusion strategies. SpectraDINO achieves state-of-the-art performance across most benchmarks, validating its effectiveness as a general-purpose backbone for spectral generalization. The code and weights for model variants are available at https://github.com/Yonsei-STL/SpectraDINO.

</details>


### [403] [Rethinking Electro-Optical Vision Foundation Models for Remote Sensing Retrieval: A Controlled Comparison with Generalist VFM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02283)
*Hyobin Park,Minseok Seo,Dong-Geol Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A comparison of electro-optical (EO)-specific and generalist vision foundation models for remote sensing image retrieval reveals that generalist models often outperform EO-specific models in cross-scene evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Remote sensing imagery requires costly data acquisition and expert annotations; vision foundation models can mitigate this by leveraging large-scale unlabeled visual data.

Method: Controlled comparison of representative EO-specific and generalist models using identical datasets, retrieval protocol, and evaluation metrics, focusing on in-domain and cross-scene performances.

Result: Generalist models perform competitively, sometimes outperforming EO-specific models, especially in cross-scene evaluation where EO-specific models exhibit degradation.

Conclusion: Current EO-specific pretraining needs improvement as generalist models demonstrate stable transfer. Future efforts should integrate remote sensing imagery's unique physical, spatial, spectral, and geographic properties.

Abstract: Vision foundation models have attracted significant attention for their ability to leverage large-scale unlabeled visual data. This advantage is particularly important in remote sensing, where data acquisition is costly and annotation often requires expert knowledge. Recent electro-optical vision foundation models aim to learn domain-specific representations from remote sensing imagery, but it remains unclear whether they are more effective than strong generalist vision foundation models under retrieval-based evaluation. In this study, we conduct a controlled comparison between representative EO-specific and generalist vision foundation models for remote sensing image retrieval. Using the same datasets, retrieval protocol, and evaluation metric, we evaluate both in-domain performance and cross-scene generalization. Our results show that strong generalist vision foundation models are competitive with, and in some cases outperform, existing EO-specific models. Moreover, EO-specific models often suffer from substantial degradation under cross-scene evaluation, while generalist models show more stable transfer. These findings suggest that EO pretraining alone does not guarantee stronger retrieval-oriented remote sensing representations. We discuss the limitations of current EO-specific pretraining strategies and highlight the need for future EO vision foundation models to better exploit the physical, spatial, spectral, and geographic characteristics of remote sensing imagery.

</details>


### [404] [Beyond Known Objects: A Novel Framework for Open-Set Object Detection using Negative-Aware Norm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02284)
*Yuchen Zhang,Yao Lu,Johannes Betz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces NAN-SPOT, a lightweight framework for open-set object detection (OSOD) that performs better in unknown object detection without retraining traditional detectors. 


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation for this paper is to address the challenges in autonomous driving perception systems, specifically the ability to identify and localize both known and unseen objects without extensive computational and retraining efforts.

Method: The approach relies on standard detectors trained on diverse known categories and introduces a training-light framework that uses a metric called Negative-Aware Norm (NAN) from hidden layers. NAN-SPOT requires minimal training (just minutes on hundreds of images) without retraining the base detector.

Result: NAN-SPOT demonstrates competitive performance on detecting unknown objects while maintaining high accuracy for known objects. A new dataset, COCO-Open, was introduced to support more comprehensive OSOD evaluations.

Conclusion: NAN-SPOT is an efficient, robust solution for open-world perception systems, particularly in autonomous driving settings, as it reduces resource usage while maintaining reliable detection capabilities.

Abstract: Open-Set Object Detection (OSOD) is crucial for autonomous driving, where perception systems must recognize and localize both known and previously unseen objects in complex, dynamic environments. While recent approaches deliver promising results, they often require retraining the detector extensively to learn objectness, which describes the likelihood that a bounding box tightly encloses a valid object, regardless of whether its category was learned during training. Deviating from existing work, we hypothesize that standard off-the-shelf detectors may already contain helpful cues for objectness, owing to their training on numerous and diverse known categories. Building on this idea, we propose NAN-SPOT, a training-light framework that does not require to retrain the base object detector and estimates objectness by leveraging a hidden layer metric called Negative-Aware Norm (NAN), requiring only minutes of training on just hundreds of images. To support comprehensive evaluation, we introduce COCO-Open, an expanded version of the existing COCO-Mixed dataset, increasing unknown object annotations from 433 to 1853, making it the most exhaustively labeled dataset for OSOD to the best of our knowledge. Experimental results demonstrate that NAN-SPOT achieves even better performance on unknown object detection than methods requiring heavy training, without compromising performance on known objects. This efficiency and robustness make NAN-SPOT a promising step towards open-world perception in autonomous driving.

</details>


### [405] [LabBuilder: Protocol-Grounded 3D Layout Generation for Interactable and Safe Laboratory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02288)
*Jianbao Cao,Zhangrui Zhao,Bohan Feng,Zixuan Hu,Rui Li,Haiyuan Wan,Chenxi Li,Jingyuan Li,Wenzhe Cai,Lei Bai,Wanli Ouyang,Lingyu Duan,Di Huang,Mingting Pan,Sha Zhang,Xinzhu Ma,Shixiang Tang,Dongzhan Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LabBuilder is an end-to-end system designed to generate and verify 3D laboratory layouts from textual inputs, ensuring functional validity and safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The deployment of automated laboratories is challenging due to difficulties in designing safe and functional environments using existing 3D scene generation methods, which focus largely on visual aesthetics rather than practical scientific needs.

Method: The system, LabBuilder, consists of three components: LabForge (curates a meta-dataset and processes natural language inputs into structured protocols), LabGen (generates layouts via constraint-aware optimization), and LabTouchstone (evaluates layouts for functional validity and safety).

Result: LabBuilder outperformed state-of-the-art 3D scene generation methods, providing realistic, valid, and safe laboratory setups suitable for complex experiments.

Conclusion: LabBuilder effectively bridges the gap between simulator-based design and the practical functional requirements of automated laboratories, promoting safer and more efficient experimental environments.

Abstract: Automated laboratories hold the promise of accelerating scientific discovery, yet their deployment is bottlenecked by the difficulty of designing safe and executable environments. While simulator-based design offers scalability, existing 3D scene generation methods are primarily tailored for household settings, optimizing for visual plausibility while neglecting the rigorous functional semantics and safety constraints essential for scientific experimentation. We present LabBuilder, an end-to-end system that generates and verifies 3D laboratory layouts from concise textual specifications. It operates through three tightly coupled components: LabForge first curates a meta-dataset of annotated assets and chemical knowledge, translating natural language specifications into structured protocols; building on these protocols, LabGen synthesizes laboratory layouts via an iterative, constraint-aware optimization strategy; finally, LabTouchstone evaluates the resulting layouts as a unified benchmark. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LabBuilder significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, producing laboratory environments that are not only realistic but also functionally valid and safe for complex experimental workflows.

</details>


### [406] [A Hybrid Approach for Closing the Sim2real Appearance Gap in Game Engine Synthetic Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02291)
*Stefanos Pasios*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper investigates enhancing photorealism in synthetic datasets using a state-of-the-art diffusion model (FLUX.2-4B Klein) and a traditional translation model (REGEN), alongside proposing a hybrid approach combining both techniques for improved results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the sim2real gap that limits the utility of synthetic datasets in real-world applications by leveraging advanced image manipulation techniques.

Method: A comparison of the photorealism capabilities of FLUX.2-4B Klein and REGEN models, alongside a proposed hybrid approach that integrates diffusion-based and image-to-image translation methods.

Result: REGEN outperformed FLUX.2-4B Klein, and the proposed hybrid approach delivered improved visual realism and maintained semantic consistency better than either model individually.

Conclusion: Enhancing visual realism in synthetic datasets through a hybrid method effectively mitigates the sim2real appearance gap, providing more realistic datasets for computer vision applications.

Abstract: Video game engines have been an important source for generating large volumes of visual synthetic datasets for training and evaluating computer vision algorithms that are to be deployed in the real world. While the visual fidelity of modern game engines has been significantly improved with technologies such as ray-tracing, a notable sim2real appearance gap between the synthetic and the real-world images still remains, which limits the utilization of synthetic datasets in real-world applications. In this letter, we investigate the ability of a state-of-the-art image generation and editing diffusion model (FLUX.2-4B Klein) to enhance the photorealism of synthetic datasets and compare its performance against a traditional image-to-image translation model (REGEN). Furthermore, we propose a hybrid approach that combines the strong geometry and material transformations of diffusion-based methods with the distribution-matching capabilities of image-to-image translation techniques. Through experiments, it is demonstrated that REGEN outperforms FLUX.2-4B Klein and that by combining both FLUX.2-4B Klein and REGEN models, better visual realism can be achieved compared to using each model individually, while maintaining semantic consistency. The code is available at: https://github.com/stefanos50/Hybrid-Sim2Real

</details>


### [407] [Momentum-Anchored Multi-Scale Fusion Model for Long-Tailed Chest X-Ray Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02292)
*Duy Hoang Khuong,Duy Nguyen Huu,Ngu Huynh Cong Viet*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Chest X-ray classification suffers from class imbalance, and this paper introduces a Momentum-Anchored Multi-Scale Fusion Network that addresses this issue by using exponential moving averages (EMA) to stabilize features. It achieves superior performance, especially on rare pathologies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Class imbalance in Chest X-ray classification leads to gradient-driven feature instability and poor performance on rare, critical pathologies.

Method: The paper proposes a method using exponential moving averages (EMA) for momentum anchoring to stabilize feature representations. This is combined with selective momentum updates in an EfficientNet backbone and multi-scale spatial fusion.

Result: The approach achieves 0.8682 average AUC on the ChestX-ray14 dataset, with strong performance improvements on rare pathologies such as Hernia (0.9470 AUC) and Pneumonia (0.8165 AUC).

Conclusion: Momentum anchoring mitigates feature instability and significantly improves performance in the context of long-tailed medical image classification, particularly for rare pathologies.

Abstract: Chest X-ray classification suffers from severe class imbalance where gradient updates bias toward majority classes, causing feature drift and poor performance on rare but critical pathologies. We propose a Momentum-Anchored Multi-Scale Fusion Network that uses exponential moving averages (EMA) as a temporal anchoring mechanism to stabilize feature representations under long-tailed distributions. Our approach applies selective momentum updates to the final expansion block of an EfficientNet backbone, creating a slowly-evolving reference branch that resists gradient-induced drift while preserving discriminative patterns for minority classes. Combined with multi-scale spatial fusion ($1\times 1$, $3 \times 3$, $5 \times 5$ convolutions), this anchoring strategy maintains representational stability throughout training. On ChestX-ray14, our method achieves 0.8682 average AUC, outperforming state-of-the-art approaches and showing particular improvements on rare pathologies like Hernia (0.9470) and Pneumonia (0.8165). The results demonstrate that momentum anchoring effectively counters feature instability in long-tailed medical image classification.

</details>


### [408] [Open-access model for detecting openly dumped dispersed municipal solid waste from crowdsourced UAV imagery in Sub-Saharan Africa](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02316)
*Steffen Knoblauch,Ram Kumar Muthusamy,Luis M. A. Bettencourt,Costas Velis,Pierre Chrzanowski,Edward Charles Anderson,Pete Masters,Innocent Maholi,Antonio Inguane,Levi Szamek,Alexander Zipf*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a deep learning model trained with UAV imagery to detect dispersed solid waste in Sub-Saharan Africa, aiming to aid localized waste management.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sub-Saharan Africa faces challenges in waste management due to informal dumping and the lack of efficient spatial monitoring tools.

Method: A deep learning model was trained on crowdsourced UAV imagery and manually annotated data across diverse regions to detect and predict waste accumulation.

Result: The model showed excellent performance, revealing waste accumulation patterns tied to population density and infrastructure deficits, while weakly correlated with broader regional development metrics.

Conclusion: The open-access model empowers local stakeholders to use UAV imagery for informed waste management interventions, addressing environmental and health risks effectively.

Abstract: Managing municipal solid waste in rapidly urbanizing Sub-Saharan Africa remains challenging due to dispersed informal dumping and limited high-resolution datasets for spatial monitoring. We present an open-access deep learning model for automated detection of openly dumped dispersed solid waste via crowdsourced UAV imagery, trained and evaluated across 29 regions in 10 countries, encompassing diverse environmental contexts. A deep learning model trained on manually annotated image tiles achieved excellent performance in detecting openly dumped dispersed solid waste across all study regions. Predicted distributions reveal heterogeneous accumulation patterns, ranging from localized hotspots - often along waterways, where waste can exacerbate flood and public health risks - to more dispersed litter across urban areas. Waste accumulation is most strongly associated with population density and indicators of lack of local infrastructure access, whereas its relationship with broader measures of regional development is weaker, highlighting the importance of fine-scale data for understanding localized waste dynamics. By releasing the model, this study provides a ready-to-use tool for UAV imagery collected by municipalities and local mapping communities, enabling openly dumped dispersed solid waste monitoring without extensive technical expertise. This approach empowers local practitioners to convert UAV imagery into actionable insights, supporting targeted interventions and improved municipal solid waste management across Sub-Saharan Africa.

</details>


### [409] [Improving Imbalanced Multi-Label Chest X-Ray Diagnosis via CBAM-Enhanced CNN Backbones](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02328)
*Duy Nguyen Huu,Duy Hoang Khuong,Ngu Huynh Cong Viet*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes an enhanced CNN model with integrated CBAM to improve the multi-label classification of thoracic diseases in chest radiographs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The interpretation of chest radiography is challenging due to its reliance on expert knowledge, class imbalance issues, and difficulties in localizing multiple co-existing pathologies in images.

Method: A novel integration strategy of Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) with traditional CNN blocks is used to refine features and improve accuracy for multi-label classification tasks.

Result: The method achieved a mean AUC of 0.8695 on the ChestXray14 dataset, surpassing state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: Integrating CBAM with traditional CNN blocks effectively enhances feature extraction and multi-label classification performance for thoracic disease diagnosis using chest radiographs.

Abstract: Chest radiography is a widely used imaging modality for thoracic disease diagnosis, yet its conventional interpretation remains time-consuming and heavily dependent on expert knowledge. While deep learning has improved diagnostic efficiency through automated feature extraction, challenges such as class imbalance and the localization of multiple co-existing pathologies remain unsolved. In this paper, inspired by the strength of Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) in feature refinement and the capability of CNN blocks in feature extraction, we propose a strategy to integrate CBAM into traditional CNN blocks to enhance performance in multi-label classification tasks. Our method achieves a mean AUC of 0.8695 on ChestXray14 dataset, outperforming several state-of-the-art baselines.Our source code is available at: https://github.com/NNNguyenDuyyy/FETC_CBAM_Enhanced_CNN.git

</details>


### [410] [Channel-Level Relation to Attentive Aggregation with Neighborhood-Homogeneity Constraint for Point Cloud Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02357)
*Jiaqi Shi,Jin Xiao,Xiaoguang Hu,Wenxuan Ji,Zichong Jia,Zifan Long,Tianyou Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces PointCRA, a novel network for 3D point cloud understanding, enhancing feature discrimination via a channel-level metric-based mechanism. It achieves strong performance on key benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for 3D point cloud understanding face significant information loss due to their coarse-grained evaluation metrics, which limits downstream task precision.

Method: The authors propose a network named PointCRA that uses a temporal trend variation dimension and a multi-level weight calibration framework, paired with a dedicated loss function, to improve channel discriminability and feature aggregation.

Result: PointCRA achieves strong performance on various datasets: 77.5% mIoU on S3DIS, 90.4% OA on ScanObjectNN, and 87.4% instance mIoU on ShapeNetPart, demonstrating transferability and parameter efficiency.

Conclusion: The approach is effective, interpretable, and efficient, reducing information loss compared to prior methods. Its superiority is validated through benchmarks and analytical experiments, with the code publicly available.

Abstract: In 3D point cloud understanding, the core challenge lies in accurately capturing discriminative features within complex neighborhoods, which directly affects the execution precision of downstream tasks such as embodied AI and autonomous driving. Existing methods explore feature correlation discrimination but are limited to point-level spatial distribution or channel responses, enabling only coarse-grained level evaluation. For modern multi-scale point cloud networks, such coarse-grained metrics inevitably incur significant information loss in deeper layers. To address this issue, we propose a novel network equipped with a channel-level metric-based enhancement mechanism, termed the PointCRA network. Our core idea is to introduce temporal trend variation as a new evaluation dimension to avoid the information loss caused by weight dimension collapse in existing spatial and channel attention mechanisms. On this basis, we construct a multi-level calibration framework guided by neighborhood homogeneity for weight calibration, and design a dedicated loss function to enhance channel discriminability. The module effectively leverages the intrinsic feature priors of deep networks to adaptively correct the feature aggregation process, offering strong interpretability with low parameter overhead. Furthermore, our proposed method exhibits strong transferability, interpretability, and parameter efficiency. We validate the proposed method effectiveness on diverse datasets and benchmark models, and further demonstrate its rationality through extensive analytical experiments. Our PointCRA achieves 77.5% mIoU on the S3DIS dataset, 90.4% OA on the ScanObjectNN dataset, and 87.4% instance mIoU on the ShapeNetPart dataset. The code and pretrained weights are publicly available on GitHub:

</details>


### [411] [Graph-Augmented Topological Internalization with Dual-Stream Classifiers for Medical Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02376)
*Moyu Tang,Chupei Tang,Junxiao Kong,Di Wang,Tianchi Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes GDMRG, integrating graph-based knowledge and dual-stream classification to improve automated medical report generation, addressing disease co-occurrence and subtle lesion identification. Results show competitive clinical efficacy on MIMIC-CXR and robust generalization on IU X-Ray.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enhance the accuracy and reasoning capabilities of automated medical report generation systems, moving beyond isolated target classifications to address challenges stemming from complex disease relationships and subtle lesions.

Method: The method involves a Graph-Augmented Dual-Stream system, including a Topological Knowledge Internalization module using Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) for disease co-occurrence modeling, an optimized classification approach for imbalanced samples, and a Diagnosis-Guided Spatial Attention mechanism to link diagnosis with visual grounding.

Result: The proposed model achieved competitive clinical efficacy on the MIMIC-CXR dataset, maintained natural language fluency, and demonstrated strong zero-shot generalization performance on the IU X-Ray dataset.

Conclusion: The study provides an integrated, interpretable framework that addresses challenges in disease correlation, imbalanced data handling, and vision-language alignment, paving the way for more accurate and efficient medical report generation systems.

Abstract: Automated medical report generation, MRG, holds substantial value for alleviating radiologist workload and enhancing diagnostic efficiency. However, mainstream approaches typically treat diverse chest abnormalities as isolated classification targets. This paradigm often overlooks inherent disease co-occurrences and struggles to translate medical topological structures into explicit data correlations, constraining the model's reasoning capacity on complex or subtle lesions. To address this, we propose a Graph-Augmented Dual-Stream Medical Report Generation with Topological Internalization, GDMRG.
