Result: PFlowNet achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks V* Bench (90.6%) and MME-RealWorld-lite (67.0%), demonstrating its effectiveness in reasoning-oriented visual perception.

Conclusion: PFlowNet successfully tackles limitations of geometric priors by introducing a reasoning-effective visual framework, combining perceptual reliability and advanced visual reasoning.

Abstract: Despite the success of Large-Vision Language Models (LVLMs), general optimization objectives (e.g., standard MLE) fail to constrain visual trajectories, leading to language bias and hallucination. To mitigate this, current methods introduce geometric priors from visual experts as additional supervision. However, we observe that such supervision is typically suboptimal: it is biased toward geometric precision and offers limited reasoning utility. To bridge this gap, we propose Perceptual Flow Network (PFlowNet), which eschews rigid alignment with the expert priors and achieves interpretable yet more effective visual reasoning. Specifically, PFlowNet decouples perception from reasoning to establish a self-conditioned generation process. Based on this, it integrates multi-dimensional rewards with vicinal geometric shaping via variational reinforcement learning, thereby facilitating reasoning-oriented perceptual behaviors while preserving visual reliability. PFlowNet delivers a provable performance guarantee and competitive empirical results, particularly setting new SOTA records on V* Bench (90.6%) and MME-RealWorld-lite (67.0%).

</details>


### [442] [SIAM: Head and Brain MRI Segmentation from Few High-Quality Templates via Synthetic Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02737)
*Romain Valabregue,Ines Khemir,Eric Badinet,François Rousseau,Guillaume Auzias,Reuben Dorent*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The Segment It All Model (SIAM) is a novel 3D MRI segmentation model trained on only six high-quality manually annotated templates. It segments 16 anatomical structures, surpassing limitations of prior synthetic models in flexibility and scope.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of current synthetic brain MRI segmentation models which rely on hundreds of labeled templates, introducing biases and limited in incorporating new structures.

Method: Utilized domain randomization for both intensity and shape to create synthetic images, capturing variability in image contrasts and anatomical shapes. Trained using six annotated templates to segment 16 head structures.

Result: SIAM achieved high performance across diverse datasets, segments brain and extra-cerebral tissues, and demonstrates consistency across modalities (T1, T2, CT) and repeated scans with improved sensitivity for subtle gray matter changes.

Conclusion: SIAM advances brain MRI segmentation by offering improved flexibility, performance, and anatomical scope over existing methods, and is released openly for broad usability.

Abstract: Synthetic training has recently advanced brain MRI segmentation by enabling contrast-agnostic models trained entirely on generated data. However, most existing approaches rely on hundreds of automatically labeled templates, introducing systematic biases and limiting their flexibility to incorporate new anatomical structures. We present the Segment It All Model (SIAM), a 3D whole-head segmentation framework for 16 anatomical structures, trained using only six high-quality, manually annotated templates. SIAM extends domain randomization to both intensity and shape domains: synthetic image generation ensures contrast variability, while high-resolution spatial transformations model anatomical differences in cortical thickness and deep nuclei morphology. Unlike prior synthetic models, SIAM simultaneously segments brain as well as extra-cerebral tissues, including cerebrospinal fluid, vessels, dura mater, skull, and skin, enabling fully automated, preprocessing-free analysis. Evaluation across eight heterogeneous datasets (N=301), that include multiple contrasts (T1-weighted, T2-weighted, CT) and span a wide range of ages, demonstrates that SIAM matches or outperforms state-of-the-art methods for brain structures, in addition to extending automated segmentation to non-brain structures. The model also exhibits superior consistency across contrasts and repeated acquisitions, together with improved sensitivity to subtle gray matter atrophy. We openly release the model and the label templates at https://github.com/romainVala/SIAM.

</details>


### [443] [Virtual Scanning for NSCLC Histology: Investigating the Discriminatory Power of Synthetic PET](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02746)
*Fatih Aksu,Laura Ciuffetti,Francesco Di Feola,Filippo Ruffini,Giulia Romoli,Fabrizia Gelardi,Arturo Chiti,Valerio Guarrasi,Paolo Soda*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The study enhances CT-based classification of NSCLC subtypes by utilizing synthetic PET features generated through a 3D Pix2Pix GAN, achieving improved performance compared to using CT alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Differentiating between ADC and SCC histological subtypes in NSCLC is crucial for personalized treatment, although limitations exist with existing PET/CT applications due to their costs and radiation risks.

Method: A 3D Pix2Pix GAN, pretrained on the FDG-PET/CT dataset, synthesized pseudo-PET data from CT scans, which were then integrated with CT data in the MINT intermediate fusion framework for subtype classification.

Result: Integrating synthetic PET features improved classification metrics significantly, increasing the AUC from 0.489 to 0.591 and the GMean from 0.305 to 0.524 on a multi-center dataset of 714 subjects.

Conclusion: Synthetic PET data provide valuable complementary information for enhancing histological subtype classification in NSCLC, suggesting potential for applications in settings lacking traditional PET scanning.

Abstract: Accurate histological differentiation between adenocarcinoma (ADC) and squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is critical for personalized treatment in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). While [$^{18}$F]FDG PET/CT is a standard tool for the clinical evaluation of lung cancer, its utility is often limited by high costs and radiation exposure. In this paper, we investigate the feasibility of "virtual scanning" as a feature-enhancement strategy by evaluating whether synthetic PET data can provide complementary feature representations to supplement anatomical CT scans in histological subtype classification.
  We propose a framework that leverages a 3D Pix2Pix Generative Adversarial Network (GAN), pretrained on the FDG-PET/CT Lesions dataset, to synthesize pseudo-PET volumes from anatomical CT scans. These synthetic volumes are integrated with structural CT data within the MINT framework, a multi-stage intermediate fusion architecture.
  Our experiments, conducted on a multi-center dataset of 714 subjects, demonstrate that the inclusion of synthetic metabolic features significantly improves classification performance over a CT-only baseline. The multimodal approach achieved a statistically significant increase in the Area Under the Curve (AUC) from 0.489 to 0.591 and improved the Geometric Mean (GMean) from 0.305 to 0.524. These results suggest that synthetic PET scans provide discriminatory metabolic cues that enable deep learning models to exploit complementary cross-modal information, offering a potential feature-enhancement strategy for clinical scenarios where physical PET scans are unavailable.

</details>


### [444] [Does it Really Count? Assessing Semantic Grounding in Text-Guided Class-Agnostic Counting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02752)
*Giacomo Pacini,Luca Ciampi,Nicola Messina,Nicola Tonellotto,Giuseppe Amato,Fabrizio Falchi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper addresses flaws in open-world text-guided class-agnostic counting models, introducing robust evaluation protocols and datasets to improve their prompt grounding and semantic reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current CAC models struggle to align text prompts with visual scenes, leading to incorrect counting and unreliable performance in real-world scenarios. The paper aims to address these limitations by introducing a robust evaluation framework.

Method: The authors propose PrACo++, a test suite with negative-label and distractor tests paired with specialized metrics, and MUCCA, a new multi-category annotated dataset for evaluation. They also performed extensive experiments on 10 state-of-the-art models.

Result: Their evaluation revealed weaknesses in current models with respect to grounding text descriptions in visual objects. They showed how semantic similarity between prompts influences counting errors.

Conclusion: The paper emphasizes the necessity for semantically grounded architectures and introduces a reliable evaluation framework for future development in open-world CAC methods.

Abstract: Open-world text-guided class-agnostic counting (CAC) has emerged as a flexible paradigm for counting arbitrary object classes by using natural language prompts. However, current evaluation protocols primarily focus on standard counting errors within single-category images, overlooking a fundamental requirement: the ability to correctly ground the textual prompt in the visual scene. In this paper, we show that several state-of-the-art CAC models often struggle to determine which object class should be counted based on the given prompt, revealing a misalignment between textual semantics and visual object representations. This limitation leads to spurious counting responses and reduced reliability in real-world scenarios. To systematically address these limitations, we propose a new evaluation framework focused on model robustness and trustworthiness. Our contribution is two-fold: (i) we introduce PrACo++ (Prompt-Aware Counting++), a novel test suite featuring two dedicated evaluation protocols -- the negative-label test and the distractor test -- paired with new specialized metrics; and (ii) we present the MUCCA (MUlti-Category Class-Agnostic counting) evaluation dataset, a new collection of real-world images featuring multiple annotated object categories per scene, unlike existing CAC benchmarks that typically include a single category per image. Our extensive experimental evaluation of 10 state-of-the-art methods shows that, despite strong performance under standard counting metrics, current models exhibit significant weaknesses in understanding and grounding object class descriptions. Finally, we provide a quantitative analysis of how semantic similarity between prompts influences these failures. Overall, our results underscore the need for more semantically grounded architectures and offer a reliable framework for future assessment in open-world text-guided CAC methods.

</details>


### [445] [Unified Map Prior Encoder for Mapping and Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02762)
*Zongzheng Zhang,Sizhe Zou,Guantian Zheng,Zhenxin Zhu,Yu Gao,Guoxuan Chi,Shuo Wang,Yuwen Heng,Zhigang Sun,Yiru Wang,Hao Sun,Chao Ma,Zhen Li,Anqing Jiang,Hao Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces UMPE, a Unified Map Prior Encoder, to integrate diverse map priors effectively for autonomous driving tasks. This improves mapping accuracy and reduces planning trajectory errors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current autonomous driving approaches overly rely on sensor data, underutilizing diverse map priors due to challenges like pose drift and inconsistent availability.

Method: UMPE has two branches: a vector encoder (for SE(2) corrections and polyline confidence scoring) and a raster encoder (with ResNet-18 and FiLM for alignment and prior fusion). It uses a geometry-first, appearance-second fusion approach.

Result: UMPE improved mapping metrics on nuScenes (+5.9 mAP for MapTRv2, +5.3 mAP for MapQR) and Argoverse2 (+4.1 mAP). For E2E planning, trajectory error reduced by 0.30 m and collision rate reduced by 0.10%.

Conclusion: The unified approach of UMPE effectively combines heterogeneous map priors, significantly enhancing both mapping and planning in autonomous driving systems.

Abstract: Online mapping and end-to-end (E2E) planning in autonomous driving remain largely sensor-centric, leaving rich map priors, including HD/SD vector maps, rasterized SD maps, and satellite imagery, underused because of heterogeneity, pose drift, and inconsistent availability at test time. We present UMPE, a Unified Map Prior Encoder that can ingest any subset of four priors and fuse them with BEV features for both mapping and planning. UMPE has two branches. The vector encoder pre-aligns HD/SD polylines with a frame-wise SE(2) correction, encodes points via multi-frequency sinusoidal features, and produces polyline tokens with confidence scores. BEV queries then apply cross-attention with confidence bias, followed by normalized channel-wise gating to avoid length imbalance and softly down-weight uncertain sources. The raster encoder shares a ResNet-18 backbone conditioned by FiLM with scaling and shift at every stage, performs SE(2) micro-alignment, and injects priors through zero-initialized residual fusion, so the network starts from a do-no-harm baseline and learns to add only useful prior evidence. A vector-then-raster fusion order reflects the inductive bias of geometry first, appearance second. On nuScenes mapping, UMPE lifts MapTRv2 from 61.5 to 67.4 mAP (+5.9) and MapQR from 66.4 to 71.7 mAP (+5.3). On Argoverse2, UMPE adds +4.1 mAP over strong baselines. UMPE is compositional: when trained with all priors, it outperforms single-prior models even when only one prior is available at test time, demonstrating powerset robustness. For E2E planning with the VAD backbone on nuScenes, UMPE reduces trajectory error from 0.72 to 0.42 m L2 on average (-0.30 m) and collision rate from 0.22% to 0.12% (-0.10%), surpassing recent prior-injection methods. These results show that a unified, alignment-aware treatment of heterogeneous map priors yields better mapping and better planning.

</details>


### [446] [FoR-Net: Learning to Focus on Hard Regions for Efficient Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02764)
*Hsin-Jui Pan,Sheng-Wei Chan,Meng-Qian Li,Chun-Po Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introducing FoR-Net, a lightweight semantic segmentation model leveraging selective focus on hard regions for efficient and competitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for efficient semantic segmentation models that can handle challenging areas like thin structures and object boundaries without heavy global modeling.

Method: FoR-Net employs a selector module to predict region-wise importance, multi-scale convolutional branches for diverse context aggregation, and a Top-K activation mechanism to prioritize hard regions.

Result: FoR-Net achieves competitive performance on the Cityscapes benchmark under limited computational resources, showing improved accuracy in challenging regions.

Conclusion: Region-focused reasoning provides an effective inductive bias for lightweight and efficient semantic segmentation, as demonstrated by FoR-Net's competitive results.

Abstract: We present FoR-Net, a lightweight architecture for semantic segmentation that focuses on identifying and enhancing hard regions. Instead of relying on heavy global modeling, FoR-Net adopts an efficient strategy that selectively emphasizes informative regions through a learned importance map and a Top-K activation mechanism. Specifically, a selector module predicts region-wise importance, enabling the model to focus on challenging areas such as thin structures and object boundaries. Multi-scale reasoning is achieved using convolutional branches with different receptive fields, allowing diverse spatial context aggregation. We evaluate FoR-Net on the Cityscapes benchmark under limited computational resources. Despite its lightweight design and standard training configuration, FoR-Net achieves competitive performance and demonstrates improved consistency in challenging regions. These results suggest that region-focused reasoning provides a simple yet effective inductive bias for efficient semantic segmentation.

</details>


### [447] [TOC-SR: Task-Optimal Compact diffusion for Image Super Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02767)
*Sowmya Vajrala,Akshay Bankar,Manjunath Arveti,Shreyas Pandith,Sravanth Kodavanti,Subhajit Sanyal,Amit Unde,Srinivas Soumitri Miriyala*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes TOC-SR, a framework for efficient one-step super-resolution models by combining compact diffusion backbones and generative distillation, offering substantial reductions in computational complexity while maintaining high-quality reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models excel at image restoration and super-resolution but are computationally expensive due to their large size and iterative sampling, necessitating a more efficient method.

Method: The authors created a sixteen-channel latent diffusion model, optimized its architecture using generative distillation and Bayesian Optimization with constraints, and adapted the compact backbone for single-step super-resolution.

Result: The model achieves a 6.6x reduction in parameters and 2.8x reduction in GMACs compared to standard diffusion models, while maintaining strong reconstruction capabilities.

Conclusion: TOC-SR provides an efficient and high-performing solution to the computational challenges of diffusion-based super-resolution, reducing complexity without sacrificing fidelity.

Abstract: Diffusion models have recently demonstrated strong performance for image restoration tasks, including super-resolution. However, their large model size and iterative sampling procedures make them computationally expensive for practical deployment. In this work, we present TOC-SR, a framework for building efficient one-step super-resolution models by first discovering a compact diffusion backbone. Starting from a sixteen-channel latent diffusion model, we construct parameter-efficient surrogate blocks using feature-wise generative distillation and perform architecture discovery using epsilon-constrained Bayesian Optimization to minimize model complexity while preserving generative fidelity. The resulting compact diffusion backbone achieves a 6.6x reduction in parameters and a 2.8x reduction in GMACs compared to the expanded diffusion model. We then adapt this backbone for super-resolution and distill the diffusion process into a single-step generator. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed approach enables efficient super-resolution while maintaining strong reconstruction quality.

</details>


### [448] [Linearizing Vision Transformer with Test-Time Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02772)
*Yining Li,Dongchen Han,Zeyu Liu,Hanyi Wang,Yulin Wang,Gao Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper tackles the gap between linear-complexity attention mechanisms and Softmax attention's pretrained Transformers by proposing a direct weight transfer method using TTT architecture and introducing representational alignment techniques. The method enables faster inference while maintaining image generation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Linear-complexity attention mechanisms are hindered by high training costs compared to pretrained Softmax attention models. There is a need to bridge the representational gap for effective reuse of pretrained weights.

Method: The authors utilize Test-Time Training (TTT) as an aligned architecture for weight inheritance from pretrained Softmax attention. They also introduce key instance normalization and locality enhancement modules for representational alignment.

Result: The proposed SD3.5-T^5 model achieves comparable text-to-image generation quality to fine-tuned Softmax models, while offering faster inference speed—1.32× at 1K resolution and 1.47× at 2K resolution—with minimal fine-tuning efforts.

Conclusion: By addressing architectural and representational gaps, the work demonstrates a feasible path for weight transfer to linear-attention models, making them practical for applications requiring faster inference while preserving output quality.

Abstract: While linear-complexity attention mechanisms offer a promising alternative to Softmax attention for overcoming the quadratic bottleneck, training such models from scratch remains prohibitively expensive. Inheriting weights from pretrained Transformers provides an appealing shortcut, yet the fundamental representational gap between Softmax and linear attention prevents effective weight transfer. In this work, we address this conversion challenge from two perspectives: architectural alignment and representational alignment. We identify Test-Time Training (TTT) as a linear-complexity architecture whose two-layer dynamic formulation is structurally aligned with Softmax attention, enabling direct inheritance of pretrained attention weights. To further align representational properties, including key shift-invariance and locality, we introduce key instance normalization and a lightweight locality enhancement module. We validate our approach by linearizing Stable Diffusion 3.5 and introduce SD3.5-T$^5$ (Transformer To Test Time Training). With only 1 hour of fine-tuning on 4$\times$H20 GPUs, SD3.5-T$^5$ achieves comparable text-to-image quality to the fine-tuned Softmax model, while accelerating inference by 1.32$\times$ and 1.47$\times$ at 1K and 2K resolutions.

</details>


### [449] [HumanSplatHMR: Closing the Loop Between Human Mesh Recovery and Gaussian Splatting Avatar](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02784)
*Yeheng Zong,Pou-Chun Kung,Yike Pan,Seth Isaacson,Yizhou Chen,Ram Vasudevan,Katherine A. Skinner*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces HumanSplatHMR, a framework that jointly optimizes 3D human pose and high-fidelity avatar synthesis for improved rendering in novel poses and views.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods fail to accurately recover 3D human pose and geometry, either overfitting to 2D views or separating pose and appearance, limiting generalization.

Method: HumanSplatHMR integrates photometric, segmentation, and depth losses via a differentiable renderer to refine pose and produce better avatars, using only mesh estimates from pose estimators.

Result: The proposed approach improves pose recovery and avatar synthesis accuracy compared to baselines without image-level refinement or decoupled pose reconstruction.

Conclusion: HumanSplatHMR bridges geometric pose estimation with differentiable rendering to enhance 3D human reconstruction in practical, real-world settings.

Abstract: Accurately recovering human pose and appearance from video is an essential component of scene reconstruction, with applications to motion capture, motion prediction, virtual reality, and digital twinning. Despite significant interest in building realistic human avatars from video, this paper demonstrates that existing methods do not accurately recover the 3D geometry of humans. ViT-based approaches are not consistently reliable and can overfit to 2D views, while NeRF- and Gaussian Splatting-based avatars treat pose and appearance separately, limiting rendering generalization to new poses. To resolve these shortcomings, this paper proposes HumanSplatHMR, a joint optimization framework that refines 3D human poses while simultaneously learning a high-fidelity avatar for novel-view and novel-pose synthesis. Our key insight is to close the loop between geometric pose estimation and differentiable rendering. Unlike prior human avatar methods that rely on accurate human pose obtained through motion capture systems or offline refinement, which are impractical in in-the-wild scenarios, our approach uses only human mesh estimates from a state-of-the-art human pose estimator to better reflect real-world conditions. Therefore, instead of using the human pose only as a deformation prior, HumanSplatHMR backpropagates photometric, segmentation, and depth losses through a differentiable renderer to the pose parameters and global position. This coupling refines the global 3D pose over time, improving accuracy and alignment while producing better renderings from novel views. Experiments show consistent improvements over pose recovery baselines that omit image-level refinement and avatar baselines that decouple pose estimation from avatar reconstruction.

</details>


### [450] [Edge-Efficient Image Restoration: Transformer Distillation into State-Space Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02794)
*Srinivas Soumitri Miriyala,Sowmya Vajrala,Sravanth Kodavanti,Vikram Nelvoy Rajendiran,Sharan Kumar Allur*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a modular framework combining transformer and state-space model (SSM) blocks for image restoration, aiming to balance quality and runtime efficiency, particularly on mobile hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While transformers excel at global modeling, their inference time on high-resolution images is prohibitive on edge devices. SSMs, though faster, struggle with detailed tasks, necessitating a hybrid approach.

Method: The authors propose training lightweight SSM blocks as feature-distilled surrogates for transformer blocks, arranging them into hybrid architectures guided by Efficient Network Search (ENS), which automates block selection to optimize both restoration quality and runtime.

Result: Hybrid architectures discovered through ENS achieve up to 3.4x faster runtime than the Restormer baseline on mobile hardware while maintaining competitive image restoration quality.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates the feasibility of combining transformer's accuracy with SSM's efficiency to enhance image restoration performance on edge devices through automated architecture optimization.

Abstract: We propose a modular framework for hybrid image restoration that integrates transformer and state-space model (SSM) blocks with a focus on improving runtime efficiency on edge hardware. While transformers provide strong global modeling through self-attention, their attention kernels incur substantial latency on mobile devices, especially for high-resolution inputs. In contrast, SSMs such as Mamba offer lineartime sequence modeling with lower runtime overhead but may underperform on fine grained restoration tasks. To balance accuracy and efficiency, we train lightweight SSM blocks as feature-distilled surrogates of transformer blocks and use them to construct hybrid U-Net-style architectures. To automatically discover effective block combinations, we introduce Efficient Network Search (ENS), a multi-objective search strategy that selects task-specific hybrid configurations from pre-aligned components. ENS optimizes restoration quality while penalizing transformer usage, serving as a lightweight proxy for latency and enabling architecture discovery without repeated hardware profiling. On a Snapdragon 8 Elite CPU, the Restormer baseline requires 10119.52 ms for inference. In contrast, ENS-discovered hybrids significantly reduce runtime: ENS-Deblurring runs in 2973 ms (3.4x faster), ENS-Deraining in 5816 ms (1.74x faster), and ENS-Denoising in 8666 ms (1.17x faster), while maintaining competitive restoration quality.

</details>


### [451] [IConFace: Identity-Structure Asymmetric Conditioning for Unified Reference-Aware Face Restoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02814)
*Axi Niu,Jinyang Zhang,Senyan Qing*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces IConFace, a blind face restoration method that works with or without same-identity references, ensuring improved identity consistency and detail recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The challenge lies in tackling the ambiguity of restoring severely degraded facial images, especially when identity-critical details are lost. Same-identity references can help but often come with mismatched conditions that may result in over-reliance on the references.

Method: The authors propose IConFace, which combines reference-aware and no-reference restoration strategies. References are condensed into an AdaFace identity anchor, while the degraded image contributes structural information processed through low-rank residuals and block-wise degraded cross-attention.

Result: The IConFace model improves identity consistency, recovers fine details effectively, and provides high-quality restoration even in a no-reference scenario, under a unified framework.

Conclusion: IConFace successfully balances reference use and spatial information from degraded images, addressing challenges like mismatched reference conditions and ensuring flexible, high-quality face restoration.

Abstract: Blind face restoration is highly ill-posed under severe degradation, where identity-critical details may be missing from the degraded input. Same-identity references reduce this ambiguity, but mismatched pose, expression, illumination, age, makeup, or local facial states can lead to overuse of reference appearance. We propose \textbf{IConFace}, a unified reference-aware and no-reference framework with identity--structure asymmetric conditioning. References are distilled into a norm-weighted global AdaFace identity anchor for image-only modulation, while the degraded image is reinforced as the spatial structure anchor through low-rank residuals and block-wise degraded cross-attention with two-route memory. The resulting single checkpoint exploits references when available and falls back to no-reference restoration when absent, improving identity consistency, fine-detail recovery, and degraded-only restoration quality in a unified model.

</details>


### [452] [VideoNet: A Large-Scale Dataset for Domain-Specific Action Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02834)
*Tanush Yadav,Mohammadreza Salehi,Jae Sung Park,Vivek Ramanujan,Hannaneh Hajishirzi,Yejin Choi,Ali Farhadi,Rohun Tripathi,Ranjay Krishna*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VideoNet is introduced as an action recognition benchmark for domain-specific tasks, highlighting the challenges of vision-language models (VLMs). Models often underperform and benefit less from in-context examples compared to humans.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: This paper aims to revive interest in action recognition within video understanding, particularly in the context of modern VLMs, which have shifted away from evaluating domain-specific challenges due to dataset limitations.

Method: The authors introduce VideoNet, covering 1,000 distinct domain-specific actions from 37 domains. They test VLMs under various settings, assess their ability to learn from in-context examples, and fine-tune models on a new dataset of 500k video question-answer pairs.

Result: Gemini 3.1 Pro achieved better outcomes (69.9%) compared to Qwen3-VL-8B (45.0%). Binary settings showed slight improvements, but VLMs still lagged. Fine-tuning on the dataset allowed a Molmo2-4B model to outpace open-weight 8B models on the benchmark.

Conclusion: Current VLMs struggle with domain-specific action recognition and fail to fully leverage in-context learning. Fine-tuning with extensive domain-specific datasets shows promise in improving model performance for these tasks.

Abstract: Videos are unique in their ability to capture actions which transcend multiple frames. Accordingly, for many years action recognition was the quintessential task for video understanding. Unfortunately, due to a lack of sufficiently diverse and challenging data, modern vision-language models (VLMs) are no longer evaluated on their action recognition capabilities. To revitalize action recognition in the era of VLMs, we advocate for a returned focus on domain-specific actions. To this end, we introduce VideoNet, a domain-specific action recognition benchmark covering 1,000 distinct actions from 37 domains. We begin with a multiple-choice evaluation setting, where the difference between closed and open models is stark: Gemini 3.1 Pro attains 69.9% accuracy while Qwen3-VL-8B gets a mere 45.0%. To understand why VLMs struggle on VideoNet, we relax the questions into a binary setting, where random chance is 50%. Still, Qwen achieves only 59.2% accuracy. Further relaxing the evaluation setup, we provide $k\in\{1,2,3\}$ in-context examples of the action. Some models excel in the few-shot setting, while others falter; Qwen improves $+7.0\%$, while Gemini declines $-4.8\%$. Notably, these gains fall short of the $+13.6\%$ improvement in non-expert humans when given few-shot examples. Finding that VLMs struggle to fully exploit in-context examples, we shift from test-time improvements to the training side. We collect the first large-scale training dataset for domain-specific actions, totaling nearly 500k video question-answer pairs. Fine-tuning a Molmo2-4B model on our data, we surpass all open-weight 8B models on the VideoNet benchmark.

</details>


### [453] [Active Sampling for Ultra-Low-Bit-Rate Video Compression via Conditional Controlled Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02849)
*Amirhosein Javadi,Shirin Saeedi Bidokhti,Tara Javidi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ActDiff-VC is a diffusion-based video compression framework designed for ultra-low-bitrate scenarios, achieving high perceptual quality with efficient generative reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to leverage diffusion models for perceptually realistic video reconstruction at ultra-low bitrates by developing methods for compact and effective control signals.

Method: The method involves partitioning videos into segments, sending keyframes selectively, and summarizing motion using sparse, budget-optimized point trajectories. A conditional diffusion decoder synthesizes frames based on these inputs.

Result: ActDiff-VC showed significant improvements: up to 64.6% bitrate reduction while maintaining NIQE, and substantial gains in KID (64.6%) and FID (37.7%) over strong learned codecs in benchmarks like UVG and MCL-JCV.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates superior performance in delivering perceptual quality at extreme compression levels, outperforming both learned and diffusion-based baselines in the ultra-low-bitrate regime.

Abstract: Diffusion models provide a powerful generative prior for perceptual reconstruction at ultra-low bitrates, but effective video compression requires controlling the generative process using highly compact conditioning signals. In this work, we present ActDiff-VC, a diffusion-based video compression framework for the ultra-low-bitrate regime. Our method partitions videos into variable-length segments, transmits keyframes only when needed, and summarizes temporal dynamics using a compact set of tracked point trajectories. Conditioned on these sparse signals, a conditional diffusion decoder synthesizes the remaining frames, enabling perceptually realistic reconstruction under severe rate constraints. To support this design, we introduce two mechanisms: content-adaptive keyframe selection and budget-aware sparse trajectory selection, which together enable compact yet effective conditioning for generative reconstruction. Experiments on the UVG and MCL-JCV benchmarks show that ActDiff-VC achieves up to 64.6\% bitrate reduction at matched NIQE, improves KID by up to 64.6\% and FID by up to 37.7\% at comparable bitrates against strong learned codecs, and delivers favorable perceptual rate--distortion trade-offs relative to learned and diffusion-based baselines in the ultra-low-bitrate regime.

</details>


### [454] [Pixel Perfect: Relational Image Quality Assessment with Spatially-Aware Distortions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02863)
*Fadeel Sher Khan,Long N. Le,Abhinau K. Venkataramanan,Seok-Jun Lee,Hamid R. Sheikh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The study introduces a novel, interpretable image quality assessment (IQA) method that relies on self-supervised learning and directional, relational quality scoring, avoiding human-labeled data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the resource-intensive and non-interpretable nature of traditional IQA metrics based on mean opinion scores (MOS).

Method: A self-supervised synthetic distortion engine generates training data, followed by training a distortion prediction network with an anti-symmetric objective to produce disentangled spatial distortion maps. A scoring network is then trained using contrastive learning on ordinally ranked image sets to produce relational quality scores.

Result: The method achieves spatially-aware, interpretable distortion maps and relational quality scores without requiring manual annotations.

Conclusion: This approach facilitates granular, interpretable IQA applicable for optimizing image processing algorithms without relying on human-labeled scores.

Abstract: Traditional image quality assessment (IQA) methods rely on mean opinion scores (MOS), which are resource-intensive to collect and fail to provide interpretable, localized feedback on specific image distortions. We overcome these limitations by shifting from absolute quality prediction to a relational and directional assessment. Our approach utilizes a self-supervised synthetic distortion engine to generate training data, eliminating the need for manual annotation. A distortion prediction network is trained with an anti-symmetric objective to produce spatially-aware, disentangled maps that identify the type, intensity, and direction of distortions relative to a reference image. Subsequently, a scoring network is trained via contrastive learning on ordinally ranked image sets to predict a relational quality score. Our method provides a more granular and interpretable approach to IQA for the targeted optimization of image processing algorithms without requiring any human-labeled quality scores.

</details>


### [455] [Laplacian Frequency Interaction Network for Rural Thematic Road Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02866)
*Baiyan Chen,Weixin Zhai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces LFINet, a neural network aimed at constructing accurate rural road networks from noisy and sparse agricultural trajectory images. It outperforms prior methods with higher accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address two significant challenges in constructing thematic road networks: the loss of detail caused by downsampling methods and noise-induced fragmentation or redundancy in extracted networks.

Method: The method involves a neural network called LFINet, which separates images into low-frequency and high-frequency components and integrates them through a multi-stage architecture to reconstruct detailed and consistent road networks.

Result: The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance with an F1-score of 92.54% and IoU of 86.12%, surpassing previous methods in accuracy.

Conclusion: LFINet demonstrates its strength in constructing topological road networks effectively from complex field data, proving its practical utility in agricultural applications.

Abstract: Rural thematic road network construction aims to extract topological road structures from movement trajectory images of agricultural machinery. However, this task faces challenges where downsampling methods commonly used in existing studies tend to blur the sparse high-frequency road structures, and the heavy noise from dense field operations often leads to fragmented or redundant topologies in the extracted networks. To address these challenges, we propose LFINet, a Laplacian Frequency Interaction Network. The network begins with a Laplacian Multi-scale Separator (LMS) to decouple the image into low-frequency semantic contexts and high-frequency structural details. These components are then processed by the Cross-Frequency Interaction Block (CFIB) through a dual-pathway architecture in which a High-Frequency Block (HFB) refines local structures while a Spatial Transformer (ST) captures global semantics. Subsequently, a Frequency Gated Modulation (FGM) mechanism integrates the features from pathways by leveraging semantic contexts to calibrate the structural details. Finally, a Progressive Reconstruction Decoder iteratively fuses multi-scale features to ensure topological consistency. Experiments conducted on a real-world agricultural trajectories dataset from Henan Province, China, show that LFINet establishes a new state-of-the-art. Specifically, it achieves an F1-score of 92.54% and an IoU of 86.12%, surpassing the second-ranked method by 0.64% and 1.1%, respectively. This confirms its capability to effectively construct topological road networks from noisy and sparse field data.

</details>


### [456] [AlbumFill: Album-Guided Reasoning and Retrieval for Personalized Image Completion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02892)
*Yu-Ju Tsai,Brian Price,Qing Liu,Luis Figueroa,Daniil Pakhomov,Zhihong Ding,Scott Cohen,Ming-Hsuan Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper presents AlbumFill, a system for personalized image completion using identity-consistent references retrieved from personal photo albums via a vision-language model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the shortcomings of existing image inpainting methods, which either fail to maintain identity consistency or require explicitly provided references, by proposing a method that retrieves suitable references directly from personal photo collections.

Method: AlbumFill employs a vision-language model to infer missing semantic cues for composed image retrieval from personal albums, and these retrieved images are used by reference-based completion models.

Result: The authors also introduce a dataset with 54K human-centric samples and find through experiments that their proposed method performs well compared to baselines, demonstrating the difficulty and importance of retrieving identity-consistent references for personalized image completion.

Conclusion: AlbumFill highlights a novel, training-free approach to image inpainting that leverages personal albums for retrieving identity-consistent references, offering improved results for personalized image completion tasks.

Abstract: Personalized image completion aims to restore occluded regions in personal photos while preserving identity and appearance. Existing methods either rely on generic inpainting models that often fail to maintain identity consistency, or assume that suitable reference images are explicitly provided. In practice, suitable references are often not explicitly provided, requiring the system to search for identity-consistent images within personal photo collections. We present AlbumFill, a training-free framework that retrieves identity-consistent references from personal albums for personalized completion. Given an occluded image and a personal album, a vision-language model infers missing semantic cues to guide composed image retrieval, and the retrieved references are used by reference-based completion models. To facilitate this task, we introduce a dataset containing 54K human-centric samples with associated album images. Experiments across multiple baselines demonstrate the difficulty of personalized completion and highlight the importance of identity-consistent reference retrieval. Project Page: https://liagm.github.io/AlbumFill/

</details>


<div id='cs.DC'></div>

# cs.DC [[Back]](#toc)

### [457] [GhostServe: A Lightweight Checkpointing System in the Shadow for Fault-Tolerant LLM Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00831)
*Shakya Jayakody,Youpeng Zhao,Chinmay Dhanraj Nehate,Jun Wang*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper introduces GhostServe, a checkpointing solution to enhance fault tolerance in LLM serving systems. By using erasure coding for KV cache recovery, it significantly reduces checkpointing and recovery latencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The long-running nature of LLM tasks, driven by million-token, agent-based applications, causes susceptibility to hardware and software faults, leading to inefficiencies and poor user experience. Addressing failures in the stateful KV cache becomes crucial.

Method: The authors propose GhostServe, which applies erasure coding to the KV cache to create parity shards stored in host memory. In case of failures, this approach enables quick reconstruction of KV cache without triggering full recomputation or state replication.

Result: The solution achieves notable improvements: checkpointing latency reduced by up to 2.7x, recovery latency by 2.1x in single-batch scenarios, and 1.2x lower median response latency under failure conditions.

Conclusion: GhostServe is an effective method for cost-efficient and fault-tolerant LLM serving, offering high availability and minimized latency during failures.

Abstract: The rise of million-token, agent-based applications has placed unprecedented demands on large language model (LLM) inference services. The long-running nature of these tasks increases their susceptibility to hardware and software faults, leading to costly job failures, wasted resources, and degraded user experience. The stateful key-value (KV) cache, which grows with the sequence length, presents a central challenge as it is a critical and vulnerable component in distributed serving systems. In this work, we propose GhostServe, a novel checkpointing solution to facilitate fault-tolerant LLM serving. Specifically, GhostServe protects the streaming KV cache in the shadow by applying erasure coding to generate and store the parity shards in host memory. In the event of device failures, GhostServe enables fast reconstruction of the lost KV cache, allowing the inference process to resume seamlessly without costly full recomputation or state replication. Evaluations demonstrate that GhostServe reduces checkpointing latency by up to 2.7x and recovery latency by 2.1x for a single batch, and 1.2x median response latency compared to existing methods, in the presence of system failures, paving the way for high-availability and cost-effective LLM serving at scale.

</details>


### [458] [Separating Intelligence from Execution: A Workflow Engine for the Model Context Protocol](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00827)
*Abhinav Singh Parmar*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper introduces the MCP Workflow Engine, a system that optimizes tool execution for Large Language Models, reducing token consumption and improving efficiency in multi-step tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current architectures for Large Language Models require reasoning for every tool invocation, leading to high token costs and inefficiencies in repeated tasks.

Method: The authors propose an orchestration layer that separates planning (via declarative workflows) from execution, allowing tokens to be used only for the initial workflow creation and not for subsequent runs.

Result: The workflow engine reduces per-execution token costs by over 99%, executes complex tasks like Kubernetes synchronization in under 45 seconds, and ensures deterministic, idempotent outcomes.

Conclusion: The MCP Workflow Engine makes repeated executions of complex tasks efficient and scalable, significantly enhancing practical applications of LLM tool interactions.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents increasingly interact with external systems through tool-calling protocols such as the Model Context Protocol (MCP). In prevailing architectures, the agent must reason about every tool invocation in every session, consuming tokens proportional to the number of actions performed--even when the task has been solved before. We present the MCP Workflow Engine, a novel MCP-native orchestration layer that decouples intelligence (deciding what to do) from execution (carrying it out). An agent reasons once to produce a declarative workflow blueprint--a JSON document specifying a directed sequence of MCP tool calls with parameterized templates, loops, parallel branches, and data piping. Subsequent executions are triggered by a single run_workflow tool call, consuming one invocation's worth of tokens regardless of the blueprint's internal complexity. We formalize the MCP Mediator architectural pattern--an MCP server that simultaneously acts as a client to downstream MCP servers--and implement it in TypeScript against the MCP SDK. We evaluate the engine on a production-scale Kubernetes CMDB synchronization task spanning 67 orchestrated steps across 2 MCP servers, 38 namespaces, 13 worker nodes, and 22 distinct resource types. The engine reduces per-execution token cost by over 99%, completes the full cluster graph--comprising 1,200+ nodes and 2,800+ relationships across 20 relationship types--in under 45 seconds, and achieves deterministic, idempotent execution with zero agent involvement at run time.

</details>


### [459] [Canonical LST: A Protocol-Native Liquid Staking Solution for Tezos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00828)
*Mathias Bourgoin,Arthur Breitman,Pierrick Couderc,Zaynah Dargaye,Diane Gallois-Wong,Marina Polubelova,Lucas Randazzo,Julien Tesson*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: Canonical LST (sTEZ) is a protocol-native liquid staking mechanism for Tezos, balancing centralization risks and offering a neutral, accrual-based staking alternative.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address centralization risks in liquid staking mechanisms by introducing a protocol-native and neutral alternative, enabling decentralized and transparent staking participation.

Method: The method involves implementing sTEZ, an accrual-based mechanism reflecting staking rewards and penalties in its exchange rate, with governance anchored in the amendment process.

Result: sTEZ facilitates fungible and precise economic staking representation, operating as transparent network infrastructure rather than a commercial product.

Conclusion: Canonical LST is essential for decentralized systems, ensuring liquid staking serves as transparent infrastructure while mitigating centralization risks.

Abstract: Canonical LST (sTEZ) is an enshrined, protocol-native mechanism designed to mitigate the centralization risks associated with liquid staking intermediaries. Intended to complement direct staking rather than replace it, Canonical LST provides a neutral, public alternative managed directly by the Tezos protocol. It allows any tez holder to participate in aggregated staking without reliance on third-party operators. sTEZ follows an accrual-based design: all slashing events and rewards are reflected in the token's exchange rate to tez, keeping balances fungible while exposing holders to the precise economics of staking. This approach ensures that liquid staking functions as fundamental network infrastructure--with deterministic lifecycle rules, transparent on-chain data, and governance anchored in the amendment process--rather than as a discretionary commercial product. This white paper summarises the motivation for enshrining liquid staking, the core mechanics, exchange-rate model, regulatory touchpoints, risk posture, and forward-looking roadmap for Canonical LST.

</details>


### [460] [Efficient Accelerated Graph Edit Distance Computation on GPU](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00830)
*Adel Dabah,Andreas Herten*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: FAST-GED is an innovative framework accelerating Graph Edit Distance (GED) computations on GPUs, offering significant speedups and high accuracy compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address computational limitations in efficient GED computation for large-scale graphs in real-world applications.

Method: Developed FAST-GED, a GPU-optimized framework designed to efficiently compute GED while minimizing host-device communication and maximizing accuracy.

Result: FAST-GED achieves orders of magnitude faster performance than Python NetworkX, while delivering highly accurate and scalable solutions surpassing state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: FAST-GED effectively broadens the usability and efficiency of GED computations, driving adoption in diverse domains with large graphs.

Abstract: Graph representation is a powerful abstraction of real-world objects and relations. Computing the Graph Edit Distance (GED) between graphs is critical in domains such as bioinformatics, machine learning, and pattern recognition. GED measures the minimum number of edit operations required to transform one graph into another. However, the high computational complexity of optimal and near-optimal methods limits their applicability to large-scale graphs, making high-performance parallel GED computation essential. To address this, we propose FAST-GED, a fast and scalable open-source framework for GED computation on GPUs. FAST-GED overcomes existing limitations by combining high accuracy with fast execution through GPU-friendly algorithmic design and efficient mapping to GPU hardware, minimizing host-device communication. The implementation is optimized and tested across multiple GPU architectures. We validate FAST-GED on real and synthetic datasets with diverse graph sizes and densities. It achieves speedups of several orders of magnitude over the Python NetworkX library while reaching optimal solutions in most cases. Moreover, it outperforms state-of-the-art approximate methods in both accuracy and scalability. We show that FAST-GED enables broader adoption of GED-based solutions in real-world applications.

</details>


### [461] [SCION: Size-aware Policy Orchestration for Nonstationary Object Caches (Long Paper Version)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01055)
*Qizhi Wang*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: SCION introduces a lightweight framework for optimizing object cache policies using workload fingerprints and a trained selector, achieving robust performance across varied workloads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Object caching in cloud and edge services faces challenges from heterogeneous, nonstationary, and throughput-constrained workloads. Traditional non-ML policies often perform well, so any novel approach needs to outperform these baselines while handling workload variability and operational constraints.

Method: SCION orchestrates between deployable cache policies using a framework called AUTO. It computes compact workload fingerprints (e.g., metrics like object size and reuse) off the critical path, then selects the best policy using an offline-trained linear model. AUTO integrates multiple strong cache policies, adapting dynamically to the workload.

Result: AUTO improves cacheable-only object miss ratios on the majority of workloads tested, aligns closely with the best fixed single policy, supports tradeoffs between object miss ratio and byte miss ratio, and remains cost-efficient under fast-policy constraints.

Conclusion: SCION addresses workload variability in object caching by selecting appropriate policies dynamically. It ensures robust performance, avoids regime mismatches, and retains operational simplicity by minimizing changes to critical system paths.

Abstract: Object caches underpin cloud and edge services, but production workloads are heterogeneous, nonstationary, and throughput-constrained. Recent simple non-ML policies such as SIEVE and S3-FIFO set a strong baseline, so any learned method must be overhead-aware, robust under drift, and competitive with strong experts. We present SCION, a lightweight policy-orchestration framework that selects among a small set of deployable cache policies using a tiny workload fingerprint computed off the critical path. Our prototype, AUTO, uses short-prefix statistics of object size, cacheability, reuse, and cache size, then applies an offline-trained linear selector to choose among GDSF, S3-FIFO, SIEVE, LHD, W-TinyLFU-AV, and DynamicAdaptiveClimb; a simpler SCION-P90 variant uses only a p90 threshold. In a CPU-only, trace-driven evaluation on 30 public object-cache traces and a separate HR-Cache simulator subset, AUTO improves cacheable-only object miss ratio over SIEVE on a majority of workloads, stays close to the best single expert on average, enables explicit OMR/BMR tradeoff selection, and remains competitive on byte miss ratio. Under a fast-policy budget, AUTO-fast achieves lower cost than the best fixed fast policy. SCION reduces regime-mismatch risk while keeping the hot path unchanged.

</details>


### [462] [SURGE: SuperBatch Unified Resource-efficient GPU Encoding for Heterogeneous Partitioned Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01060)
*Shashank Kapadia,Deep Narayan Mishra,Sujal Reddy Alugubelli,Ajay Kumar,Swapnil Yadav,Rishi Bhatia*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper introduces SURGE, a GPU encoding system designed for efficient large-scale text embedding, achieving significant memory savings, throughput, and fault tolerance over traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address inefficiencies and limitations in large-scale embedding pipelines, like high memory usage and throughput constraints when processing many partitions independently.

Method: SURGE uses analytical contributions like cost models, memory-safety bounds, and a decision framework to optimize memory and improve processing speed, alongside engineering solutions like zero-copy serialization and asynchronous I/O.

Result: SURGE achieves high throughput (26,413 texts/s), drastically reduced memory consumption (2.6 GB), and faster time-to-first-output (68x faster) compared to conventional methods on a large-scale dataset.

Conclusion: SURGE resolves critical embedding pipeline inefficiencies, proving scalable and efficient for large workloads, with robust results validated on different encoder models and datasets.

Abstract: We present SURGE, a streaming GPU encoding system deployed in production to generate embeddings for over 800 million texts across 40,000 logical partitions. Production embedding pipelines face a tension between logical data partitioning and efficient GPU utilization: processing each partition independently incurs $P$ inter-process communication (IPC) calls whose overhead limits throughput for compute-light models. Our contributions are analytical: (i) a cost model (Theorem 1) predicting throughput within 2% across three encoders spanning a 15$\times$ parameter range; (ii) a memory-safety bound (Lemma 3) enabling a streaming two-threshold policy with peak memory $O(B_{\min} + n_{\max})$ rather than $O(N)$; and (iii) a $φ$/CV decision framework characterizing when the pattern applies beyond our workload. The naive fix of batching at fixed size requires $O(N)$ peak memory (32.7 GB at 10M texts; infeasible beyond ~60M on 192 GB nodes), produces no output until all encoding completes, and offers no fault tolerance. SURGE achieves the same throughput with $O(B_{\min} + n_{\max})$ bounded memory (2.6 GB), 68$\times$ faster time-to-first-output, and crash recovery at SuperBatch granularity. On 10M texts with 4 NVIDIA L4 GPUs, SURGE delivers 26,413 texts/s -- matching fixed-batch throughput while using 12.6$\times$ less memory. We validate on bge-base (109M, $d$=768, error 1.3%) and across log-normal $σ$ in {1.0, 1.72, 2.5} (speedup invariant within $\pm$3%), and compare against a partition-batched baseline (PB-PBP-LB), against which SURGE retains a 7% throughput edge and 2.5$\times$ faster TTFO. Complementary engineering -- zero-copy Arrow serialization (22-25$\times$ speedup) and async I/O pipelining (up to 93% benefit) -- realizes the design but is not the contribution.

</details>


### [463] [FPTC: A Fast Parallel Transform-based Codec for Efficient Asymmetric Signal Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01086)
*Ben Mechels,Ryan Billmeyer,Alexander Chen,Shiyang Li,Caiwen Ding*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper introduces FPTC, a high-throughput, GPU-optimized signal codec for efficient compression and decompression of large-scale signal data, achieving better compression ratios across various datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of efficiently compressing large volumes of signal data on resource-constrained devices while optimizing for both reconstruction quality and high decompression throughput on centralized servers.

Method: FPTC employs a lightweight encoder and a GPU-based massively parallel decoder. It utilizes windowed discrete cosine transform (DCT) for frequency-domain sparsity, hybrid three-zone quantization, and Huffman entropy coding with a novel packing scheme.

Result: FPTC achieves improved compression ratios compared to existing frameworks across ten datasets from diverse signal domains while maintaining competitive decompression throughput.

Conclusion: FPTC effectively balances compression efficiency and scalability, making it well-suited for modern IoT and high-performance computing deployments that require high-throughput signal processing.

Abstract: Modern high-performance computing and Internet-of-Things deployments increasingly generate large volumes of signal data that must be compressed efficiently on resource-constrained acquisition devices and decompressed at scale on centralized servers. Lossy compression is widely adopted to minimize storage and transmission costs on low-power hardware sensors, yet existing methods rarely optimize for both reconstruction quality and decompression throughput simultaneously, nor do they apply methods that generalize across signal domains. In this work, we introduce FPTC, a high-throughput asymmetric signal codec that pairs a lightweight sequential encoder with a massively parallel GPU decoder designed for server-side batch decompression. FPTC applies a windowed discrete cosine transform (DCT) to exploit frequency-domain sparsity, quantizes spectral coefficients with a hybrid three-zone mapping, and entropy codes the result using Huffman coding with a novel packing scheme. The pipeline used in FPTC is designed to be throughput oriented on the GPU, maximizing performance without sacrificing reconstruction quality. We evaluate FPTC on ten datasets spanning four signal domains: biomedical diagnostic, seismic reflections, power-grid production metrics, and meteorological recordings. Our results demonstrate that FPTC outperforms existing frameworks in compression ratio while maintaining competitive throughput, achieving multiplicative compression performance of 3.6x (power), 3.1x (meteorological), 1.5x (biomedical), and 1.2x (seismic) over existing frameworks.

</details>


### [464] [ncsim: A Lightweight Simulator for Networked Edge Computing with Wireless Interference Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01094)
*Bhaskar Krishnamachari,Maya Gutierrez,Jared Coleman*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: This paper presents ncsim, a simulator that combines DAG task scheduling with wireless interference modeling to address issues caused by interference-free evaluation, demonstrating significant inaccuracies in scheduler ranking under realistic wireless conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the inadequacy of existing tools that evaluate DAG task schedulers without accounting for the interplay between compute placement and wireless interference, leading to incorrect scheduler rankings.

Method: The authors developed ncsim, a lightweight discrete-event simulator that integrates DAG scheduling and IEEE 802.11 CSMA/CA-based wireless interference modeling into one tool.

Result: The study found that interference-free evaluations led to rank inversions in 27.8% of cases, with some schedulers performing up to 2.7x worse than simpler baselines. Scaling up the simulation increased rank inversions to 50%.

Conclusion: Interference-free evaluations are flawed and can lead to incorrect algorithm choices; ncsim provides a more realistic and accurate tool for evaluating DAG task schedulers under wireless constraints.

Abstract: Evaluating DAG task schedulers for wireless edge computing requires jointly modeling compute placement and wireless interference, yet existing tools treat them in isolation. This gap leads to rank inversions: the scheduler that appears optimal under an interference-free model can be the worst choice under realistic wireless conditions. We present ncsim, a lightweight discrete-event simulator that bridges this gap by combining DAG workflow scheduling with physically-grounded IEEE 802.11 CSMA/CA interference modeling in a single Python package. A 108-run factorial experiment reveals rank inversions in 27.8% of scenarios, with the interference-free-optimal scheduler producing up to 2.7x worse makespan than a simple round-robin baseline; scaling to a 100-node random geometric graph raises the inversion rate to 50%. These rank inversions show that interference-free evaluation can select the wrong algorithm entirely, justifying the design and use of ncsim.

</details>


### [465] [Position: LLM Serving Needs Mathematical Optimization and Algorithmic Foundations, Not Just Heuristics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01280)
*Zijie Zhou*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper advocates transitioning from heuristic approaches to mathematically optimized, algorithm-based methods for improving LLM inference serving.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors observe that current LLM inference serving systems rely on generic and outdated methods such as FIFO scheduling, round-robin routing, and LRU eviction, which fail to account for unique LLM demands.

Method: The paper encourages the use of mathematical modeling to capture distinctive LLM characteristics (e.g., dynamic KV memory, prefill-decode asymmetry) and to design algorithms with provable performance guarantees, distinguishing itself from purely heuristic methods.

Result: It highlights recent advancements in bridging operations research with ML systems, showing these approaches can provide not only superior performance but also theoretical backing.

Conclusion: A shift towards foundational algorithmic research in LLM inference serving is crucial to overcome limitations and enhance reliability across varied scenarios.

Abstract: This position paper argues that LLM inference serving has outgrown generic heuristics and now demands mathematical optimization and algorithmic foundations. Despite rapid advances in serving systems such as vLLM and SGLang, their algorithmic cores remain largely unchanged from classical distributed computing: request routing uses join-shortest-queue or round-robin, scheduling defaults to FIFO, and KV cache eviction follows LRU. These general-purpose policies ignore the distinctive structure of LLM inference--dynamically growing KV cache memory, prefill-decode phase asymmetry, unknown output lengths, and continuous batching constraints. We contend that the field must develop mathematical models capturing these characteristics, enabling the design of algorithms with provable performance guarantees across diverse workloads, rather than heuristics that may succeed in some scenarios but fail unpredictably in others. Emerging work at the intersection of operations research and ML systems demonstrates that principled methods can match or exceed heuristic performance while providing theoretical guarantees. We call on the community to recognize algorithmic design for LLM serving as a research frontier.

</details>


### [466] [CvxCluster: Solving Large, Complex, Granular Resource Allocation Problems 100-1000x Faster](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01614)
*Obi Nnorom,Stephen Boyd,Philip Levis*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: CvxCluster reimagines cluster resource allocation as a convex optimization problem, achieving blazing speedups (100-2,500x) while staying close to optimal results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the computational inefficiency of current methods such as mixed integer programming or heuristics in cluster resource allocation.

Method: Introduced a two-stage algorithm: first, solving a convex relaxation to determine resource prices; second, employing a greedy method for task placements.

Result: CvxCluster handles up to 100,480 servers and 500,000x workload rates, is 100-2,500x faster than a state-of-the-art MIP solver, and maintains less than 3% deviation from optimal outcomes.

Conclusion: Reformulating resource placement as a continuous problem dramatically improves efficiency without sacrificing result quality.

Abstract: Cluster resource allocation is a multidimensional search problem that finds the best allocation of tasks to servers. Because the search space grows exponentially, modern approaches frame it as a mixed integer program (MIP) or a complex set of search heuristics. This paper proposes using a different approach: convex optimization, which has extremely fast solution methods. The research challenge is devising how to transform cluster resource allocation into a convex problem that generates good placements. We describe CvxCluster, which allocates cluster resources with a two-stage algorithm. The first stage solves a convex relaxation of the placement problem to yield a principled set of per-machine resource prices. The second stage uses these prices to drive a lightweight greedy procedure to place tasks. Experimental results with Azure traces find that CvxCluster scales to 100,480 servers under proportional workload growth and sustains arrival rates up to 500,000x the baseline trace. CvxCluster runs 100 to 2,500x faster than a state-of-the-art MIP solver while remaining within 3% of the optimal objective. CvxCluster can support complex constraints such as job anti-affinity, machine types, and GPU servers. The key insight behind CvxCluster is that reformulating placement as a continuous rather than discrete problem enables much faster methods that find solutions just as good or better than prior heuristics.

</details>


### [467] [SplitZip: Ultra Fast Lossless KV Compression for Disaggregated LLM Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01708)
*Yipin Guo,Siddharth Joshi*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: SplitZip is a GPU-friendly lossless compressor designed to tackle bottlenecks in KV-cache transfer during large language model serving, achieving remarkable speed improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address bottlenecks in KV-cache transfer during LLM serving, especially for workloads with long inputs, which are vital for scalability.

Method: SplitZip uses redundancy in floating-point exponents with a calibrated top-16 exponent codebook for lossless compression and efficient encoding-decoding pathways on GPUs.

Result: SplitZip achieves high compression and decompression throughput, showing up to 1.32× speed improvements in key transfer metrics.

Conclusion: SplitZip demonstrates significant performance enhancements in KV-cache transfers for LLMs, addressing critical scalability issues with efficient GPU-based solutions.

Abstract: Contemporary systems serving large language models (LLMs) have adopted prefill-decode disaggregation to better load-balance between the compute-bound prefill phase and the memory-bound decode phase. Under this design, prefill workers generate a KV cache that must be transferred to decode workers before token generation can begin. With these workers residing on different physical systems, this transfer becomes a significant bottleneck to serving LLMs at scale. This bottleneck gets exacerbated for long-input and agentic workloads, which typically require long inputs. Existing lossless codecs are not well suited to this setting as they primarily target offline weight compression, rely on CPU-side, or use variable-length coding that decompresses fast but compresses too slowly for online use. SplitZip is a GPU-friendly lossless compressor for KV-cache transfer. It exploits redundancy in floating-point exponents of KV activations, encoding the most frequent exponent values with fixed-length codes, and encoding (position, value) pairs and value of rare exponents in an escape stream. An offline calibrated top-16 exponent codebook enables online encoding, while the regular dense path and sparse escape correction make both encoding and decoding efficient on GPUs. On real BF16 activation tensors, SplitZip achieves 613.3 GB/s compression throughput and 2181.8 GB/s decompression throughput, substantially outperforming prior lossless compressors on the latency-critical codec path. End-to-end transfer experiments show up to 1.32$\times$ speedup for BF16 KV-cache transfer, 1.30$\times$ speedup for TTFT and 1.23$\times$ increase on Request Throughput.

</details>


### [468] [Joint Temporal-Structural Representation Learning for Distributed Fault Discrimination in Microservice Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01776)
*Yihan Xue,Yuxiao Wang,Ao Zhu,Xiaoxuan Sun,Chong Zhang*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: This paper introduces a distributed fault discrimination model using temporal graph neural networks for microservice systems, achieving state-of-the-art performance through a novel approach combining temporal and structural modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges like diverse fault structures, complex dependencies, and fluctuating operational states inherent in microservice distributed systems.

Method: Utilizes temporal graph neural networks to construct dynamic graph sequences, incorporating service-level observations, structured temporal coding, attention-based message passing, and dual readout mechanisms for enhanced fault discrimination.

Result: The proposed model exhibited superior performance on multiple metrics, demonstrating its ability to handle complex dependencies and noise conditions effectively.

Conclusion: Joint modeling of temporal evolution and structural dependencies significantly improves fault discrimination capabilities in distributed microservice systems, advancing the state-of-the-art in this area.

Abstract: Addressing the diverse fault morphologies, complex dependencies, and time-varying operational states in microservice distributed systems, this paper proposes a distributed fault discrimination model based on temporal graph neural networks. This model characterizes the microservice operation process as a dynamic graph sequence evolving, and performs joint representation learning of temporal modeling and structural interactions within a unified framework. First, service-level multi-source observation signals are aligned and characterized to construct node feature sequences and their corresponding time-dependent dependencies. Then, a temporal coding module is introduced to extract the dynamic evolution representation of service states, and at each time step, attention-based structured message passing is used to characterize dependency interactions and propagation associations, forming a structure-enhanced temporal node representation. Furthermore, a dual readout mechanism is employed to aggregate the node and temporal dimensions, obtaining a system-level global representation and outputting the fault category distribution. Finally, supervised learning objectives are used to optimize model parameters, enabling the model to learn stable discrimination evidence under complex interactions and multi-source noise conditions. Comparative experimental results show that the proposed method achieves superior performance on multiple evaluation metrics, validating the effectiveness of jointly modeling temporal evolution and dependency structures in improving the distributed fault discrimination capability of microservices.

</details>


### [469] [nvPAX: Constrained Optimization for Dynamic Power Allocation in Hierarchical and Multi-Tenant Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01837)
*Hadar Sivan,Gil Shabat,Yoel Shkolnisky*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: nvPAX is a power-allocation policy for datacenters that uses a three-phase optimization to improve power distribution efficiently and fairly, achieving high satisfaction ratios and robust performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of dynamically allocating power in hierarchical datacenter power systems while respecting tenant-level contractual constraints and maximizing utilization.

Method: A hybrid QP/LP three-phase optimization approach is used: Phase I minimizes deviation from device power requests based on job priorities, Phase II fairly distributes excess power to active devices, and Phase III allocates remaining power to idle devices.

Result: nvPAX achieves a mean satisfaction ratio of 98.92% with a runtime of 264.69 ms per allocation interval, outperforming baseline methods in simulations on production datacenter GPU power telemetry.

Conclusion: nvPAX enables effective power oversubscription and balanced utilization in datacenters, ensuring both operational efficiency and fairness under hierarchical bottlenecks.

Abstract: Power oversubscription is increasingly central to datacenter operation as power density grows, making it necessary to dynamically allocate limited power budgets across devices based on real-time demand. Existing approaches typically assume flat power domains, whereas in practice power distribution is hierarchical and allocation decisions must additionally respect tenant-level contractual constraints. We present nvPAX, a constrained-optimization policy that computes feasible power allocations at every control step via a three-phase hybrid QP/LP procedure. Phase I allocates power with minimum deviation from each device's power request, while respecting job priorities. Phase II fairly distributes excess power among active devices. Phase III fairly distributes any remaining power to idle devices. The rationale behind the three phases is to allow power oversubscription while maximizing datacenter utilization. On a trace-driven large-scale simulation using GPU power telemetry from a production datacenter, nvPAX runs with a mean wall-clock time of 264.69 ms per allocation interval and achieves a mean satisfaction ratio of 98.92%, outperforming static equal-share allocation and providing robustness beyond greedy proportional allocation in the presence of non-uniform hierarchical bottlenecks.

</details>


### [470] [Decentralized Stratified Sampling for Low-Latency Approximate Geospatial Data Stream Processing in Edge-Cloud Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01922)
*Isam Mashhour Al Jawarneh,Lorenzo Felletti,Luca Foschini,Paolo Bellavista*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: EdgeApproxGeo introduces a novel edge-cloud architecture performing decentralized, geospatial online sampling at network edges, which reduces cloud dependency and optimizes data analytics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The challenge is that conventional cloud-based analytics face network bandwidth limitations and latency due to the exponential growth of geospatial data streams from IoT devices and centralized sampling.

Method: The paper proposes EdgeApproxGeo, which implements decentralized spatial sampling (EdgeSOS), geohash-based stratified sampling, spatial-aware Kafka routing, and optimizations for edge-cloud data processing.

Result: Evaluation on real-world mobility and air quality datasets shows faster processing times (with reduced cloud reliance) and controlled error rates (MAPE < 10%). Edge-specific tunability such as coarser geohashes improves accuracy-efficiency trade-offs by 30%.
