Method: The authors outline a framework based on three design axes: architectural parameters, optimization decomposition, and communication realization. They analyze these through scenarios in wireless edge intelligence.

Result: The study reveals that convergence in HFL is architecture-dependent, shaped by the hierarchical structure, optimization roles, and communication methods used.

Conclusion: HFL is not just about network efficiency but is essential for multi-tier networked AI design, enabling tailored methodologies for varied communication and computational scenarios.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) is fundamentally a distributed optimization problem executed by communicating agents with local data, local computation, and partial system visibility. Once FL is viewed through that lens, hierarchy is not merely a scalability mechanism. It becomes the natural place to rethink how distributed optimization should be organized over real multi-tier networks. This article argues that hierarchical federated learning (HFL) should move beyond its common framing as a communication-saving protocol and instead be viewed as an architecture-aware design framework for networked AI. The framework is organized around three coupled design axes: architectural parameters, layer-wise optimization decomposition, and layer-wise communication realization. The first axis determines the coordination geometry of learning through hierarchy depth, layer asymmetry, and layered connectivity. The second determines how the global FL objective is decomposed across layers and highlights modular multi-layer optimization as a major opportunity beyond one dominant method everywhere. The third determines how the distributed optimization is physically realized under heterogeneous communication regimes, from interference-limited lower tiers to reliable upper tiers. A central message is that, in HFL, convergence becomes architecture-dependent: it is directly shaped by the chosen hierarchy, the assigned optimization roles, and the communication mechanisms that connect them. We develop this viewpoint using large-scale wireless edge intelligence as a flagship networked AI setting, then provide a comparative perspective on flat FL, two-tier HFL, and deep HFL together with a regime-oriented design map. The resulting perspective positions HFL as a practical methodology for designing future networked AI systems.

</details>


### [500] [CGM-JEPA: Learning Consistent Continuous Glucose Monitor Representations via Predictive Self-Supervised Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00933)
*Hada Melino Muhammad,Zechen Li,Flora Salim,Ahmed A. Metwally*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces CGM-JEPA, a self-supervised learning framework that generates transferable representations for metabolic analysis across different modalities. It achieves state-of-the-art performance in cohort generalization, venous-to-CGM transfer, and home CGM settings, addressing issues with single-view representations and inconsistent baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to overcome challenges in deploying Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM) at scale, particularly addressing the issues of modality shifts in data representation and inconsistent performance of analysis models across different settings.

Method: CGM-JEPA is a self-supervised pretraining framework that predicts masked latent representations, enabling abstraction from specific data modalities. The enhanced version, X-CGM-JEPA, integrates cross-view objectives using Glucodensity distributions. The approach incorporates a pretraining dataset of ~389k CGM readings and evaluation across clinical cohorts using multiple validation regimes.

Result: CGM-JEPA demonstrates superior or competitive AUROC results across all tasks and settings, exceeding baseline performance by up to +6.5 percentage points. It reduces subgroup disparities under modality shifts and significantly improves clustering metrics with sparse venous data.

Conclusion: The proposed CGM-JEPA framework successfully addresses critical challenges in CGM deployment, demonstrating robust transferability across data modalities and reducing subgroup disparities. These advancements make it a reliable and equitable tool for metabolic analysis at a population scale.

Abstract: Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM) can detect early metabolic subphenotypes (insulin resistance, IR; $β$-cell dysfunction), but population-scale deployment faces two coupled problems. First, the same physiological state appears through multiple views (CGM time series, venous OGTT, Glucodensity summaries), so single-view representations fail to transfer when deployment shifts the modality or setting. Second, baselines perform inconsistently across these shifts. Both problems point to one remedy: representations that abstract away from any single view to capture higher-level temporal and distributional structure. We propose CGM-JEPA, a self-supervised pretraining framework which predicts masked latent representations rather than raw values, yielding abstraction that transfers across modalities. X-CGM-JEPA adds a masked Glucodensity cross-view objective for complementary distributional information. We pretrain on $\sim$389k unlabeled CGM readings from 228 subjects and evaluate on two clinical cohorts ($N=27$ and $N=17$ public-release subsets) across three regimes (cohort generalization, venous-to-CGM transfer, home CGM) under 20-iteration $\times$ 2-fold cross-validation. X-CGM-JEPA ranks first or second on AUROC for both endpoints across all three regimes while no baseline does, exceeding the strongest baseline by up to $+6.5$ pp in cohort generalization and $+3.6$ pp in venous-to-CGM transfer (paired Wilcoxon, $p<0.001$). Under modality shift, it matches mean AUROC while redistributing toward weaker subgroups (ethnicity AUROC gap shrinks 25-54%); on sparse in-domain venous data, the distributional view lifts label-aware clustering (ARI $+39\%$, NMI $+40\%$). Code and weights: https://github.com/cruiseresearchgroup/CGM-JEPA

</details>


### [501] [Structured Analytic Coherent Point Drift for Non-Rigid Point Set Registration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00934)
*Wei Feng,Haiyong Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Analytic-CPD enhances coherent point drift (CPD) for non-rigid point set registration by introducing an analytic and structured deformation estimator, leading to faster convergence and lower errors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the limitations of standard coherent point drift (CPD) in non-rigid registration, particularly the computational inefficiency and lack of structure in traditional kernel-based systems.

Method: The approach replaces the standard Gaussian-kernel displacement-field estimator in CPD with a finite-dimensional analytic mapping estimator, employing a truncated multivariate Taylor mapping and a degree-continuation strategy for stability.

Result: Analytic-CPD achieves reduced registration errors and faster convergence compared to standard CPD, especially in cases involving large deformations, as validated by experiments on both 2D and 3D data.

Conclusion: Analytic-CPD is a promising alternative to kernel-based non-rigid registration, combining probabilistic correspondences with structured analytic mappings for improved performance and interpretability.

Abstract: We introduce Analytic-CPD, a structured analytic variant of coherent point drift for non-rigid point set registration. The method retains the CPD posterior correspondence layer, but replaces the point-indexed Gaussian-kernel displacement-field M-step with a finite-dimensional structured analytic mapping estimator. Posterior probabilities from the Gaussian mixture model are condensed through a barycentric identity into weighted soft target points, converting the CPD pairwise soft-correspondence objective into a weighted analytic fitting problem. The deformation is represented by a truncated multivariate Taylor mapping of a vector-valued function, so the number of deformation parameters is controlled by the ambient dimension and the analytic order rather than by an M-by-M kernel system over the moving points. A degree-continuation strategy is further introduced to stabilize large-deformation registration by progressively activating higher-order analytic modes. Experiments on two-dimensional analytic deformations and three-dimensional smooth non-analytic deformations show that Analytic-CPD achieves lower final errors and faster convergence than standard CPD in representative large-deformation settings. The results suggest that CPD-style probabilistic correspondences and structured analytic mappings provide a compact and interpretable alternative to kernel-based non-rigid registration. Code is available at https://github.com/monge-ampere/Analytic-CPD.

</details>


### [502] [Watch Your Step: Information Injection in Diffusion Models via Shadow Timestep Embedding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00935)
*An Huang,Junggab Son,Zuobin Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper explores the potential risks and underutilized capabilities of timestep embeddings in diffusion models, specifically for encoding malicious or side-channel information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the overlooked role of timestep embeddings in diffusion models, especially their potential to encode side-channel information for security risks and reliable provenance.

Method: The study introduces Shadow Timestep Embedding (STE), analyzes timestep embeddings as position-encoding mappings, and derives a mutual coherence evaluation to examine their separability.

Result: Finds that timesteps have distinct representational capabilities and can encode side-channel information, which can be exploited both for attacks and defenses via scheduler interfaces.

Conclusion: Timestep embeddings in diffusion models represent a powerful side channel for malicious information injection, opening new avenues for adversarial generative modeling by leveraging the temporal dimension.

Abstract: Diffusion models have become the foundation of modern generative systems, with most research focusing primarily on improving generation efficiency and output quality. The timestep embedding component is a crucial part of the diffusion pipeline, which provides a temporal conditioning signal to the denoising network, enabling it to adapt its predictions across different noise levels throughout the process. Despite their potential to contain substantial information, timestep embeddings remain underexplored in current research, especially for security risks and reliable provenance. To fill this gap, we introduce Shadow Timestep Embedding (STE), a novel mechanism that investigates the underutilized temporal space for malicious information injection into diffusion models. In particular, when zooming in on the timestep embedding space, we find that different timesteps exhibit distinct representational capabilities that can encode side-channel information. Moreover, such encoded information can be utilized for attack and defense purposes through the scheduler interface. We present a theoretical analysis of timestep embeddings as position-encoding mappings and derive a mutual coherence evaluation that explains the separability of disjoint timestep intervals. Our findings reveal the diffusion model's timestep as a powerful side channel for carrying dedicated information, motivating new directions for adversarial generative modeling by understanding the temporal dimension.

</details>


### [503] [EventADL: Open-Box Anomaly Detection and Localization Framework for Events in Cloud-Based Service Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00936)
*Luan Pham,Victor Nicolet,Joey Dodds,Hui Guan,Daniel Kroening*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EventADL is the first open-box framework designed for detecting and localizing anomalies in cloud systems using event data. It achieves high accuracy and interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address a gap in anomaly detection and localization (ADL) for cloud systems by focusing on event data, which remains unexplored compared to metric and log data.

Method: EventADL works in three phases: offline training (learning event semantic and frequency patterns), online anomaly detection (identifying deviations from learned patterns), and root cause localization (using an Intervention Graph to model relationships and pinpoint root causes).

Result: EventADL outperforms existing ADL methods with at least 90% F1-scores for anomaly detection and 100% top-3 accuracy in root cause localization, as shown in evaluations on real cloud systems.

Conclusion: EventADL demonstrates its effectiveness in utilizing event data for both anomaly detection and root cause analysis. It provides interpretable and efficient results, filling a key gap in cloud system reliability.

Abstract: Anomaly detection and localization (ADL) is critical for maintaining reliability and availability in cloud systems. Recent ADL developments focus on metric and log data, leaving event data unexplored. To address this gap, we propose EventADL, the first open-box event-based ADL framework for cloud-based service systems. To motivate the design of our framework, we conduct a systematic analysis on 520 real-world incidents, and provide insights into how anomalies and their root causes manifest through event data. EventADL has three phases: offline training, online anomaly detection, and root cause localization. During the training phase, EventADL first learns Event Semantic Patterns (ESPs), which capture normal interactions between system entities using historical event data, and then learns Event Frequency Patterns (EFPs), which capture the normal frequency of known ESPs. In the online anomaly detection phase, any data in the event stream that deviates significantly from either pattern is identified as anomalous. For localization, EventADL constructs an Intervention Graph that models the relationships between recent system interactions and the detected anomalies for automatic root cause localization. The framework is designed to operate efficiently with unlabeled data and to produce interpretable anomalies with their corresponding root causes. Our evaluation on three real cloud service systems and two real-world incidents demonstrates that EventADL outperforms existing methods, achieving F1-scores of at least 90% for anomaly detection and 100% top-3 accuracy in root cause localization.

</details>


### [504] [Fusing Urban Structure and Semantics: A Conditional Diffusion Model for Cross-City OD Matrix Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00938)
*Bin Chen,Zhuoya Meng,Fang Yang,Runkang Guo,Jingtao Ding,Yin Zhang,Chuan Ai,Zhengqiu Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces SEDAN, a graph-based model for generating commuting flow matrices that integrates semantic regional attributes and geographic structures. It outperforms existing methods in accuracy and robustness across diverse urban scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate modeling of commuting flows is essential for urban planning and resource allocation, but existing methods struggle due to the heterogeneity of commuting patterns across cities.

Method: The proposed SEDAN model represents cities as attributed graphs, combining demographic and point-of-interest data at nodes and commuting flows as weighted edges. It integrates semantic (regional demand) and spatial (proximity and structure) information using graph transformers and diffusion-based constraints.

Result: SEDAN achieves a 7.38% improvement in RMSE compared to the state-of-the-art model WEDAN and performs robustly across different urban setups.

Conclusion: SEDAN effectively integrates spatial and semantic data to generate commuting flow matrices that are both accurate and generalizable, offering a significant advancement in urban commuting modeling.

Abstract: Accurate modeling of commuting flows is important for urban governance, traffic planning, and resource allocation. However, the combined influence of individual intentions, geographic constraints, and social dynamics leads to considerable heterogeneity in commuting patterns, making it difficult to develop generation models that generalize across cities. To address this issue, we propose SEDAN, a Structure-Enhanced Diffusion model conditioned on Attributed Nodes for generalizable OD matrix generation. SEDAN models a city as an attributed graph. Each region is treated as a node with demographic and point-of-interest features, and commuting flows are modeled as weighted edges. Adjacency and distance matrices are incorporated to characterize spatial structure. Based on this representation, we design a fusion mechanism within SEDAN to jointly model semantic information and spatial information. Regional semantic attributes are used to model latent travel demand through graph-transformer-based node interactions, while spatial structure is injected into the generation process as explicit constraints. The adjacency matrix guides attention weights to strengthen interactions between neighboring regions. Meanwhile, the distance matrix serves as a diffusion condition to capture spatial proximity and travel impedance. The fusion of urban semantics and spatial constraints enables SEDAN to generate OD matrices that are both behaviorally plausible and geographically coherent. Experiments on real-world OD datasets from U.S. cities show that SEDAN achieves a 7.38\% improvement in RMSE over the state-of-the-art baseline, WEDAN. It also remains robust across heterogeneous urban scenarios and varying structural patterns. Our work provides an effective and generalizable solution for commuting OD matrix generation. The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SEDAN.

</details>


### [505] [From Flat Facts to Sharp Hallucinations: Detecting Stubborn Errors via Gradient Sensitivity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00939)
*Yee Zhing Liew,Andrew Huey Ping Tan,Anwar P. P Abdul Majeed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses stubborn hallucinations in LLMs using a novel geometric approach called EPGS, outperforming current methods in detecting such errors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current hallucination detection methods fail to identify high-confidence yet incorrect outputs (stubborn hallucinations) often produced by LLMs.

Method: Proposed the Embedding-Perturbed Gradient Sensitivity (EPGS) method, which measures robust vs brittle knowledge by analyzing gradient sensitivity to input embedding perturbations.

Result: EPGS showed superior performance over entropy-based and representation-based methods in detecting stubborn hallucinations during experiments.

Conclusion: EPGS provides an efficient and reliable method for distinguishing stable knowledge from unstable memorization in LLM outputs, addressing a critical limitation in hallucination detection.

Abstract: Traditional hallucination detection fails on "Stubborn Hallucinations" -- errors where LLMs are confidently wrong. We propose a geometric solution: Embedding-Perturbed Gradient Sensitivity (EPGS). We hypothesize that while robust facts reside in flat minima, stubborn hallucinations sit in sharp minima, supported by brittle memorization. EPGS detects this sharpness by perturbing input embeddings with Gaussian noise and measuring the resulting spike in gradient magnitude. This acts as an efficient proxy for the Hessian spectrum, differentiating stable knowledge from unstable memorization. Our experiments show that EPGS significantly outperforms entropy-based and representation-based baselines, providing a robust signal for detecting high-confidence factual errors.

</details>


### [506] [Interpretable experiential learning based on state history and global feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00940)
*Anton Kolonin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The study introduces an interpretable experiential learning model designed for resource-constrained reinforcement learning, showing comparable performance to neural network-based approaches on Atari Breakout.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resource-constrained environments pose a challenge to conventional reinforcement learning methods, necessitating an interpretable and efficient solution.

Method: The paper presents a model learning behavioral transitions between states using history and feedback, evaluated with utilities and evidence counts.

Result: Evaluation on Atari Breakout benchmark shows performance comparable to neural network-based approaches despite simpler model design.

Conclusion: This interpretable model is a promising alternative for reinforcement learning in constrained scenarios, balancing efficiency and efficacy.

Abstract: A new interpretable experiential learning model based on state history and global feedback is presented. It is capable of learning a behavioral model represented by a transition graph between sets of states, with transitions attributed with utility and evidence count. This model is expected to be suitable for solving reinforcement learning problem in resource-constrained environments. The model was thoroughly evaluated on the OpenAI Gym Atari Breakout benchmark, demonstrating performance comparable to some known neural network-based solutions.

</details>


### [507] [Divergence is Uncertainty: A Closed-Form Posterior Covariance for Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00941)
*Jiarui Xing,Song Wang,Jian Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces the divergence-uncertainty identity, enabling exact uncertainty quantification for generative models using flow matching, without requiring retraining, ensembles, or costly computations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for uncertainty quantification in flow matching generative modeling are either computationally expensive or lack accuracy.

Method: The authors derive the divergence-uncertainty identity, which provides a closed-form solution for computing uncertainty using the divergence of a velocity field in pre-trained flow matching models.

Result: Experiments on MNIST show that the proposed method produces semantically meaningful uncertainty maps and tracks prediction error with significantly reduced computational cost compared to traditional methods.

Conclusion: The paper introduces a novel, computationally efficient way to quantify uncertainty in flow matching generative models, eliminating previous trade-offs in training or inference costs.

Abstract: Flow matching has become a leading framework for generative modeling, but quantifying the uncertainty of its samples remains an open problem. Existing approaches retrain the model with auxiliary variance heads, maintain costly ensembles, or propagate approximate covariance through many integration steps, trading off training cost, inference cost, or accuracy. We show that none of these trade-offs is necessary. We prove that, for any pre-trained flow matching velocity field, the trace of the posterior covariance over the clean data given the current state equals, in closed form, the divergence of the velocity field, up to a known time-dependent prefactor and an additive constant. We call this the \emph{divergence-uncertainty identity} for flow matching. The matrix-level form of the identity is similarly closed-form, depending solely on the velocity Jacobian. Because the identity is exact and post-hoc, it is computable on any pre-trained flow matching model, with no retraining and no architectural modification. For one-step generators such as MeanFlow, the same identity yields the exact end-to-end generation uncertainty in a single forward pass, eliminating the multi-step variance propagation required by all prior methods. Experiments on MNIST confirm that the resulting per-pixel uncertainty maps are semantically meaningful, concentrating on digit boundaries where inter-sample variation is highest, and that the scalar uncertainty score tracks actual prediction error, all at roughly 10,000$\times$ less total compute than ensembling or Monte Carlo dropout.

</details>


### [508] [Graph Rewiring in GNNs to Mitigate Over-Squashing and Over-Smoothing: A Survey](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00951)
*Hugo Attali,Nathalie Pernelle,Davide Buscaldi,Fragkiskos D. Malliaros*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper surveys graph rewiring techniques to tackle over-squashing and over-smoothing in Graph Neural Networks, enhancing information propagation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from addressing the limitations of GNNs, particularly over-squashing and over-smoothing, which degrade their performance on graph-structured data.

Method: The paper reviews various graph rewiring techniques, exploring their theoretical foundations, practical applications, and trade-offs.

Result: It provides a comprehensive review of state-of-the-art graph rewiring methods to enhance information flow in GNNs.

Conclusion: Graph rewiring is essential for addressing key limitations in GNNs, and the paper consolidates knowledge on effective strategies to improve their performance.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks are powerful models for learning from graph-structured data, yet their effectiveness is often limited by two critical challenges: over-squashing, where information from distant nodes is excessively compressed, and over-smoothing, where repeated propagation makes node representations indistinguishable. Both phenomena stem from the interaction between message passing and the input topology, ultimately degrading information flow and limiting the performance of GNNs. In this survey, we examine graph rewiring techniques, a class of methods designed to modify the graph topology to enhance information propagation in GNNs. We provide a comprehensive review of state-of-the-art rewiring approaches, delving into their theoretical underpinnings, practical implementations, and performance trade-offs.

</details>


### [509] [Physiology-Aware Masked Cross-Modal Reconstruction for Biosignal Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00973)
*Hao Zhou,Simon A. Lee,Cyrus Tanade,Keum San Chun,Juhyeon Lee,Migyeong Gwak,Megha Thukral,Justin Sung,Eugene Hwang,Mehrab Bin Morshed,Li Zhu,Viswam Nathan,Md Mahbubur Rahman,Subramaniam Venkatraman,Sharanya Arcot Desai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces xMAE, a biosignal pretraining framework that uses masked cross-modal reconstruction to improve physiological signal representations, outperforming existing methods across various tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the oversight in most self-supervised learning methods that ignore the sequential temporal relationships between multimodal biosignals, such as ECG and PPG.

Method: The proposed xMAE framework incorporates masked cross-modal reconstruction as a training constraint to embed meaningful temporal and physiological structure in learned representations of multimodal biosignals.

Result: xMAE pretrained models achieve improved performance on 15 of 19 downstream tasks, including tasks like cardiovascular outcome prediction and sleep staging, while demonstrating robustness across devices and contexts.

Conclusion: The framework effectively incorporates temporal structure into multimodal pretraining, better capturing physiological relationships between signals like ECG and PPG, and offers broader generalizability.

Abstract: Biosignals acquired from different locations on the body often provide temporally ordered views of the same underlying physiological process. However, most existing self supervised learning methods treat these signals as interchangeable views, overlooking the directional temporal dynamics that link them. A canonical example is the relationship between electrocardiography (ECG), which captures the electrical activation initiating each heartbeat, and photoplethysmography (PPG), which records the resulting peripheral pulse delayed by vascular dynamics. To capture this structured relationship, we introduce xMAE, a biosignal pretraining framework that leverages masked cross modal reconstruction across temporally ordered biosignals as a training time constraint to encourage physiologically meaningful timing structure in the learned representations. We show that pretraining with xMAE yields representations that outperform both unimodal and multimodal baselines on 15 of 19 downstream tasks, including cardiovascular outcome prediction, abnormal laboratory test detection, sleep staging, and demographic inference, while generalizing across devices, body locations, and acquisition settings. Further analysis suggests that the ECG PPG timing structure is reflected in the learned PPG representations. More broadly, xMAE demonstrates the effectiveness of incorporating temporal structure into multimodal pretraining when signals observe different stages of a shared underlying process. Code is available at https://github.com/hzhou3/xMAE.

</details>


### [510] [Diffusion Operator Geometry of Feedforward Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01107)
*Kanishka Reddy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper develops a smooth operator-theoretic framework for analyzing the geometry of feedforward neural network representations, offering an alternative to discrete curvature methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how the geometry of learned representations in neural networks affects key properties like separation, contraction, and generalization, and to provide a stable framework for analysis.

Method: A Gaussian-kernel diffusion Markov operator is used for analyzing feedforward representation snapshots, with derived observables based on Bakry-Emery $Γ$-calculus. Analytical results are validated on synthetic and MNIST datasets.

Result: The framework successfully tracks training dynamics, network width effects, and perturbation stability, offering a robust alternative to neighborhood-graph-based analyses.

Conclusion: The proposed operator-geometric framework provides a smooth, stable, and interpretable approach for analyzing neural network representation geometry, with validated effectiveness through theoretical and experimental results.

Abstract: Neural networks transform data through learned representations whose geometry affects separation, contraction, and generalization. Recent work studies this geometry using discrete curvature on neighborhood graphs, suggesting Ricci-flow-like behavior across layers. We develop a smooth operator-theoretic alternative for feedforward representation snapshots. Each feature cloud induces a Gaussian-kernel diffusion Markov operator, and transport, spectral, label-boundary, and local-scale observables are derived from this single object via Bakry-Emery $Γ$-calculus. In a balanced Gaussian class-conditional snapshot model with shared covariance, the population operator has closed-form class affinities, leakage, and coarse spectra, all controlled by pairwise regularized Mahalanobis separations $c_\varepsilon^{(a,b)}$. We also prove that the resulting operator observables vary smoothly under feature perturbations, while hard neighborhood-graph diagnostics can change discontinuously. Synthetic experiments validate the closed-form Gaussian bridge, while learned MNIST experiments show that the same operator observables track training, width, and perturbation stability. Together, these results give a stable operator-geometric framework for analyzing feedforward representation geometry.

</details>


### [511] [Continual Learning of Feedback-based Molecular Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01020)
*Siddhant Setia,Junichi Suzuki,Tadashi Nakano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a continual learning-based method for performance estimation in sequential molecular communication simulations. It improves estimation accuracy while maintaining learned tasks efficiently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of performance estimation in sequential experimental settings for molecular communication protocols without compromising previous knowledge.

Method: The method leverages continual learning algorithms, integrating regularization and replay strategies into neural networks to handle sequential simulation tasks incrementally.

Result: Experimental results show enhanced estimation accuracy of the proposed method compared to baseline neural networks at various computational costs.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates the effectiveness of continual learning models in molecular communication and highlights their potential to enhance performance estimations in this field.

Abstract: This paper proposes and evaluates a new performance estimation method that leverages continual learning (CL) algorithms to carry out sequential simulation experiments for a feedback-based molecular communication protocol. As the protocol is sequentially examined in various experimental settings, the proposed CL-based performance estimators incrementally learn a series of unexperienced estimation tasks without compromising those that have been learned in the past. They are designed to work on a standard neural network architecture by customizing regularization and replay strategies in the loss function. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed estimators can effectively learn on a continuous stream of simulation results and enhance the baseline neural network by improving estimation accuracy at a variety of computational costs. This paper's contribution is to establish the implications of CL in the field of molecular communication.

</details>


### [512] [Topological Neural Tangent Kernel](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01110)
*Sanjukta Krishnagopal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Topological Neural Tangent Kernel (TopoNTK), an infinite-width kernel for learning from higher-order interactions in simplicial complexes, extending beyond pairwise graph structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph neural tangent kernels are limited to pairwise interactions, but many relational systems involve higher-order interactions best represented by simplicial complexes.

Method: The authors develop TopoNTK, which uses lower and upper Hodge interactions to capture both graph-like and higher-order structures. It also incorporates a learning geometry based on edge signal decompositions and the spectrum of the TopoNTK.

Result: The kernel effectively distinguishes topologies beyond graphs, learning efficiently from multiple signal components and validating topological spectral bias on simulations and higher-order link prediction tasks.

Conclusion: TopoNTK demonstrates that topology adds significant value to relational learning by enhancing expressivity, interpretability, and task effectiveness through its integration of higher-order simplicial structures.

Abstract: Graph neural tangent kernels give a principled infinite-width theory for graph neural networks, but inherit a basic limitation of graph models: they see only pairwise structure. Many relational systems contain higher-order interactions that are more naturally represented by simplicial complexes. We introduce the Topological Neural Tangent Kernel (TopoNTK), an infinite-width kernel for simplicial message passing on edge features. TopoNTK combines lower Hodge interactions, capturing graph-like coupling through shared vertices, with upper Hodge interactions, capturing coupling through filled simplices. This makes the kernel sensitive to topology invisible to graph kernels, allowing complexes with the same graph but different filled simplices to induce different kernels. Beyond expressivity, the Hodge structure gives the kernel an interpretable learning geometry. Edge signals decompose into gradient-like, harmonic, and local circulation components, and the spectrum of the TopoNTK determines how quickly each component is learned. This yields a topological form of spectral bias: components aligned with large-eigenvalue modes are learned quickly, while global harmonic modes, retained through the residual channel, often lie at smaller eigenvalues and are learned more slowly. We prove expressivity, Hodge-alignment, spectral learning, and stability properties, and validate them on synthetic simplicial tasks and DBLP higher-order link prediction. The results show that topology is not merely extra structure; it can provide coordinates that make relational learning more faithful, interpretable, and effective.

</details>


### [513] [Finite-Sample Analysis of Elimination in Active Hypothesis Testing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01039)
*Ziyuan Lin,Hoang Ngoc Nguyen,Jie Xu,Ivan Ruchkin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces an extended Track-and-Stop algorithm for sequential hypothesis testing, using hypothesis elimination to improve efficiency, and validates it with theoretical bounds and experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in safety-critical applications by improving sequential hypothesis testing through hypothesis elimination, enabling faster and more efficient decision-making under finite-sample constraints.

Method: The authors propose an elimination-augmented Track-and-Stop algorithm that prunes hypothesis sets and reallocates sensing efforts, introducing an aggressiveness parameter and deriving a non-asymptotic bound on expected stopping time.

Result: The enhanced algorithm shows finite-sample improvements in stopping time due to hypothesis elimination, supported by tighter tracking/concentration constants and confirmed through experiments on Gaussian instances.

Conclusion: Hypothesis elimination enhances the efficiency of sequential hypothesis testing, offering a trade-off between stopping time and confidence guarantees, with practical and theoretical validation.

Abstract: A fixed-confidence, finite-sample problem of active hypothesis testing arises in many safety-critical applications. Situated in the context of sequential hypothesis testing, this paper studies the effect of hypothesis elimination on the stopping time. We introduce an elimination-augmented Track-and-Stop algorithm, in which champion-specific active-opponent sets are progressively pruned, and sensing effort is reallocated toward the surviving alternatives. Our analysis derives a non-asymptotic upper bound on the expected stopping time. The gain in finite-sample from elimination appears on the scale of the non-leading term, resulting from tighter tracking and concentration constants on the reduced hypothesis set. Furthermore, we introduce an aggressiveness parameter to modulate the trade-off between faster elimination and weaker confidence guarantee. An experimental study on synthetic Gaussian instances confirms the theoretical predictions.

</details>


### [514] [Spectral Graph Sparsification Preserves Representation Geometry in Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01136)
*Sanjukta Krishnagopal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper examines how spectral graph sparsification impacts learned embeddings' geometry in GNNs, indicating stability in representation structure even under sparsification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether spectral graph sparsification preserves the geometry of learned embeddings in graph neural networks (GNNs), vital for downstream tasks and interpretability.

Method: Analytically proves stability properties of polynomial graph filters and embedding perturbations under spectral sparsification; validates through experiments using effective-resistance sparsification on real datasets and synthetic graphs.

Result: Demonstrates stability in embedding geometry, training dynamics, and downstream metrics like neighborhood and class-centroid preservation under sparsification.

Conclusion: Spectral graph sparsification retains representation geometry, ensuring its utility in downstream GNN embeddings while offering computational efficiency.

Abstract: Spectral graph sparsification is a classical tool for reducing graph complexity while preserving Laplacian quadratic forms. In graph neural networks (GNNs), sparsification is often used to accelerate computation while maintaining predictive performance. In this work, we study a complementary representation-level question: does sparsification preserve the geometry of learned embeddings?
  For polynomial-filter GNNs, we prove that any $ε$-spectral sparsifier induces $O(ε)$ perturbations in polynomial graph filters, multilayer hidden representations, and their Gram matrices. These guarantees imply stability of squared pairwise distances, class means, and covariance structure in embedding space. We further establish finite-time training stability: under smoothness and boundedness assumptions, gradient descent on dense and sparsified graphs produces weight trajectories whose separation grows at most proportionally to the sparsification distortion.
  Empirically, effective-resistance sparsification validates the predicted perturbation chain on synthetic graphs and preserves hidden representation geometry on real datasets. In our experiments, the gram matrix and training dynamics show low divergence even under substantial sparsification, consistent with the predicted stability under spectral sparsification. Hidden Gram preservation strongly predicts neighborhood preservation and class-centroid stability across FashionMNIST, Cora, and Paul15. Together, these results show that spectral sparsification preserves not only graph operators, but also the representation geometry that supports downstream use of GNN embeddings for interpretability.

</details>


### [515] [Learning in the Fisher Subspace: A Guided Initialization for LoRA Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01046)
*Zhi-Quan Feng,Ying-Jia Lin,Hung-Yu Kao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a novel data-aware method for initializing LoRA, which significantly improves large language model performance by leveraging downstream data dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of current LoRA initialization methods, which rely solely on pre-trained weight properties and overlook interactions with downstream data.

Method: A Fisher-guided framework is introduced to choose LoRA directions by analyzing curvature information from downstream data, ensuring parameter updates align with task-specific objectives.

Result: The proposed approach consistently enhances the performance of large language models across various tasks and modalities over conventional initialization strategies.

Conclusion: Integrating data-aware sensitivity into LoRA initialization ensures better alignment of parameter adaptation with downstream data, leading to improved task performance.

Abstract: LoRA adapts large language models (LLMs) by restricting updates to low-rank subspaces of pre-trained weights. While this substantially reduces training cost, the effectiveness of adaptation critically depends on which subspace is chosen at initialization: a poor initialization that allocates capacity to task-irrelevant directions can severely hinder downstream performance. Existing initialization strategies primarily rely on the intrinsic properties of pre-trained weights, implicitly assuming that weight geometry alone reflects task relevance. However, such criteria overlook how the model interacts with the downstream data distribution. In this work, we formulate LoRA initialization as identifying the degree of impact of directions in parameter space under the target data distribution. We argue that data-aware sensitivity, rather than weight-only magnitude, should govern the choice of adaptation subspaces. Building on this perspective, we propose a Fisher-guided framework that leverages curvature information induced by downstream data to characterize how parameter perturbations influence model predictions. This perspective yields a principled, task-dependent criterion for selecting LoRA directions that better align adaptation with the target objective. Empirical results across diverse tasks and modalities demonstrate that data-aware initialization consistently and significantly improves downstream performance over existing approaches.

</details>


### [516] [A Theory of Generalization in Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01172)
*Elon Litman,Gabe Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a non-asymptotic generalization theory for deep learning based on the neural tangent kernel splitting output space into signal and noise channels, addressing phenomena like benign overfitting and more. It also introduces a practical population-risk objective enhancing several applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding generalization in deep learning, especially phenomena like benign overfitting and implicit bias, and creating a quantifiable method to improve learning outcomes.

Method: Analysis of generalization using the empirical neural tangent kernel's eigenvalues to distinguish signal from noise, and deriving a population-risk metric without validation data supported by mathematical proofs.

Result: Demonstrated theoretical insights into phenomena like grokking and double descent, and practical gains like accelerated grokking, suppressed memorization in PINNs, and improved noisy preference fine-tuning.

Conclusion: The work provides a unified explanation for various learning phenomena and offers direct improvements in practical tasks through a novel population-risk metric and SNR preconditioning with minimal additional computational cost.

Abstract: We present a non-asymptotic theory of generalization in deep learning where the empirical neural tangent kernel partitions the output space. In directions corresponding to signal, error dissipates rapidly; in the vast orthogonal dimensions corresponding to noise, the kernel's near-zero eigenvalues trap residual error in a test-invisible reservoir. Within the signal channel, minibatch SGD ensures that coherent population signal accumulates via fast linear drift, while idiosyncratic memorization is suppressed into a slow, diffusive random walk. We prove generalization survives even when the kernel evolves $\mathcal{O}(1)$ in operator norm, the full feature-learning regime. This theory naturally explains disparate phenomena in deep learning theory, such as benign overfitting, double descent, implicit bias, and grokking. Lastly, we derive an exact population-risk objective from a single training run with no validation data, for any architecture, loss, or optimizer, and prove that it measures precisely the noise in the signal channel. This objective reduces in practice to an SNR preconditioner on top of Adam, adding one state vector at no extra cost; it accelerates grokking by $5 \times$, suppresses memorization in PINNs and implicit neural representations, and improves DPO fine-tuning under noisy preferences while staying $3 \times$ closer to the reference policy.

</details>


### [517] [LEAP: Layer-wise Exit-Aware Pretraining for Efficient Transformer Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01058)
*Shashank Kapadia,Deep Naryan Mishra,Sujal Reddy Alugubelli,Haoan Wang,Saipraveen Vabbilisetty,Rishi Bhatia,Anupriya Sharma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Distillation and early-exit techniques in transformers are typically incompatible, but the proposed LEAP method resolves this issue, yielding significant computational efficiency improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the incompatibility between layer-aligned distillation and convergence-based early exit in transformer models, which limits computational efficiency.

Method: The paper introduces LEAP, a training objective that ensures intermediate transformer layers approximate final-layer representations, reconciling distillation and early-exit mechanisms.

Result: LEAP-MiniLM achieves up to 1.61× speedup in wall-clock time with 91.9% of samples exiting by layer 7, while preserving accuracy on benchmarks like STS-B (0.760±0.006) and BEIR.

Conclusion: LEAP effectively reconciles two previously incompatible efficiency paradigms in transformer models, enabling practical early-exit implementation without architectural changes.

Abstract: Layer-aligned distillation and convergence-based early exit represent two predominant computational efficiency paradigms for transformer inference; yet we establish that they exhibit systematic incompatibility under standard deployment conditions for convergence-based early exit. Distillation objectives that align intermediate student layers to teacher representations suppress the representational convergence that early-exit mechanisms exploit, rendering such mechanisms ineffective on distilled models. We introduce LEAP (Layer-wise Exit-Aware Pretraining), an auxiliary training objective that reconciles this incompatibility. LEAP requires no architectural modifications; it augments standard distillation with a single constraint ensuring intermediate layers approximate final-layer representations. LEAP-MiniLM achieves 1.61$\times$ measured wall-clock speedup (batch=1, NVIDIA L4) at $θ$=0.95, with 91.9% of samples exiting by layer 7 and 1.80$\times$ theoretical layer reduction, where standard distilled models achieve zero effective speedup. We validate across sentence similarity (STS-B: 0.760 $\pm$ 0.006) and retrieval benchmarks (BEIR), providing operational guidance including latency measurements, decision thresholds, and deployment criteria.

</details>


### [518] [A Theory of Saddle Escape in Deep Nonlinear Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01288)
*Divit Rawal,Michael R. DeWeese*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper explores training dynamics in deep nonlinear networks, focusing on feature-acquisition transitions and identifying activation function classes alongside deriving critical escape dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep networks often face training plateaus and sharp feature transitions, challenging understanding when extending from shallow and linear cases to deep nonlinear networks.

Method: The authors derive a Frobenius norm imbalance identity, classify activation functions into universality classes, and simplify dynamics into critical escape time laws governed by bottleneck layers.

Result: The proposed theoretical framework predicts training behavior, including depth-escape laws and universality classes, and shows strong agreement with numerical simulations.

Conclusion: Critical layer dynamics and imbalances influence training in deep nonlinear networks, and activation classes play a key role in scalability and efficiency insights.

Abstract: In deep networks with small initialization, training exhibits long plateaus separated by sharp feature-acquisition transitions. Whereas shallow nonlinear networks and deep linear networks are well studied, extending these analyses to deep nonlinear networks remains challenging. We derive an exact identity for the imbalance of Frobenius norms of layer weight matrices that holds for any smooth activation and any differentiable loss and use this to classify activation functions into four universality classes. On the permutation-symmetric submanifold, the identity combines with an approximate balance law to reduce the full matrix flow to a scalar ODE, giving a critical-depth escape time law $τ_\star = Θ(\varepsilon^{-(r-2)})$ governed by the number $r$ of layers at the bottleneck scale rather than the total depth $L$. We find that this same $r-2$ exponent is recovered under He-normal initialization with $r$ bottleneck layers rescaled by $\varepsilon$, where the symmetry manifold is preserved by the flow but not attracting. We find close agreement between our theory and numerical simulations.

</details>


### [519] [GEODE: Angle-Adaptive OOD Detection with Universal Scorer Compatibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01063)
*Bruno Abrahao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces GEODE, a synthetic calibration method for out-of-distribution (OOD) detection that preserves feature geometry through an angle-adaptive norm loss. It consistently outperforms existing methods across various scoring metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Outlier Exposure (OE) is effective for OOD detection but has limitations like scorer-dependency and reliance on curated datasets. This paper aims to address these shortcomings by understanding OE's mechanism and creating a synthetic alternative.

Method: GEODE employs an angle-adaptive norm loss that scales targets per sample with cosine similarity to class means, preserving feature geometry critical for distance-based scorers. The design is backed by four theorems rooted in neural collapse theory.

Result: GEODE achieves strong OOD detection performance across multiple scorers, outperforming OE on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 benchmarks, without catastrophic failures. It improves AUROC metrics significantly and works effectively without curated auxiliary data.

Conclusion: GEODE is a robust and versatile OOD detection solution that preserves feature geometry, making it superior to OE and other null-space methods. It is applicable across scorer metrics and OOD regimes, ensuring consistent detection performance.

Abstract: Outlier Exposure (OE) is among the strongest training-based OOD detectors on standard benchmarks but exhibits scorer-dependent tradeoffs (e.g., strong on MSP, weak on KNN) and requires curated auxiliary data. We show why OE works: its features sit at the same geometric locus as real near-OOD data, with the boundary-adjacent quartile driving nearly all of OE's gain. OE is boundary calibration, not OOD coverage. GEODE (GEOmetry-preserving DEtection) replicates this calibration synthetically through an angle-adaptive norm loss in which targets scale per-sample with cosine similarity to the nearest class mean, preserving feature geometry where boundary structure matters. Four theorems grounded in neural collapse justify the design. GEODE works across all seven standard scorers on CIFAR-10 (near-OOD AUROC 89.0-92.3, far-OOD reaching 93.05; no catastrophic failure on any scorer). Since the OOD regime is unknown at deployment, this is the test that matters. GEODE outperforms vanilla CE at matched epoch counts. Combined with OE, GEODE reaches 95.0 MSP / 94.8 KNN on CIFAR-10 and beats OE on every scorer on CIFAR-100. The gains hold on WRN-28-10 (+4.5 Energy, 3 seeds). Unlike methods that push OOD into the classifier null space (e.g., PFS, 14.38 KNN AUROC, worse than random), GEODE's adaptive target preserves the geometry that distance-based scorers depend on.

</details>


### [520] [The Partial Testimony of Logs: Evaluation of Language Model Generation under Confounded Model Choice](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01311)
*Jikai Jin,Vasilis Syrgkanis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Offline evaluation of language models from usage logs is biased due to confounding factors in user-side model choice. Combining observational logs, randomized experiments, and offline simulators can overcome this bias and yield causal insights into model performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of biased offline evaluations of language models where user-related factors confound comparisons, making raw scores unreliable for causal assessments.

Method: The proposed approach uses a combined method involving three sources: observational logs (OBS) for scale, randomized experiments (EXP) for unconfounded scoring, and offline simulators (SIM) that replay candidate models on cached contexts. An identification theorem supports this method.

Result: Six estimator families are assessed using semi-synthetic validation and practical benchmarks for summarization and coding tasks. Results show that relative performance depends on EXP supervision and alignment of target rewards with OBS structures.

Conclusion: A three-source approach successfully breaks bias in model evaluations, enabling causal comparisons of language models. Real-world impacts depend on balancing supervision scale and reward alignment.

Abstract: Offline evaluation of language models from usage logs is biased when model choice is confounded: the same user-side factors that influence which model is used can also influence how its output is judged, so raw comparisons of logged scores mix self-selected populations rather than estimating a common quantity of interest. A small randomized experiment can break this bias by overriding model choice, but in practice such experiments are scarce and costly. We study a three-source design that combines a large confounded observational log (OBS) for scale, a small randomized experiment (EXP) for unconfounded scoring, and an offline simulator (SIM) that replays candidate models on cached contexts. Our main result is an identification theorem showing that the randomized experiment and the simulator are together enough to recover causal model values; the observational log enters only afterward, to reduce estimation error rather than to make the causal comparison valid. Six estimator families are evaluated in a controlled semi-synthetic validation and in two real-task cached benchmarks for summarization and coding. No family dominates every regime; relative performance depends on the amount of unbiased EXP supervision and on how closely the target reward aligns with OBS-derived structure.

</details>


### [521] [A dimensional R2 regression metric](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01066)
*Jaesung Yoo,Stefan Lemke,Jian Zhong Guo,Kanaka Rajan,Adam Hantman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Dimensional R2 score (Dim-R2) as an alternative to the standard R2 score for evaluating regression tasks, overcoming its limitations and offering multidimensional insights into prediction accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current R2 score has limitations, including dimensional restrictions, lack of interpretability, and sensitivity to low-variance noise impacting regression task accuracy evaluation.

Method: The authors propose Dim-R2, a modified metric that extends R2 to handle arbitrary dimensional inputs, provide multidimensional accuracy patterns, and reduce noise sensitivity.

Result: Dim-R2 performance is validated using synthetic and multidimensional regression datasets, showing improved interpretability and flexibility compared to the standard R2.

Conclusion: Dim-R2 enhances regression evaluation by addressing shortcomings of the standard R2, better guiding prediction accuracy assessment and modeling decisions.

Abstract: R2 score is the standard metric for evaluating regression tasks, offering a normalized magnitude-agnostic measure of accuracy that captures variance. However, R2 has three key limitations: it is limited to at most two dimensional inputs, it reduces the score to a single scalar that hides rich patterns of prediction accuracy, and it is sensitive to low-variance noise channels which can yield large, uninterpretable negative values. We introduce the Dimensional R2 score (Dim-R2), a simple extension of R2 that accepts data of arbitrary dimensionality, provides a multidimensional view of accuracy, and reduces sensitivity to noise. We demonstrate its advantages on both synthetic sinusoidal data and three multidimensional regression datasets. Dim-R2 offers an interpretable and flexible metric that highlights patterns in regression accuracy, guiding regression modeling.

</details>


### [522] [Deep Variational Inference Symbolic Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01067)
*James Butterworth,Gevik Grigorian,Alejandro DiazDelaO*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Deep Variational Inference Symbolic Regression (DVISR), a Bayesian extension of Deep Symbolic Regression (DSR) for recovering posterior distributions of symbolic models under uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation was to enhance symbolic regression methodologies by integrating Bayesian techniques to quantify uncertainties, especially when data is noisy or sparse.

Method: The paper developed DVISR by modifying the reward mechanism to incorporate Bayesian evidence lower bound and extended neural network architecture to output distributions over constants in expressions.

Result: DVISR demonstrated capability in recovering true posterior distributions under various conditions, including scenarios with and without constant tokens, and its scalability was examined as the expression space expanded.

Conclusion: DVISR marks an advancement toward scalable Bayesian symbolic regression, offering a method to infer and quantify uncertainties across symbolic models.

Abstract: Symbolic regression discovers explicit, interpretable equations without assuming a functional form in advance. A Bayesian approach strengthens this through probability distributions over candidate expressions, thus quantifying uncertainty in the presence of noisy and limited data. Deep Symbolic Regression (DSR) uses a neural network to generate symbolic expressions, but it is designed to identify a single best-fitting expression rather than infer a posterior distribution over models. We introduce Deep Variational Inference Symbolic Regression (DVISR), a variational Bayesian extension of DSR. DVISR replaces the original reward with the integrand of the evidence lower bound. It also extends the network architecture to output distributions over constants within expressions, enabling posterior inference over both expression trees and their associated constants. We show that DVISR can recover the true posterior in simple settings, both with and without constant tokens, and we examine how its performance changes as the size of the expression space increases. These results position DVISR as a step toward scalable Bayesian symbolic regression with uncertainty over full symbolic models.

</details>


### [523] [Evaluating LLMs on Large-Scale Graph Property Estimation via Random Walks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01484)
*Sunil Kumar Maurya,Xin Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces EstGraph, a benchmark dataset for evaluating LLM reasoning on large graph properties with limited accessibility, using random walk sampling to overcome context length constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to assess and enhance the reasoning abilities of LLMs for working with real-world, large-scale graph data that is not fully accessible due to context length limitations.

Method: The authors propose the EstGraph dataset and design four tasks. They use task-specific prompts constructed with random walk sampling to represent large graph properties within the LLMs' context length constraints.

Result: Evaluation demonstrates the framework's utility, enabling LLMs to infer properties of large graphs effectively despite partial accessibility.

Conclusion: The study bridges the gap in evaluating LLMs on reasoning tasks with large graph structures, proving that random walk sampling is a viable method for context-limited scenarios.

Abstract: With the rapidly improving reasoning abilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), there is also a rising demand to use them in a wide variety of domains. This brings about the need to carefully evaluate the limits of the capabilities of these models with various tests and benchmarks. Graph structures are ubiquitous in real-world data, and are often used to represent and analyze relationship patterns within data. Many benchmarks have already been proposed in the graph literature to test the reasoning ability of LLMs to follow and execute graph algorithms. However, due to the limited context length of LLMs, these benchmarks consist of very small graphs. In real-world data, the size of graphs can be significantly larger, and in many cases, not fully accessible. In this paper, we examine a class of problems that arises with very large graphs having limited accessibility. We propose a large graph benchmark dataset, EstGraph, and introduce four distinct tasks designed to estimate large graph properties. We evaluate the reasoning abilities of LLMs on these tasks using a wide variety of graph datasets. In addition, we provide task-specific prompt constructions based on random walk sampling of large graphs (up to millions of nodes) that effectively convey sufficient information to LLMs within the limits of context length.

</details>


### [524] [Networked Information Aggregation for Binary Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01082)
*MohammadHossein Bateni,Zahra Hadizadeh,MohammadTaghi Hajiaghayi,Mahdi JafariRaviz,Shayan Taherijam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper examines whether agents in a directed acyclic graph (DAG) combining local and received features through sequential logit-passing can achieve comparable classification performance to a centralized setting with access to all features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The aim is to understand if distributed sequential learning in a DAG can achieve effective information aggregation and minimize predictive loss compared to a centralized model.

Method: The authors propose and analyze a sequential logit-passing protocol for agents in a DAG. These agents use binary cross-entropy and logistic regression to classify data, aiming to pool feature information distributed across the network efficiently.

Result: The authors prove an excess loss upper bound of $O(M/\sqrt{D})$ under specific conditions and identify network depth as a critical limitation to information aggregation. They also provide a matching lower bound.

Conclusion: While the approach shows promise, network depth fundamentally limits information aggregation. Deeper studies are needed to overcome this bottleneck and refine distributed learning approaches for classifications.

Abstract: We study networked binary classification on a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where each agent observes only a subset of the feature columns of a shared dataset. Agents act sequentially along the DAG: each receives prediction columns from its parents (if any), augments its local features with these columns, fits a logistic predictor by minimizing binary cross-entropy (BCE), and forwards its prediction column to its outgoing neighbors. We ask whether this sequential distributed training procedure achieves information aggregation, meaning that some agent attains small excess loss compared to the best logistic predictor trained with access to all feature columns.
  This question was studied for linear regression under squared loss by Kearns, Roth, and Ryu (SODA 2026). Extending their guarantees to classification is nontrivial because their analysis relies on quadratic structure that does not directly transfer to BCE with a logistic link. We analyze the resulting sequential logit-passing protocol and prove: (i) an excess loss upper bound of $O(M/\sqrt{D})$ on depth-$D$ paths under the condition that every $M$ contiguous subsequence of $M$ agents collectively observe all features, and (ii) a close lower bound showing instances with excess loss of at least $Ω(k/D)$ where $k$ is the dimension of the feature space. Together, these results identify network depth as a fundamental bottleneck for information aggregation in networked logistic regression.

</details>


### [525] [Stochastic Sparse Attention for Memory-Bound Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01910)
*Kyle Lee,Corentin Delacour,Kevin Callahan-Coray,Kyle Jiang,Can Yaras,Samet Oymak,Tathagata Srimani,Kerem Y. Camsari*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces SANTA, a method that sparsifies autoregressive decoding by reducing computational demands using stochastic sampling techniques for value-cache access and score stage operations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the computational inefficiencies in autoregressive decoding caused by the need to access all key and value vectors from the KV cache during long-context tasks.

Method: SANTA employs stochastic sampling for sparsifying value-cache access and introduces stratified sampling for variance reduction. It also proposes Bernoulli sampling to sparsify the score stage, facilitating energy-efficient and faster inference while maintaining accuracy.

Result: The proposed algorithms achieve a 1.5x speedup in decode-step attention kernel performance over existing techniques on an NVIDIA RTX 6000 Ada, without compromising accuracy in 32k-token contexts.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates the feasibility of sparse, multiplier-free, and energy-efficient inference for large contexts. It offers a complementary direction to other optimization techniques, paving the way for enhanced decoding processes.

Abstract: Autoregressive decoding becomes bandwidth-limited at long contexts, as generating each token requires reading all $n_k$ key and value vectors from KV cache. We present Stochastic Additive No-mulT Attention (SANTA), a method that sparsifies value-cache access by sampling $S \ll n_k$ indices from the post-softmax distribution and aggregates only those value rows. This yields an unbiased estimator of the post-softmax value aggregation while replacing value-stage multiply-accumulates with gather-and-add. We introduce stratified sampling to design variance-reduced, GPU-friendly variants, demonstrating $1.5\times$ decode-step attention kernel speedup over FlashInfer and FlashDecoding on an NVIDIA RTX 6000 Ada while matching baseline accuracy at 32k-token contexts. Finally, we propose Bernoulli $qK^\mathsf{T}$ sampling as a complementary technique to sparsify the score stage, reducing key-feature access through stochastic ternary queries. Both methods are orthogonal to upstream techniques such as ternary quantization, low-rank projections, and KV-cache compression. Together, they point toward sparse, multiplier-free, and energy-efficient inference. We open-source our kernels at: https://github.com/OPUSLab/SANTA.git

</details>


### [526] [Learning Discriminators for Resampling in the Ensemble Gaussian Mixture Filter through a Normalizing Flow Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01089)
*Zain Jabbar,Andrey A. Popov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper improves the Gaussian mixture filter by introducing a discriminator-informed resampling method to enhance the physical plausibility of posterior samples, demonstrating reduced error in numerical experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The current ensemble Gaussian mixture filter sometimes yields physically unrealistic samples, which degrade the quality of subsequent forecasts.

Method: The authors propose a discriminator-informed resampling approach leveraging normalizing flow to reject physically implausible particles during the resampling step.

Result: Numerical experiments with the Ikeda map and Lorenz '63 system confirm that the new method reduces error in scenarios with low ensemble sizes.

Conclusion: Discriminator-informed resampling provides a robust improvement for filtering methods, enhancing sample validity and reducing prediction error in non-linear systems.

Abstract: The ensemble Gaussian mixture filter (EnGMF) is a powerful, convergent particle filter capable of medium-to-high dimensional non-linear filtering. The EnGMF relies on a resampling step that can generate physically unrealistic posterior samples, that would subsequently produce physically meaningless forecasts. This work introduces the discriminator-informed resampling procedure, that augments the posterior resampling step with a discriminator that accepts or rejects candidate particles based on their physical plausibility. In this work these discriminators are learned through a normalizing flow approach. Numerical experiments on both the Ikeda map and the Lorenz '63 system show that discriminator informed resampling procedure consistently reduces error relative to the standard EnGMF in low-ensemble regimes.

</details>


### [527] [Learning to Race in Minutes: Infoprop Dyna on the Mini Wheelbot](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01096)
*Devdutt Subhasish,Henrik Hose,Sebastian Trimpe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a method allowing robots to learn directly from real-world interactions without the need for simulators. The Mini Wheelbot learns to race around a track in just 11 minutes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome the dependency on physics-based simulators and domain randomization for reinforcement learning in robotics by enabling robots to train directly in the real world.

Method: Applies the Infoprop Dyna framework, an uncertainty-aware model-based reinforcement learning method, to allow real-world robot training.

Result: The Mini Wheelbot successfully learns to race around a track within 11 minutes of real-world training.

Conclusion: Infoprop Dyna can serve as an efficient approach for enabling robots to train and adapt directly in real-world scenarios, eliminating the reliance on simulators.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has the potential to enable robots with fast, nonlinear, and unstable dynamics to reach the limits of their performance. However, most recent advances rely on carefully designed physics-based simulators and domain randomization to achieve successful sim-to-real transfer within reasonable wall-clock time. In this work, we bypass the need for such simulators and demonstrate that Infoprop Dyna, a state-of-the-art uncertainty-aware model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) framework, can enable robots to learn directly from real-world interactions. Using Infoprop Dyna, the Mini Wheelbot, an underactuated unicycle robot, learns to race around a track within 11 minutes of real-world experience.

</details>


### [528] [Stable GFlowNets with Probabilistic Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01729)
*Zengxiang Lei,Ananth Shreekumar,Jonathan Rosenthal,Ruoyu Song,Alvaro A. Cardenas,Daniel J. Fremont,Dongyan Xu,Satish Ukkusuri,Z. Berkay Celik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses training instability in Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) and introduces a theoretically grounded algorithm, Stable GFlowNets, to improve training and distribution fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training Generative Flow Networks often experiences instability such as loss spikes and mode collapse, which diminishes their practical application despite strong theoretical potential.

Method: The authors analyze GFlowNet objectives to understand their sensitivity and develop loss-to-TV bounds for fidelity guarantees. Based on these insights, they design the Stable GFlowNets algorithm to mitigate instability during training.

Result: Stable GFlowNets exhibit improved stability during training and better match the target distribution empirically.

Conclusion: By establishing theoretical guarantees and implementing Stable GFlowNets, the paper enhances the practical usability of GFlowNets in sample-efficient learning tasks.

Abstract: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) learn to sample states proportional to an unnormalized reward. Despite their theoretical promise, practical training is often unstable, exhibiting severe loss spikes and mode collapse. To tackle this, we first assess the sensitivity of GFlowNet objectives, demonstrating that a small Total Variation (TV) distance between the learned and target distributions does not preclude unbounded training loss. Motivated by this mismatch, we establish converse guarantees by deriving loss-to-TV bounds that certify global fidelity from bounded trajectory balance losses. Lastly, we propose Stable GFlowNets, an algorithm that leverages our theoretical results to stabilize training, and empirically demonstrate improved training behavior and superior distributional fidelity.

</details>


### [529] [Almost for Free: Crafting Adversarial Examples with Convolutional Image Filters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01098)
*Alexander Warnecke,Konrad Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a simple method to generate adversarial examples using optimized image filters, inspired by classic edge detection algorithms, and demonstrates high efficiency and transferability in deceiving machine learning models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of adversarial example generation by proposing a simpler and computationally efficient approach inspired by explainable machine learning.

Method: The method involves designing adversarial image filters based on classic edge detection algorithms, optimized specifically to deceive neural networks. These filters are lightweight, requiring only a single pass over the input.

Result: The proposed method achieves success rates of 30% to 80% across different neural networks using 3x3 filters. It significantly reduces the number of parameters compared to generative-model-based attacks by five orders of magnitude while maintaining high transferability.

Conclusion: The study reveals the fragility of neural networks to malicious noise and provides a lightweight, efficient approach for crafting adversarial examples. The findings also highlight shared structures between adversarial filters and classic image filters, contributing to insights into neural network vulnerabilities.

Abstract: Adversarial examples in machine learning are typically generated using gradients, obtained either directly through access to the model or approximated via queries to it. In this paper, we propose a much simpler approach to craft adversarial examples, drawing inspiration from insights of explainable machine learning. In particular, we design \emph{adversarial image filters} that are based on classic edge detection algorithms but optimized to deceive learning models. The resulting untargeted attacks are transferable and require only a single pass over the input. Empirically, we find that 3x3 filters already enable success rates between 30% and 80% on different neural networks. Compared to related approaches using generative models for crafting adversarial examples, we reduce the number of parameters by five orders of magnitude, resulting in a very efficient attack. When investigating the parameters of the learned filters, we observe interesting properties such as a high transferability between models and structures common to classic image filters. Our results provide further insights into the vulnerability of neural networks and their fragility to malicious noise.

</details>


### [530] [Large margin classifier with graph-based adaptive regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02027)
*Vítor M. Hanriot,Turíbio T. Salis,Luiz C. B. Torres,Frederico Coelho,Antonio P. Braga*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper explores the use of per-class regularization for Gabriel graph-based classifiers to improve handling outliers and class imbalance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in Gabriel graph-based binary classifiers concerning outliers and class imbalance, ensuring enhanced accuracy and flexibility.

Method: Introduction of per-class regularization hyperparameters and analysis of their impact on classifier training, using algorithms like hyperparameter tuning and validated by Friedman test.
