Our approach leverages denoising score models by estimating joint-window scores over consecutive activity snapshots (i.e., brain states) and converting these scores into calibrated, directed edge tests via cross-block score products. The key insight is that these products recover the Jacobian of the transition map between brain states under nonlinear dynamics. To cleanly separate lag-specific effects, we introduce minimal multi-block windows that condition on intermediate time points, avoiding the omitted-lag bias inherent in pairwise analyses. The resulting method, Score--Block Time Graphs (SBTG), identifies lag-specific directed interactions in sampled neuronal population data. We specifically apply SBTG to whole-brain C. elegans calcium imaging data to recover lag-specific circuit structure not resolved by current methods, including improved alignment with independent connectomes, cell-type-specific temporal organization, and neuromodulatory profiles consistent with known receptor kinetics. These findings highlight the potential for SBTG to serve as a practical ``AI for science'' tool by turning high-dimensional neural population recordings into statistically testable circuit hypotheses.

</details>


<div id='stat.ML'></div>

# stat.ML [[Back]](#toc)

### [799] [Mean Testing under Truncation beyond Gaussian](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01335)
*Yuhao Wang,Roberto Imbuzeiro Oliveira,Themis Gouleakis*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper explores the theoretical limitations and methodologies for high-dimensional mean testing under truncation, focusing on the impact of truncation-induced bias and its detectability limits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is motivated by the challenges truncation poses in high-dimensional mean testing, especially when the truncation set is unknown and affects the statistical reliability of decisions.

Method: The authors analyze the bias induced by truncation using distributional moments and directional median regularity assumptions. They derive sample complexity bounds for testing and propose a second-order test achieving near-optimal efficiency.

Result: Key findings include identifying a detectability threshold based on truncation bias, deriving sample complexity limits, and demonstrating improved bias regularization under the median regularity assumption.

Conclusion: This work provides a unified framework that bridges finite-moment and sub-Gaussian assumptions for mean testing under truncation, offering insights into signal detectability and sample-efficiency trade-offs.

Abstract: We characterize the fundamental limits of high-dimensional mean testing under arbitrary truncation, where samples are drawn from the conditional distribution $P(\cdot \mid S)$ for an unknown truncation set $S$ that may hide up to an $\varepsilon$-fraction of the probability mass. For distributions with $p$-th directional moments of magnitude at most $ν_{P,p}$, truncation induces a bias of order $O(ν_{P,p}\varepsilon^{1-1/p})$. This bias creates a sharp information-theoretic detectability floor: when the signal $α$ falls below this threshold, the null and alternative hypotheses are indistinguishable even with infinite data. Above this floor, we prove that a simple second-order test achieving near-optimal sample complexity $n = O\!\left(\frac{\|Σ_P\|}{(α-4ν_{P,p}\varepsilon^{1-1/p})^2}\sqrt{d}\right)$. We further identify a structural escape from this finite-moment bias barrier. Under a directional median regularity assumption, truncation bias improves to linear order $O(\varepsilon)$. This reveals an intermediate regime in which estimation requires $Θ(d)$ samples for uniform recovery, while testing recovers the classical $Θ(\sqrt d)$ rate once truncation bias is eliminated. Together, our results provide a unified framework for mean testing under truncation, connecting finite-moment, sub-Gaussian, and median-regular structural regimes.

</details>


### [800] [Stabilizing Private LASSO under Heterogeneous Covariates via Anisotropic Objective Perturbation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01492)
*Haruka Tanzawa,Ayaka Sakata*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper addresses the challenges of high-dimensional LASSO under differential privacy in settings with heterogeneous covariate scales by proposing a Gram-based anisotropic objective perturbation to stabilize and improve algorithm performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard data preprocessing in differential privacy consumes privacy budget and struggles with heterogeneous covariate scales, degrading algorithm stability and accuracy during estimation.

Method: The authors propose a Gram-based anisotropic objective perturbation, which uses a 'pre-distortion' approach to neutralize the distortion induced by covariate structures and restore isotropy in the estimation process.

Result: Using Approximate Message Passing (AMP) and state evolution analysis, the method significantly stabilizes algorithm convergence, enhances statistical efficiency, and improves privacy performance compared to uniform noise injection.

Conclusion: The proposed approach provides a more stable and efficient framework for private estimation in high-dimensional settings, avoiding the need for data-dependent preprocessing while maintaining differential privacy guarantees.

Abstract: We study high-dimensional LASSO under differential privacy via objective perturbation with heterogeneous covariate scales. In practical scenarios, covariates often exhibit diverse scales; however, standard preprocessing is problematic under privacy constraints, as it consumes additional privacy budget. This heterogeneity induces effective anisotropy in the objective perturbation via the inverse Gram matrix of covariates, which can degrade the stability and accuracy of algorithms. To address this, we propose a Gram-based anisotropic objective perturbation, a ``pre-distortion" strategy that counteracts the distortion from the covariate structure to restore isotropy in the estimation process. Using an Approximate Message Passing (AMP) framework and state evolution analysis, we demonstrate that our proposed perturbation significantly stabilizes convergence and improves both statistical efficiency and privacy performance compared to standard uniform noise injection. Our results provide theoretical insights into designing stable and efficient private estimators without relying on data-dependent preprocessing.

</details>


### [801] [Self-Normalized Martingales and Uniform Regret Bounds for Linear Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01628)
*Fan Chen,Jian Qian,Alexander Rakhlin,Nikita Zhivotovskiy*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper examines the scale-invariance of self-normalized martingale inequalities and their applications in online learning, uncovering fundamental limitations and possibilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of existing self-normalized martingale inequalities that rely on restrictive assumptions like bounded covariates and explicit regularization, and to explore scale-invariant bounds significant for bandit and reinforcement learning problems.

Method: The paper investigates the mathematical conditions under which scale-invariant bounds are obtainable, proving impossibility results in higher dimensions while deriving constructive bounds in low-dimensional settings. It further blends these results into practical algorithms and connects them with adaptive learning concepts like doubly-uniform regret.

Result: The key findings include:
1. Existence of $O(\log T)$ scale-invariant bounds in one-dimensional cases without assumptions on covariates.
2. Impossibility of nontrivial scale-invariant bounds in general for $d>1$.
3. Resolution of an open problem on doubly-uniform regret, showing it is feasible in $d=1$ but impossible sublinearly in $d>1$ under full generality.
4. Sublinear regret recovery for $d>1$ under added smoothness conditions.

Conclusion: This paper identifies a fundamental dimensional dichotomy for scale-invariant bounds in self-normalized martingales. It presents both theoretical impossibility results and practical algorithms that resolve longstanding open questions, advancing understanding in online learning and adaptive methods.

Abstract: Self-normalized martingale inequalities lie at the heart of confidence ellipsoids for online least squares and, more broadly, many bandit and reinforcement-learning results. Yet existing vector and scalar results typically rely on bounded covariates and an explicit regularization matrix, producing bounds that are \emph{not scale-invariant}: although the self-normalized quantity is scale-invariant by definition, its standard upper bounds are not.
  We characterize when scale-invariant upper bounds on self-normalized martingales are possible. Without further assumptions, we prove that nontrivial scale-invariant bounds exist only in dimension $d=1$; moreover, in $d=1$ we obtain $O(\log T)$ scale-invariant self-normalized bounds without any assumptions on the covariates. In contrast, for $d>1$ we show that no nontrivial scale-invariant bound can hold in full generality. We then connect this dichotomy to \emph{doubly-uniform} regret in online linear regression (i.e., regret bounds that are simultaneously independent of the covariate scale and the comparator norm) and use it to resolve the open question of Gaillard, Gerchinovitz, Huard, and Stoltz, \emph{``Uniform regret bounds over $\mathbb{R}^d$ for the sequential linear regression problem with the square loss''} (ALT 2019): in $d=1$ we give an explicit algorithm with $O(\log T)$ doubly-uniform regret, whereas for $d>1$ sublinear doubly-uniform regret is impossible.
  Finally, under a natural \emph{smoothness} condition (bounded Radon--Nikodym derivatives of the conditional covariate laws with respect to a fixed base measure), we recover sublinear regret for $d>1$ without bounded covariates and derive a self-normalized concentration inequality free of the usual regularization penalties, yielding arguably a first natural scale-invariant bound for adaptive, non-i.i.d. vector martingales.

</details>


### [802] [PRCD-MAP: Learning How Much to Trust Imperfect Priors in Causal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01669)
*Xihang Shan,Da Zhou*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces PRCD-MAP, a method for causal discovery using priors of varying reliability, and demonstrates its effectiveness across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations in existing causal discovery methods which either ignore priors or apply uniform trust to them, despite varying reliability.

Method: Develop a soft prior-consumption framework with edge-specific trust calibration through empirical Bayes, integrated into MAP optimization using adjusted regularization terms.

Result: PRCD-MAP achieves better performance than existing baselines, handles uninformative priors effectively, and shows scalable trust adjustment across diverse datasets.

Conclusion: PRCD-MAP provides an efficient and reliable method for causal discovery with heterogeneous priors, proving robust across real and synthetic settings.

Abstract: External priors of unknown reliability create a brittle trade-off in causal discovery: blind trust amplifies errors, blind rejection wastes signal. Real priors are also \emph{heterogeneously} reliable -- physical laws are trustworthy, LLM-suggested edges are speculative -- yet existing methods either ignore priors or impose them through globally uniform trust. We propose \textbf{PRCD-MAP}, a soft prior-consumption layer that assigns \emph{per-edge} trust to an imperfect prior and uses it to modulate a prior-aware $\ell_1$ penalty and prior-weighted $\ell_2$ regularizer in a MAP objective. Trust is calibrated by empirical Bayes on a Laplace-approximated marginal likelihood and propagated along the prior graph by an MLP, so that data-confirmed neighborhoods boost trust and contradictions suppress it. PRCD-MAP enjoys a population-level safety guarantee: it is $\varepsilon$-safe in expectation over the prior-generation distribution, with $\varepsilon = O(d^2/T)$ -- inheriting the oracle convergence rate. When the prior is uninformative, learned trust provably collapses to its floor and the method recovers a no-prior baseline. Empirically, on real CausalTime data PRCD-MAP exploits informative priors when present ($+0.123$ AUROC on AQI, $+0.043$ on Medical over PCMCI+), auto-attenuates on the anonymous-variable Traffic stress test, and retains a lead at $d{=}300$; against BayesDAG~\citep{annadani2023bayesdag} -- the closest soft-Bayesian baseline -- PRCD-MAP wins on every CausalTime dataset under a matched $W_0$-only protocol. A four-way ablation isolates each component: EB calibration and MLP trust propagation jointly carry the plurality of the gain, with positive sign on every dataset. Extensions to nonlinear (NAM) and cross-sectional settings show the calibrated-trust principle is setting-agnostic.

</details>


### [803] [Missingness-aware Data Imputation via AI-powered Bayesian Generative Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01676)
*Qiao Liu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: MissBGM is a Bayesian generative modeling approach to impute missing data, offering improvements in uncertainty quantification and performance over traditional and neural network-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the persistent challenge of missing data imputation, especially in scenarios where quantifying uncertainty is crucial, leveraging the expressive power of AI and the statistical rigor of Bayesian inference.

Method: MissBGM combines Bayesian generative modeling with neural networks to jointly model data generation and missingness mechanisms. It optimizes a stochastic framework with alternating updates for missing values, model parameters, and latent variables.

Result: MissBGM outperforms traditional and modern neural network-based imputation methods in multiple experimental setups, with theoretical convergence guarantees under mild conditions.

Conclusion: MissBGM is a scalable and principled solution for uncertainty-aware missing data imputation, combining the strengths of neural networks and Bayesian inference. It is publicly available for use by the research community.

Abstract: Missing data imputation remains a fundamental challenge in modern data science, especially when uncertainty quantification is essential. In this work, we propose MissBGM, an AI-powered missing data imputation method via Bayesian generative modeling that bridges the expressive flexibility of neural networks with the statistical rigor of Bayesian inference. Unlike existing methods that often focus on point estimates or treat the missingness mechanism implicitly, MissBGM explicitly and jointly models the data-generating and missingness mechanisms, providing principled posterior uncertainty over imputations rather than a single point estimate. We develop a stochastic optimization framework with alternating updates among missing values, model parameters, and latent variables until convergence. Our theoretical analysis shows that estimates of missing values from MissBGM converge consistently under mild assumptions. Empirically, we demonstrate that MissBGM achieves superior performance over traditional imputers and recent neural network-based methods across extensive experimental settings. These results establish MissBGM as a principled and scalable solution for modern missing data imputation. The code for MissBGM is open sourced at https://github.com/liuq-lab/MissBGM.

</details>


### [804] [Distributional Causal Mediation via Conditional Generative Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01765)
*Jinlun Zhang,Haoneng Huang,Zishu Zhan,Chunquan Ou*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces Distributional Causal Mediation Analysis (DCMA), a new framework for analyzing the impact of mediators on full outcome distributions rather than simple summary statistics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional mediation analysis methods often fail to capture complex causal mechanisms as they focus solely on summary statistics like mean effects, ignoring potential distributional changes.

Method: The proposed DCMA framework uses generative learning to estimate conditional distributions of mediators and outcomes from observational data. It employs Monte Carlo simulations to reconstruct interventional outcome distributions, extending the analysis beyond classical effects to capture intricate distributional contrasts.

Result: DCMA effectively identifies and estimates both classical summary effects and novel distributional disparities, validated by numerical experiments and real-world data applications.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates a significant advancement in understanding causal mechanisms by transitioning from mean-based contrasts to distributional analysis, offering deeper insights into treatment effects.

Abstract: Mediation analysis has traditionally focused on outcome-level summary contrasts, such as mean effects, which may obscure substantial distributional changes induced by complex and nonlinear causal mechanisms. We propose Distributional Causal Mediation Analysis (DCMA), a generative learning framework for identifying and estimating treatment effects on entire outcome distributions transmitted through multiple mediators. DCMA learns conditional generative models for the mediators and the outcome, recovering the relevant conditional distributions from observational data. Leveraging the identification formulas, it reconstructs interventional outcome distributions via Monte Carlo forward simulation by noise resampling, enabling the capture of both classical summary effects and rich distributional contrasts such as energy distance and the Wasserstein distance. Analytical error bounds are derived to decompose how estimation errors in the learned conditional models propagate to the reconstructed interventional outcome distributions. The empirical effectiveness of DCMA is demonstrated through numerical experiments and real-world data applications.

</details>


### [805] [A Semi-Supervised Kernel Two-Sample Test](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01775)
*Gyumin Lee,Shubhanshu Shekhar,Ilmun Kim*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes a semi-supervised two-sample testing method utilizing unlabeled covariate data, achieving superior power and maintaining asymptotic normality under the null for easier calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard two-sample tests fail to utilize covariate information, which could enhance performance, but including covariates complicates statistical assumptions and calibration procedures.

Method: A semi-supervised testing approach is introduced, using covariate data to design a test statistic that retains asymptotic normality under the null hypothesis, ensuring both effective calibration and improved test power.

Result: The proposed method demonstrates higher asymptotic power compared to kernel tests without covariates, with theoretical proof of consistency against various alternatives and validation through simulations.

Conclusion: The method effectively integrates covariate information in two-sample tests, providing a practical and theoretically robust solution with enhanced performance.

Abstract: We consider the problem of two-sample testing in a semi-supervised setting with abundant unlabeled covariate data. Standard two-sample tests neglect covariate information, which has the potential to significantly boost performance. However, incorporating covariates potentially breaks the exchangeability assumption under the null, which further complicates a calibration procedure. To address these issues, we propose a semi-supervised method that produces a test statistic with asymptotic normality, while effectively integrating additional information from covariates. Our test is straightforward to calibrate due to the asymptotic normality under the null and achieves asymptotic power that is often much higher than existing kernel tests without covariates. Furthermore, we formally show that the proposed method is consistent in power against fixed and local alternatives. Simulations confirm the practical and theoretical strengths of our approach.

</details>


### [806] [Stable Blanket with Hidden Variables and Cycles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01856)
*Hanqing Xiang*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper explores stable blankets in graphical causal models, expanding their characterization to include hidden variables and causal cycles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of existing stabilized regression methods, which primarily focus on acyclic causal models without hidden variables or cycles, and adapt them to more complex setups.

Method: The study uses acyclic directed mixed graphs (ADMGs) with $m$-separation for hidden variables and directed graphs (DGs) with $σ$-separation for cycles, introducing novel concepts like intervened sub-districts and strongly connected components.

Result: Provides graphical characterizations of Markov blankets, along with stable frontiers and blankets, under complex settings of hidden variables and cycles. Additionally, it identifies conditions for conditional independence and minimal predictor sets.

Conclusion: The research extends stabilized regression's theoretical framework to more generalized and practical scenarios, ensuring broader applicability.

Abstract: Stabilized regression aims to identify a set of predictors whose conditional relationship with a response variable remains invariant across different environments. Existing graphical characterizations of the stable blanket are mainly developed for structural causal models (SCMs) without hidden variables or causal cycles. However, latent variables and feedback relationships naturally arise in many applications, and they can change both the Markov blanket and the set of predictors that remain stable under interventions. This paper studies stable blankets in graphical causal models with hidden variables, causal cycles, and both features simultaneously. For models with hidden variables, we use acyclic directed mixed graphs (ADMGs) and $m$-separation to characterize the Markov blanket and to construct intervention-stable predictor sets. We introduce the notion of an intervened sub-district and use it to describe how interventions may affect districts connected to the response. For models with cycles, we work with directed graphs (DGs) and directed mixed graphs (DMGs) together with $σ$-separation, treating strongly connected components (SCCs) as the basic graphical units. We then combine these ideas to analyze models with both hidden variables and cycles. The main results give graphical characterizations of Markov blankets, stable frontiers, and stable blankets in these generalized settings. In particular, we identify conditions under which the response is conditionally independent of intervention variables given a suitable predictor set, and we describe when such sets are minimal or unique. These results extend the graphical interpretation of stabilized regression beyond acyclic fully observed models.

</details>


### [807] [Online Generalised Predictive Coding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02675)
*Mehran H. Z. Bazargani,Szymon Urbas,Adeel Razi,Thomas Brendan Murphy,Karl Friston*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper presents Online Dynamic Expectation Maximisation (ODEM), an extension of generalised filtering for real-time data assimilation, parameter learning, and uncertainty estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve online data assimilation methods for dynamic systems by introducing a biologically inspired framework that handles state estimation, parameter learning, and uncertainty quantification simultaneously.

Method: The authors adapt the Dynamic Expectation Maximisation (DEM) model for online settings by enabling slow updating of parameters and precisions, while allowing rapid Bayesian updating of dynamic hidden states. They validate the Online DEM framework using numerical simulations involving non-linear and chaotic generative models.

Result: The proposed ODEM framework demonstrates robust performance in tracking latent states of generative processes, even when the generative model dynamics differ significantly from the framework’s assumptions.

Conclusion: ODEM offers a powerful tool for online inference and uncertainty estimation in dynamic systems, with potential applications in biologically inspired computing models and adaptive systems.

Abstract: This paper introduces an extension of generalised filtering for online applications. Generalised filtering refers to data assimilation schemes that jointly infer latent states, learn unknown model parameters, and estimate uncertainty in an integrated framework -- e.g., estimate state and observation noise -- at the same time (i.e., triple estimation). This framework appears across disciplines under different names, including variational Kalman-Bucy filtering in engineering, generalised predictive coding in neuroscience, and Dynamic Expectation Maximisation (DEM) in time-series analysis. Here, we specialise DEM for ``online'' data assimilation, through a separation of temporal scales. We describe the variational principles and procedures that allow one to assimilate data in a way that allows for a slow updating of parameters and precisions, which contextualise fast Bayesian belief updating about the dynamic hidden states. Using numerical studies, we demonstrate the validity of online DEM (ODEM) using a non-linear -- and potentially chaotic -- generative model, to show that the ODEM scheme can track the latent states of the generative process, even when its functional form differs fundamentally from the dynamics of the generative model. Framed from a neuro-mimetic predictive coding perspective, ODEM offers a biologically inspired solution to online inference, learning, and uncertainty estimation in dynamic environments.

</details>


### [808] [Adaptive Estimation and Inference in Semi-parametric Heterogeneous Clustered Multitask Learning via Neyman Orthogonality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01907)
*Hanxiao Chen,Debarghya Mukherjee*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes a novel approach to clustered multitask learning, addressing challenges from task heterogeneity and infinite-dimensional nuisance components by introducing an adaptive fused orthogonal estimator.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multitask learning methods struggle with heterogeneity and misaligned features among tasks. The research seeks to establish a methodology to effectively address this challenge by leveraging latent cluster structures in target parameters.

Method: The paper proposes a semiparametric estimator combining Neyman-orthogonal losses with data-driven pairwise fusion penalties. Task-specific pilot estimates calibrate fusion penalties, and an adaptive aggregation approach reduces nuisance-parameter estimation error.

Result: The estimator achieves high-probability clustering recovery, parametric convergence rates proportional to cluster size, and asymptotic normality. Empirical results outperform baselines, and the method identifies meaningful clusters in U.S. electricity price elasticity.

Conclusion: The proposed method effectively addresses heterogeneity in multitask learning, offers theoretical guarantees, and demonstrates superior performance in both simulations and a real-world application, establishing it as a significant advancement in the field.

Abstract: We study clustered multitask learning in a semiparametric setting where tasks share a latent cluster structure in their target parameters but exhibit heterogeneous, potentially infinite-dimensional nuisance components. Such heterogeneity poses a major challenge for existing multitask learning methods, which typically rely on aligned feature spaces or homogeneous task structures. To address this challenge, we propose an adaptive fused orthogonal estimator that integrates Neyman-orthogonal losses with data-driven pairwise fusion penalties. Our framework leverages task-specific pilot estimates to calibrate the fusion penalties and combines adaptive aggregation with orthogonalization to mitigate the impact of nuisance-parameter estimation error. Theoretically, we show that the proposed estimator achieves exact recovery of the latent clustering with high probability and attains pooled parametric convergence rates proportional to cluster size. Moreover, we establish asymptotic normality and show that, asymptotically, our estimator matches the performance of an oracle procedure that knows the true clustering in advance. Empirically, we show that the proposed method consistently outperforms strong baselines in various simulation setups. A real-world application to U.S. residential energy consumption demonstrates the effectiveness of our approach in uncovering meaningful regional clustering in electricity price elasticity, showcasing the efficacy of our method.

</details>


### [809] [Extrapolation in Statistical Learning with Extreme Value Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01909)
*Sebastian Engelke,Nicola Gnecco,Anne Sabourin*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper reviews advancements in using extreme value theory (EVT) to improve machine learning tasks involving data scarcity in extreme regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle challenges in machine learning caused by lack of data in the tails, hindering accurate extrapolation in various domains.

Method: The authors synthesize recent developments using EVT-based methods, analyzing univariate and multivariate distributions for practical applications.

Result: The study highlights state-of-the-art EVT applications in regression, anomaly detection, AI, and dimension reduction.

Conclusion: EVT offers viable frameworks for data extrapolation in extreme-value scenarios, paving the way for innovative machine learning solutions.

Abstract: Extreme value theory provides rigorous theory and statistical tools for extrapolation in machine learning, particularly in settings where traditional methods struggle due to data scarcity in the tails. A broad range of tasks benefit from these advances, including regression and classification beyond the training data, extreme quantile regression, supervised and unsupervised dimension reduction, generative artificial intelligence and anomaly detection. This review synthesizes recent developments in these fields at the intersection of statistical learning and extreme value theory, with a focus on principled methods based on asymptotically motivated representations of the tail of univariate and multivariate distributions. We consider different theoretical frameworks for both asymptotically dependent and independent data and discuss how they translate into efficient statistical methods for extrapolation to extreme regions. By addressing both theoretical and practical aspects, we offer a comprehensive overview of the state-of-the-art in this quickly evolving field, and identify promising directions for future research.

</details>


### [810] [MIRA: A Score for Conditional Distribution Accuracy and Model Comparison](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02014)
*Sammy Sharief,Justine Zeghal,Gabriel Missael Barco,Pablo Lemos,Yashar Hezaveh,Laurence Perreault-Levasseur*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: Mira is proposed as a score to assess the accuracy of conditional distributions using joint samples, enabling model comparison without requiring evidence computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in validating Bayesian models and quantifying alignment between conditional distributions of candidate models and true processes.

Method: Developing the Mira score through an analytic expression of the Mira statistic, allowing reference values and uncertainty estimates when the candidate matches the true distribution.

Result: Demonstrations show Mira’s effectiveness in various toy problems and Bayesian inference tasks.

Conclusion: Mira facilitates Bayesian model comparison by validating posteriors directly, eliminating the need for challenging evidence computation.

Abstract: We introduce Mira, a sample-based score for assessing the accuracy of a candidate conditional distribution using only joint samples from the true data-generating process. Relying on the principle that distributions coincide if they assign equal probability mass to all regions, we derive an analytic expression for the Mira statistic, whose average defines the Mira score. This formulation further allows us to compute theoretical reference values and uncertainty estimates when the candidate distribution matches the true one. This framework enables model comparison by quantifying the alignment between the conditional distribution of a candidate model and the true data generating process. Consequently, Mira enables Bayesian model comparison through direct posterior validation, bypassing the challenging evidence computation. We demonstrate its effectiveness across several toy problems and Bayesian inference tasks.

</details>


### [811] [The Causal Description Gap: Information-Theoretic Separations Across Pearl's Hierarchy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02177)
*Seyed Morteza Emadi*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper quantifies the information gap required to answer higher-level causal queries in Pearl's hierarchy using Kolmogorov complexity and discovers tight bounds for binary SCMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine how much additional information (bits) is needed to address causal questions at higher levels of Pearl's causal hierarchy using answers from lower levels.

Method: The researchers use Kolmogorov complexity to analyze description lengths for answer oracles, focusing on binary acyclic SCMs. They establish tight bounds through mathematical constructions and analysis.

Result: They find quadratic information gaps between observational and interventional queries and demonstrate order-optimal bounds under specific conditions, including indegree constraints. Counterfactual queries maintain significant gaps as well.

Conclusion: The study rigorously quantifies the information complexity of causal hierarchies, providing insights into the limits of observational and interventional data in answering higher-level causal questions.

Abstract: Pearl's causal hierarchy shows that observational, interventional, and counterfactual queries are qualitatively distinct. We ask a quantitative version of this question: how many additional bits are needed to specify higher-rung causal answers once lower-rung answers are known? We formalize this via query-class description length, the Kolmogorov complexity of the answer oracle induced by an SCM for a class of queries. Our main construction gives binary acyclic SCMs whose observational distribution has constant description length, while the single-variable interventional answer oracle has description length $Θ(n^2)$. A degree-sensitive upper bound shows that finite-gate-schema SCMs of indegree $d$ have observational-interventional gap at most $O(nd \log(en/d) + n \log n)$, making the quadratic construction order-optimal in the dense regime and a rooted-tree construction order-optimal for bounded indegree. The quadratic separation persists under $\varepsilon$-accurate total-variation descriptions for every fixed $\varepsilon < 1/4$. At the next rung, the full hard-do interventional oracle can still leave a $Θ(n)$ counterfactual description gap. A general ambiguity-to-bits theorem and Shannon analogue show that these gaps equal the logarithm of residual higher-rung ambiguity up to lower-order terms.

</details>


### [812] [Measuring Differences between Conditional Distributions using Kernel Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02260)
*Peter Moskvichev,Siu Lun Chau,Dino Sejdinovic*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces a unified framework for measuring divergence between conditional distributions using kernel-based methods, called conditional maximum mean discrepancy (CMMD), and proposes a novel estimator with robustness properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for a unified theoretical framework to measure discrepancies between conditional distributions in statistics and machine learning, addressing fragmentation in existing kernel-based methods literature.

Method: The paper defines and formalizes CMMD as a family of metrics and studies its theoretical aspects. It introduces different levels of CMMD using various RKHS embeddings and proposes a doubly robust estimator for CMMD.

Result: The doubly robust estimator of CMMD maintains consistency if at least one underlying model is correctly specified. Numerical experiments demonstrate its ability to capture complex conditional dependencies.

Conclusion: CMMD provides a coherent, powerful, and robust framework for measuring divergence between conditional distributions, advancing both theoretical understanding and practical application of kernel-based methods.

Abstract: Comparing conditional distributions is a fundamental challenge in statistics and machine learning, with applications across a wide range of domains. While proposed methods for measuring discrepancies using kernel embeddings of distributions in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) provide powerful non-parametric techniques, the existing literature remains fragmented and lacks a unified theoretical treatment. This paper addresses this gap by establishing a coherent framework for studying kernel-based methods to measure divergence between conditional distributions through what we refer to as conditional maximum mean discrepancy (CMMD). The CMMD consists of a family of metrics which we call levels, with three special cases each using a different type of RKHS embedding: CMMD$_0$ (conditional mean operators), CMMD$_1$ (conditional mean embeddings), and CMMD$_2$ (joint mean embeddings). We additionally introduce a general level $s$ CMMD, clarifying the required assumptions, and establishing mathematical connections between the levels through the lens of operator-based smoothing. In addition to reviewing previously proposed estimators, we introduce a novel doubly robust estimator for the CMMD that maintains consistency provided at least one of the underlying models is correctly specified. We provide numerical experiments demonstrating that the CMMD effectively captures complex conditional dependencies for statistical testing.

</details>


### [813] [Active multiple matrix completion with adaptive confidence sets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02458)
*Andrea Locatelli,Alexandra Carpentier,Michal Valko*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper presents a new multi-task active learning framework for solving multiple matrix completion problems simultaneously and introduces an algorithm, MAlocate, which adapts to unknown matrix ranks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation lies in addressing challenges in market segmentation, dealing with matrices representing regional customer preferences, which vary in size and rank.

Method: The introduced algorithm, MAlocate, is designed to adaptively handle matrices of different sizes and unknown ranks by selecting samples optimally in a multi-task learning setting.

Result: The study establishes that MAlocate is minimax-optimal through theoretical analysis and validates its performance through synthetic experiments.

Conclusion: MAlocate effectively solves matrix completion challenges in multi-task settings, particularly for unknown rank and size variability, and achieves optimal performance as proven theoretically and experimentally.

Abstract: In this work, we formulate a new multi-task active learning setting in which the learner's goal is to solve multiple matrix completion problems simultaneously. At each round, the learner can choose from which matrix it receives a sample from an entry drawn uniformly at random. Our main practical motivation is market segmentation, where the matrices represent different regions with different preferences of the customers. The challenge in this setting is that each of the matrices can be of a different size and also of a different rank which is unknown. We provide and analyze a new algorithm, MAlocate that is able to adapt to the unknown ranks of the different matrices. We then give a lower-bound showing that our strategy is minimax-optimal and demonstrate its performance with synthetic experiments.

</details>


### [814] [Middle-mile logistics through the lens of goal-conditioned reinforcement learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02461)
*Onno Eberhard,Thibaut Cuvelier,Michal Valko,Bruno De Backer*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper addresses middle-mile logistics using a multi-objective goal-conditioned MDP for routing parcels and employs graph neural networks combined with model-free RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to optimize the routing of parcels through a network of hubs in middle-mile logistics, a problem characterized by finite truck capacity and complex environmental states.

Method: The proposed method integrates graph neural networks with model-free reinforcement learning, utilizing small feature graphs derived from the environment state.

Result: The approach rephrased the logistics routing problem effectively and appears to harness the capabilities of graph neural networks and RL for computational efficiency.

Conclusion: The integration of graph neural networks and model-free RL provides a novel framework to address middle-mile logistics effectively, enhancing parcel routing decisions.

Abstract: Middle-mile logistics describes the problem of routing parcels through a network of hubs linked by trucks with finite capacity. We rephrase this as a multi-object goal-conditioned MDP. Our method combines graph neural networks with model-free RL, extracting small feature graphs from the environment state.

</details>


### [815] [Black-box optimization of noisy functions with unknown smoothness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02462)
*Jean-Bastien Grill,Michal Valko,Rémi Munos*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces POO (Parallel Optimistic Optimization), an adaptive algorithm for black-box optimization under noisy conditions, which performs nearly as well as methods requiring smoothness knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of optimizing noisy, high-dimensional black-box functions without prior knowledge of their smoothness.

Method: The authors develop POO, an algorithm that adaptively optimizes black-box functions by leveraging local smoothness properties without requiring prior smoothness information.

Result: POO achieves performance close to the best-known algorithms requiring smoothness knowledge and is applicable to a broader class of difficult optimization problems.

Conclusion: POO overcomes the need for smoothness knowledge and provides strong performance guarantees, making it suited for optimizing challenging noisy functions.

Abstract: We study the problem of black-box optimization of a function f of any dimension, given function evaluations perturbed by noise. The function is assumed to be locally smooth around one of its global optima, but this smoothness is unknown. Our contribution is an adaptive optimization algorithm, POO or parallel optimistic optimization, that is able to deal with this setting. POO performs almost as well as the best known algorithms requiring the knowledge of the smoothness. Furthermore, POO works for a larger class of functions than what was previously considered, especially for functions that are difficult to optimize, in a very precise sense. We provide a finite-time analysis of POO's performance, which shows that its error after n evaluations is at most a factor of sqrt(ln n) away from the error of the best known optimization algorithms using the knowledge of the smoothness.

</details>


### [816] [ParaRNN: An Interpretable and Parallelizable Recurrent Neural Network for Time-Dependent Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02692)
*Yuxi Cai,Lan Li,Feiqing Huang,Guodong Li*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces ParaRNN, a model enhancing RNN interpretability and efficiency for time-dependent data applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address RNN limitations in interpretability and slow training despite their success in machine learning.

Method: Proposes a Parallelized RNN (ParaRNN) with an additive representation decoupling recurrent dynamics into interpretable components and enabling parallelization.

Result: ParaRNN matches vanilla RNN performance in sequential modeling tasks but provides better interpretability and training efficiency.

Conclusion: ParaRNN offers a successful trade-off between interpretability, efficiency, and performance, especially for statistical applications.

Abstract: The proliferation of large-scale and structurally complex data has spurred the integration of machine learning methods into statistical modeling. Recurrent neural networks (RNNs), a foundational class of models for time-dependent data, can be viewed as nonlinear extensions of classical autoregressive moving average models. Despite their flexibility and empirical success in machine learning, RNNs often suffer from limited interpretability and slow training, which hinders their use in statistics. This paper proposes the Parallelized RNN (ParaRNN), a novel model composed of multiple small recurrent units. ParaRNN admits an additive representation that decouples recurrent dynamics into interpretable components, whose behavior can be characterized through recurrence features. This interpretability enables its applications in nonparametric regression for time-dependent data, while the design also allows efficient parallelization. The approximation capacity and non-asymptotic prediction error bounds in a nonparametric regression setting are established for ParaRNN. Empirical results on three sequential modeling tasks further demonstrate that ParaRNN achieves performance comparable to vanilla RNNs while offering improved interpretability and efficiency.

</details>


### [817] [Random-Effects Algorithm for Random Objects in Metric Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02693)
*Marcos Matabuena,Mateo Cámara*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a nonlinear Fréchet-based algorithm for random-effects modeling in metric spaces, enabling efficient estimation and predictions in non-Euclidean data like random graphs and distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation of this paper is to address the lack of general random-effects frameworks for analyzing non-Euclidean random objects, which are increasingly common in modern datasets from various scientific fields.

Method: The authors propose a nonlinear Fréchet-based algorithm for random-effects modeling of objects in metric spaces, validating it through M-estimation theory and ensuring consistency of prediction targets.

Result: Empirical evaluations on synthetic and digital health datasets demonstrate that the proposed method can outperform existing methods designed for Hilbert spaces, even though it works in more general metric spaces.

Conclusion: The proposed random-effects model is a significant advancement for handling non-Euclidean random objects, providing both theoretical consistency and improved empirical performance over Hilbert space-based approaches.

Abstract: Across many scientific disciplines, multiple observations are collected from the same experimental units, and in modern datasets these observations often arise as non-Euclidean random objects. In such settings, the incorporation of random effects is a critical modeling step for efficient estimation and personalized prediction. Although mixed-effects models are well established for scalar outcomes and, more recently, for functional data in Hilbert spaces, general random-effects frameworks for objects in metric spaces remain underdeveloped. In this paper, we propose a nonlinear Fréchet-based algorithm for random-effects modeling of arbitrary random objects defined on a metric space. Using M-estimation theory, we establish conditions under which the proposed metric-space prediction target is consistently estimated under a working random-effects formulation. We then evaluate the empirical performance of the proposed method using both synthetic data and digital health datasets that require practical tools for analyzing random objects in metric spaces, such as multivariate probability distributions and random graphs. We show that, although our method is developed beyond Hilbert spaces, it can outperform existing Hilbert space-based methods.

</details>


<div id='math.CO'></div>

# math.CO [[Back]](#toc)

### [818] [Trees and Graphs with Non Log-concave Dominating Set Sequence via AI Tools](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02193)
*Alina Du,Steven Heilman,Greta Panova*

Main category: math.CO

TL;DR: The paper presents new examples of graphs and trees with dominating set sequences that are not log-concave, employing findings from a reinforcement learning tool, while demonstrating both exceptions and consistencies in log-concavity across graph structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to study the log-concavity properties of dominating set sequences in graphs and trees and address unanswered questions regarding occurrences of log-concavity across various graph types.

Method: The study leverages PatternBoost, a transformer-based reinforcement learning tool, to generate examples of non-log-concave dominating set sequences, and employs mathematical construction to extend results from earlier work on independent set sequences.

Result: The authors generated examples of non-log-concave dominating set sequences and proved the existence of trees with sequences that exhibit non-log-concavity across an arbitrarily large number of indices. They also established that certain caterpillar graphs and continuous analogues exhibit log-concave dominating set sequences.

Conclusion: The findings expand understanding of dominating set sequence behavior, showcasing both non-log-concave and log-concave characteristics within different classes of graphs, with implications for the study of graph properties and reinforcing existing mathematical techniques.

Abstract: We give new examples of graphs and trees with dominating set sequences that are not log-concave. These examples were generated by PatternBoost, a transformer-based reinforcement learning software developed by Charton-Ellenberg-Wagner-Williamson. We also show: for any positive integer $m$, there exists a tree whose dominating set sequence is not log-concave for at least $m$ indices by modifying a similar construction of Bautista-Ramos for the independent set sequence. We show that a large class of caterpillar graphs has log-concave dominating set sequences. A continuous analogue of the sequence is also log-concave for all graphs.

</details>


<div id='cs.GT'></div>

# cs.GT [[Back]](#toc)

### [819] [The (Marginal) Value of a Search Ad: An Online Causal Framework for Repeated Second-price Auctions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01756)
*Yuxiao Wen,Zihao Hu,Yanjun Han,Yuan Yao,Zhengyuan Zhou*

Main category: cs.GT

TL;DR: The paper addresses inefficiencies in auto-bidding algorithms for digital advertising by modeling ad value as a treatment effect instead of revenue, proposing algorithms that optimize regret in second-price auctions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current auto-bidding approaches ignore the causal impact of paid ad exposure, leading to overspending by not accounting for the revenue generated through organic search results.

Method: The authors study bidding through a causal framework and devise algorithms that utilize the information revealed by second-price payment rules to minimize regret efficiently.

Result: Developed algorithms achieve rate-optimal regret in second-price auctions and outperform similar approaches in first-price auctions.

Conclusion: Second-price auctions provide better opportunities for optimizing regret and improving bidding strategies, leveraging insights from treatment-effect modeling.

Abstract: Existing auto-bidding algorithms in digital advertising often treat the value of an ad opportunity as the revenue obtained when an ad is shown and/or clicked, and bid accordingly. This can lead to wasteful spending because the true value is the marginal gain from paid exposure: even without winning a sponsored slot, an advertiser may still earn revenue via an organic search result (e.g., on Google or Amazon). Motivated by recent work, we model ad value as a treatment effect--the outcome difference between winning and losing the auction--and study online learning for bidding in second-price (Vickrey) auctions under this causal perspective. We develop algorithms that attain rate-optimal regret under several feedback models. A key ingredient exploits the information revealed by the second-price payment rule, which strictly improves regret relative to analogous learning problems in first-price auctions.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [820] [Separation Assurance between Heterogeneous Fleets of Small Unmanned Aerial Systems via Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01041)
*Iman Sharifi,Hyeong Tae Kim,Maheed Hatem Ahmed,Mahsa Ghasemi,Peng Wei*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: This paper explores tactical deconfliction in urban airspace with heterogeneous fleets of homogeneous sUASs, using multi-agent reinforcement learning to train adaptive policies for conflict resolution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The researchers aim to ensure safe, conflict-free urban airspace for sUASs and investigate how different fleet configurations or policies impact equilibrium and fairness.

Method: A multi-agent reinforcement learning framework using an enhanced PPOA2C model was implemented. Fleets trained policies independently while ensuring privacy, and experiments were conducted in simulated urban environments.

Result: Policies using PPOA2C successfully resolved conflicts, outperforming rule-based systems. However, equilibria favored stronger or specific policy configurations, highlighting fairness challenges.

Conclusion: While tactical deconfliction is achievable with adaptive policies, inequities arise in fleet advantage, emphasizing the need for fairness-oriented methods in multi-fleet sUAS operations.

Abstract: In the envisioned future dense urban airspace, multiple companies will operate heterogeneous fleets of small unmanned aerial systems (sUASs), where each fleet includes several homogeneous aircraft with identical policies and configurations, e.g., equipage, sensing, and communication ranges, making tactical deconfliction highly complex for the aircraft. This paper aims to address two core questions: (1) Can tactical deconfliction policies converge or reach an equilibrium to ensure a conflict-free airspace when companies operate heterogeneous fleets of homogeneous aircraft? (2) If so, will the converged policies discriminate against companies operating sUASs with weaker configurations? We investigate a multi-agent reinforcement learning paradigm in which homogeneous aircraft within heterogeneous fleets operate concurrently to perform package delivery missions over Dallas, Texas, USA. An attention-enhanced Proximal Policy Optimization-based Advantage Actor-Critic (PPOA2C) framework is employed to resolve intra- and inter-fleet conflicts, with each fleet independently training its own policy while preserving privacy. Experimental results show that two fleets with distinct, shared PPOA2C policies can reach an equilibrium to maintain safe separation. While two PPOA2C policies outperform two strong rule-based baselines in terms of conflict resolution, a PPOA2C policy exhibits safer interaction with a rule-based policy, indicating adaptive capabilities of PPOA2C policies. Furthermore, we conducted extensive policy-configuration evaluations, which reveal that equilibria between similar policy types tend to favor fleets with stronger configurations. Even under similar configurations but different policy types, the equilibrium favors one of the heterogeneous policies, underscoring the need for fairness-aware conflict management in heterogeneous sUAS operations.

</details>


### [821] [The Cost of Consensus: Isolated Self-Correction Prevails Over Unguided Homogeneous Multi-Agent Debate](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00914)
*Blaž Bertalanič,Carolina Fortuna*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper explores debate dynamics among teams of homogeneous language models (LLMs). It finds that peer debate introduces failures like conformity, fragility, and consensus collapse, offering no clear benefits over isolated self-correction while being more resource-intensive.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the failure mechanics of multi-agent debate among homogeneous LLMs and assess its effectiveness compared to self-correction methods.

Method: Conducted an empirical study on teams of 10 homogeneous LLMs engaging in debates over three rounds on high-difficulty benchmarks. Analyzed dynamics like conformity, fragility, and consensus collapse, and compared them to self-correction and noise-injected baselines. Varied communication density and sampling temperatures for comprehensive insights.

Result: Peer debate fosters issues like sycophantic conformity (85.5%), contextual fragility (70% vulnerability), and consensus collapse (32.3% oracle gap). Accuracy suffered relative to isolated self-correction, and debates were significantly more resource-intensive (2.1-3.4 times more tokens).

Conclusion: Homogeneous LLM teams in debate modes without structured roles fail to outperform self-correction and are less efficient, stressing the need for structured approaches to multi-agent collaboration.

Abstract: Multi-agent debate, where teams of LLMs iteratively exchange rationales and vote on answers, is widely deployed under the assumption that peer review filters hallucinations. Yet the failure dynamics of homogeneous debate remain poorly understood, therefore we report findings from a controlled empirical study of teams of $N{=}10$ homogeneous agents (Qwen2.5-7B, Llama-3.1-8B, Ministral-3-8B) across $R{=}3$ debate rounds on two high-difficulty benchmarks (GSM-Hard and MMLU-Hard). We compare peer debate against isolated self-correction and a stochastic noise control that injects rationales from unrelated problems. We decompose debate failure into three model-dependent pathways: sycophantic conformity, where agents uncritically adopt majority answers (modal adoption up to 85.5%); contextual fragility, where peer rationales destabilize previously correct reasoning (vulnerability rate up to 70.0%); and consensus collapse, where plurality voting discards correct answers already present in the generation pool (oracle gap up to 32.3 percentage points). Ablations over communication density ($K \in \{2,4,9\}$) and sampling temperature ($T \in \{0.4, 0.7\}$) show that conformity reaches high levels at minimal peer exposure ($K{=}2$) and intensifies with greater initial diversity. Across all configurations, debate consumes 2.1-3.4$\times$ more tokens (up to 28,631 tokens per problem) than self-correction for equal or lower accuracy. Our results indicate that, within the 7-8B parameter class, homogeneous teams without structured roles do not benefit from unguided peer exchange, and that isolated self-correction consistently offers a more favorable cost-accuracy tradeoff.

</details>


### [822] [Talk is Cheap, Communication is Hard: Dynamic Grounding Failures and Repair in Multi-Agent Negotiation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01750)
*Yiheng Yao,Chelsea Zou,Robert D. Hawkins*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: This paper introduces a negotiation game focusing on dynamic grounding in multi-agent LLMs, exposing coordination failures and their causes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the overlooked ability of multi-agent systems to repair grounding breakdowns across turns in dynamic interactions.

Method: Developed a multi-turn negotiation game, analyzed coordination failures, and established measurable baselines to pinpoint issues in dynamic grounding.

Result: Identified four failure modes: absence of shared interaction history, anchoring bias, perfunctory fairness, and referential binding errors.

Conclusion: Dynamic grounding is a critical factor in multi-agent coordination, necessitating improved joint plan formation, commitment, and execution processes.

Abstract: Grounding is the collaborative process of establishing mutual belief sufficient for the current communicative purpose. While static grounding maps language to a shared, externally observable context, dynamic grounding is a joint activity where meaning is negotiated through interaction. Current multi-agent Large Language Model (LLM) benchmarks focus on static, one-shot tasks, overlooking the ability to repair grounding breakdowns across turns. We introduce an iterated, multi-turn negotiation game in which two agents allocate shared resources toward private projects with verifiable jointly optimal outcomes. While individual agents can identify Pareto-optimal allocations in isolation, agent dyads consistently fail to reach them across open- and closed-source models. Our investigation reveals four failure modes: (1) coordination degrades when shared interaction history is absent; (2) yet accumulated context can itself become a liability through stubborn anchoring, where initial proposals are treated as axiomatic rather than negotiable; (3) a reliance on perfunctory fairness (equal resource splits) over reward-maximizing coordination; and (4) failures in referential binding, where agents lose track of commitments across turns. These results highlight dynamic grounding as a critical and understudied axis of multi-agent coordination. Our framework decomposes the coordination gap into measurable components: the oracle baseline establishes that the gap is not attributable to individual reasoning limitations; the no-talk baseline establishes that communication is necessary; and a full-transparency intervention establishes that information exchange alone is insufficient: the bottleneck lies in the interactive processes of joint plan formation, commitment, and execution that constitute dynamic grounding.

</details>


### [823] [MAGIC: Multi-Step Advantage-Gated Causal Influence for Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01805)
*Haohan Yu,Jinmiao Cong,Shengzhi Wang,Lu Wang,Chanjuan Liu*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new framework, MAGIC, for multi-agent reinforcement learning that uses causal interventions to improve agent coordination, achieving significant performance gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of designing effective learning signals for agent coordination in multi-agent reinforcement learning by quantifying long-term causal influences.

Method: Introducing the MAGIC framework, which uses multi-step causal interventions and mutual information to measure agent influence, combined with an advantage-based gating mechanism to guide rewarding and exploration.

Result: MAGIC demonstrates at least a 10.1% performance improvement over state-of-the-art methods across various MARL benchmarks, including MPE and SMAC/SMACv2.

Conclusion: MAGIC effectively enhances coordination among agents in MARL scenarios by leveraging causal relationships, offering measurable advances over existing approaches.

Abstract: A key challenge in multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) lies in designing learning signals that effectively promote coordination among agents. Designing such signals necessitates the ability to quantify the true, long-term causal influence between agents. To address this, we introduce Multi-step Advantage-Gated Interventional Causal MARL (MAGIC), a framework that extracts multi-step causal influences between agents and selectively converts them into intrinsic rewards. MAGIC uses causal intervention with conditional mutual information to quantify long-horizon agent influence, and introduces an advantage-based gating mechanism to ensure exploration is directed toward beneficial, goal-aligned behaviors. Experiments across multiple standard MARL benchmarks and task families, including MPE and SMAC/SMACv2, demonstrate that MAGIC outperforms state-of-the-art methods by a significant margin, achieving an improvement of at least 10.1% in the main evaluation metric.

</details>


### [824] [Quality-Aware Exploration Budget Allocation for Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01865)
*Dahyun Oh,Minhyuk Yoon,H. Jin Kim*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper proposes a framework to improve multi-agent reinforcement learning by optimizing exploration intensity and allocation based on intrinsic reward signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Effective multi-agent collaboration is hindered by the difficulty of discovering rare coordination strategies in large state-action spaces.

Method: The framework combines a return-conditioned schedule for global exploration intensity and a per-agent metric to allocate exploration budgets based on the reliability of their intrinsic reward signals.

Result: The proposed method achieves superior performance across seven cooperative multi-agent benchmarks, showcasing its ability to enhance exploration and coordination.

Conclusion: Adapting exploration intensity globally and allocating intrinsic exploration budgets optimally across agents improves performance in cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) requires agents to discover joint strategies in a combinatorially large state-action space, yet effective coordination configurations are exceedingly rare. Intrinsic motivation, which augments task rewards with novelty bonuses, is a popular approach for driving exploration, but its effectiveness hinges on the exploration intensity $β$, where too large a value overwhelms the task signal and causes coordination collapse, while too small a value prevents discovery of rare strategies. We address two complementary challenges: adapting $β$ globally over training, and allocating the exploration budget across agents whose intrinsic reward signals vary in reliability. Our framework combines a return-conditioned sigmoid schedule (RCB) for global intensity control with a per-agent Reward Signal Quality (RSQ) metric that concentrates the exploration budget on agents with reliable signals. The core insight is that agents receiving noisy intrinsic rewards should explore less aggressively, and this allocation can be determined automatically from signal-to-noise statistics. Successor Distance (SD), a quasimetric intrinsic reward, naturally produces distinguishable per-agent signal quality, completing the framework with convergence and ordering preservation guarantees. On seven cooperative benchmarks (MPE, SMAX, MABrax), our method achieves top-tier returns across all environments.

</details>


### [825] [Coopetition-Gym v1: A Formally Grounded Platform for Mixed-Motive Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning under Strategic Coopetition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02063)
*Vik Pant,Eric Yu*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Coopetition-Gym v1 is a novel benchmark for mixed-motive multi-agent reinforcement learning with 20 environments based on four foundational concepts. It features diverse reward modes, validated historical simulations, and robust tooling for research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a standardized platform for studying mixed-motive multi-agent reinforcement learning with a focus on strategic 'coopetition' (cooperation and competition).

Method: Developed 20 environments across four mechanism classes, implemented with a parameterized reward system, and calibrated using historical coopetitive data. Provided interfaces and reference algorithms.

Result: The platform accurately reproduces outcomes of historical coopetitive cases (98.3% to 87.3% accuracy) and includes extensive training and behavioral audit corpora for researchers.

Conclusion: Coopetition-Gym v1 establishes a comprehensive, validated benchmark for studying strategies and outcomes in mixed-motive contexts, offering tools for better understanding and algorithm design.

Abstract: We present Coopetition-Gym v1, a benchmark platform for mixed-motive multi-agent reinforcement learning under strategic coopetition. The platform comprises twenty environments organized into four mechanism classes that correspond to four foundational technical reports: interdependence and complementarity (arXiv:2510.18802), trust and reputation dynamics (arXiv:2510.24909), collective action and loyalty (arXiv:2601.16237), and sequential interaction and reciprocity (arXiv:2604.01240). Each environment carries a closed-form payoff structure and a calibrated interdependence matrix derived from the corresponding report.
  Every environment exposes a parameterized reward layer configurable across three structurally distinct modes (private, integrated, cooperative). This separation of payoff from reward enables reward-type ablation, the platform's principal methodological apparatus. Four of the twenty environments are calibrated against historically documented coopetitive relationships and reproduce their outcomes at 98.3, 81.7, 86.7, and 87.3 percent on the validation rubric (Samsung-Sony LCD, Renault-Nissan Alliance, Apache HTTP Server, Apple iOS App Store).
  The platform exposes Gymnasium, PettingZoo Parallel, and PettingZoo AEC interfaces and ships 126 reference algorithms: 16 learning algorithms, 7 game-theoretic oracles, 2 heuristic baselines, and 101 constant-action policies. A reference experimental study trained the 16 learning algorithms on every environment under every reward configuration with seven random seeds, producing a 25,708-run training corpus and a 1,116-run behavioral audit corpus, both released under CC-BY-4.0 with Croissant 1.0 metadata. Coopetition-Gym v1 is the first platform to combine continuous-action mixed-motive environments, parameterized reward mutuality, calibrated interdependence coefficients, game-theoretic oracle baselines, and validated case studies.

</details>


### [826] [LLM-enabled Social Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02335)
*Önder Gürcan,Moharram Challenger*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper addresses the limitations of current language models in generating socially intelligible behavior, proposing persona-based role definitions as a foundation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of grounding in roles, norms, and contextual constraints in current language models, which limits their social participation.

Method: The authors propose grounding social agents in persona-based role definitions and suggest research directions for representation, hybrid control, and evaluation.

Result: The paper establishes the concept of persona-based role definitions as essential for enabling social behavior in language models.

Conclusion: Persona-based role definitions provide a necessary framework for LLM-enabled social agents to transform language competence into meaningful social interaction.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have transformed agent-agent and human-agent interaction by enabling software, physical, and simulation agents to communicate and deliberate through natural language. Yet fluent language use does not by itself yield socially intelligible behaviour. Most current systems remain weakly grounded in roles, norms, intentions, and contextual constraints, limiting their capacity for meaningful participation in social environments. This paper develops a conceptual baseline for LLM-enabled social agents by arguing that they should be grounded in role definitions operationalized through persona descriptions. On this basis, we outline research directions for representation, hybrid control, and evaluation. The paper concludes that persona-based role definitions are a necessary foundation for turning language competence into social behaviour.

</details>


### [827] [When Stress Becomes Signal: Detecting Antifragility-Compatible Regimes in Multi-Agent LLM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02463)
*Jose Manuel de la Chica,Juan Manuel Vera,Jairo Rodríguez*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper investigates antifragility in multi-agent LLM systems using a novel framework called CAFE, analyzing semantic stress exposure and its potential for improved learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to go beyond robustness evaluations in multi-agent LLM systems to explore whether semantic stress can reveal structured variations that might support antifragile learning.

Method: The paper introduces CAFE, a framework that compares expected and observed distributions of semantic stress using a distributional Jensen Gap under a convex stress potential to identify antifragility-compatible regimes.

Result: Using CAFE, the study finds that while semantic stress reduces system performance, all tested architectures show positive distributional Jensen Gaps, indicating learnable stress structures are exposed.

Conclusion: CAFE is a diagnostic tool, not an antifragile learner, but it identifies areas where antifragility learning could be beneficial by analyzing stress exposure in multi-agent systems.

Abstract: Multi-agent LLM systems are increasingly used to solve complex tasks through decomposition, debate, specialization, and ensemble reasoning. However, these systems are usually evaluated in terms of robustness: whether performance is preserved under perturbation. This paper studies a different question: whether semantic stress exposes structured variation that could support future antifragile learning. We introduce CAFE, a statistical framework for detecting antifragility-compatible regimes in multi-agent architectures. CAFE models a controlled expected distribution of semantic stressors, reconstructs an architecture-specific observed effective stress distribution from multi-dimensional judge signals, and compares both distributions using a distributional Jensen Gap under a convex stress potential. A positive gap does not imply immediate performance improvement; instead, it indicates a convex-expansive deformation of the observed stress distribution, suggesting that the architecture exposes learnable stress structure. We evaluate CAFE on a banking-risk analysis benchmark with five multi-agent architectures: flat, hierarchical, debate, meta-adaptive, and ensemble. Across all architectures, semantic stress reduces average judged quality by roughly one third. Yet all architectures exhibit positive distributional Jensen Gaps with bootstrap confidence intervals above zero. These results show that immediate quality degradation can coexist with statistically detectable antifragility-compatible stress geometry. CAFE is therefore not an antifragile learner itself, but a measurement layer for identifying when and where antifragility learning may be worth applying.

</details>


<div id='hep-ex'></div>

# hep-ex [[Back]](#toc)

### [828] [HepScript: A Dual-Use DSL for Human-AI Collaborative Data Analysis Workflows in High-Energy Physics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01423)
*Junkun Jiao,Tong Liu,Ke Li,Weimin Song,Yipu Liao,Bolun Zhang,Beijiang Liu,Chang-Zheng Yuan,Yue Sun*

Main category: hep-ex

TL;DR: The paper proposes HepScript, a Domain-Specific Language (DSL) for High-Energy Physics (HEP) workflows to facilitate AI-driven automation while reducing complexity and effort.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The complexity and scale of HEP data analysis necessitate automation tools that are both efficient and capable of handling domain-specific requirements.

Method: HepScript is used as a shared formal interface with constrained syntax, simplifying high-level analysis logic for HEP workflows and enabling AI agents to generate executable specifications autonomously.

Result: HepScript reduced human-written code requirements by 93% and enabled AI agents to achieve a 95% success rate in generating executable analysis code from academic literature.

Conclusion: The study highlights HepScript as an effective bridge for human-AI collaboration, solving automation challenges in complex HEP workflows with scalable results.

Abstract: The escalating data scale in High-Energy Physics (HEP) fuels a growing aspiration for higher analytical efficiency. While Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a path toward automation via agentic AI, they struggle with complex scientific workflows that require deep domain knowledge and are tightly coupled to experiment-specific codebases. To address this, we introduce a methodology centered on HepScript, a dual-use Domain-Specific Language (DSL) for HEP data analysis workflows. HepScript serves as a shared formal interface, abstracting HEP analysis logic into a constrained syntax that is both intuitive for human experts and reliably generable by AI agents. First developed for the Beijing Spectrometer III (BESIII) experiment, HepScript hides the complexity of the underlying software stack, translating high-level analysis intent into low-level, production-ready code. In our case studies, this abstraction reduces the required human-written code by 93\%. Crucially, HepScript's constrained grammar defines a tractable action space, enabling AI agents to autonomously generate executable specifications for core analysis stages directly from published literature with a 95\% success rate. Our work demonstrates a scalable pathway toward human-AI collaborative systems, where a formally specified DSL acts as an unambiguous translation layer between human expertise, AI automation, and production environment, rendering previously intractable automation problems solvable.

</details>


### [829] [Data-Driven, Geometry-Aware Optimal-Transport Calibration of Flavor Tagger](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01363)
*Yeonjoon Kim,Un-ki Yang*

Main category: hep-ex

TL;DR: Introduce a geometry-aware approach for flavor-tagger calibration using optimal transport on the probability simplex.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current flavor-tagging methods face limitations due to discrete calibrations which cause loss of information and performance restrictions in high-precision analyses.

Method: Formulate calibration as an optimal transport problem in the isometric log-ratio space, employing Aitchison geometry and extracting flavor-conditional distributions using an expectation-maximization (EM) technique.

Result: Simulation-based validation shows improved closure performance in both dedicated control regions and independent validation mixtures.
