Conclusion: The proposed framework enhances the precision of flavor tagging by leveraging continuous, geometry-aware calibrations, overcoming the limitations of traditional discrete methods.

Abstract: Flavor-tagging calibrations are often provided either as scale factors measured at a finite set of working points or as binned corrections to a chosen one-dimensional discriminant. However, this approach falls short of providing continuous, event-level calibration across the full multicomponent outputs of modern taggers. This limitation leads to information loss in analyses that demand high-performance flavor tagging, restricting analyses to a limited set of predefined variables.
  In this work, we propose a geometry-aware framework that formulates flavor-tagger calibration as an optimal transport problem on the probability simplex. The transport maps are parameterized and trained in the isometric log-ratio coordinate system. Because the quadratic Euclidean cost of Brenier transport in this coordinate system is equivalent to the Aitchison distance on the simplex, the learned map induces a minimal deformation under the Aitchison geometry. Furthermore, we extract flavor-conditional target distributions directly from control-region data using an expectation-maximization (EM) technique that simultaneously fits multiple control regions, models each flavor component with a normalizing flow, and estimates the regional mixture fractions. The extracted targets are subsequently used to learn flavor-factorized transport maps. Because the joint estimation of mixture fractions and flexible component densities admits weakly constrained directions, we further introduce a linearized feedback-operator analysis that propagates the fitted composition covariance into the extracted component densities, separating data-constrained modes from those dominated by the composition prior. The simulation-based closure study demonstrates improved closure in dedicated control regions and in independent validation mixtures.

</details>


### [830] [From Experimental Limits to Physical Insight: A Retrieval-Augmented Multi-Agent Framework for Interpreting Searches Beyond the Standard Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02491)
*Altan Cakir,Ayca Yerlikaya*

Main category: hep-ex

TL;DR: HEP-CoPilot is an AI framework that simplifies the exploration and interpretation of high-energy physics literature by integrating diverse data sources such as texts, numerical datasets, and graphical plots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-energy physicists face challenges in manually integrating diverse data from rapidly growing literature for studying physics beyond the Standard Model.

Method: The paper introduces a retrieval-augmented multi-agent AI framework called HEP-CoPilot, which unifies textual publications, datasets, and reconstructed plots into a multimodal system for evidence-based reasoning.

Result: HEP-CoPilot was tested on CMS searches and demonstrated capabilities such as retrieving relevant data, reconstructing exclusion limits, and enabling cross-paper comparisons.

Conclusion: HEP-CoPilot highlights the potential of retrieval-augmented AI systems to accelerate scientific workflows in particle physics by reducing manual efforts and structuring heterogeneous evidence.

Abstract: Modern searches for physics beyond the Standard Model produce rapidly expanding literature containing heterogeneous information, including textual analyses, numerical datasets, and graphical exclusion limits. Integrating these distributed sources remains a time-consuming and manual process for physicists.
  We present HEP-CoPilot, a retrieval-augmented multi-agent AI framework for the exploration and interpretation of high-energy physics literature. The system unifies textual information from publications, structured experimental data from HEPData, and reconstructed physics plots within a multimodal retrieval and reasoning architecture. By combining retrieval-augmented language models with coordinated agent workflows, it enables evidence-grounded reasoning over experimental analyses and structured interpretation of collider results.
  We evaluate the framework on recent CMS searches for physics beyond the Standard Model. Case studies show that HEP-CoPilot can retrieve relevant measurements, reconstruct exclusion limits directly from HEPData records, and perform cross-paper comparisons of experimental constraints. This enables consistent, physics-aware comparison across analyses without manual data integration.
  These results demonstrate that retrieval-augmented AI systems can function as scientific co-pilots for particle physics, facilitating navigation of complex literature, structuring heterogeneous evidence, and accelerating the interpretation pipeline for new physics searches.

</details>


<div id='hep-th'></div>

# hep-th [[Back]](#toc)

### [831] [Reconstructing conformal field theoretical compositions with Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01072)
*Haotian Cao,Garrett Merz,Kyle Cranmer,Gary Shiu*

Main category: hep-th

TL;DR: The paper studies using transformers for reconstructing the composition of two-dimensional RCFT tensor products, achieving 98% accuracy and demonstrating generalization to out-of-domain cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the combinatorial complexity of identifying constituent theories of RCFT tensor products, which has implications for understanding bulk reconstruction in AdS/CFT.

Method: Transformers were utilized to analyze low-energy spectra and identify constituent RCFT theories, training on Wess-Zumino-Witten models and extending with out-of-domain examples.

Result: The approach attained 98% accuracy in reconstructing tensor products of RCFTs and generalized effectively to larger central charges and unseen RCFT classes.

Conclusion: Transformers provide a powerful tool for understanding the structure of RCFTs and offer potential applications in bulk reconstruction in AdS/CFT contexts.

Abstract: We study the use of transformers to reconstruct the compositions of tensor products of two-dimensional rational conformal field theories (RCFTs) based on their low-energy spectra. The task is challenging due to its combinatorial nature. The constituent theories are characterized by their central charges and affine Lie algebra labels. We achieve 98% accuracy in recovering the constituents of tensor products theories constructed from Wess-Zumino-Witten models. We further demonstrate that our method generalizes to CFTs with larger central charge and unseen classes of RCFTs by adding a small number of out-of-domain examples. Our results show that transformers are effective at this task and point towards a new tool for bulk reconstruction in AdS/CFT.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [832] [SwiftChannel: Algorithm-Hardware Co-Design for Deep Learning-Based 5G Channel Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01931)
*Shengzhe Lyu,Yuhan She,Di Duan,Tao Ni,Yu Hin Chan,Chengwen Luo,Ray C. C. Cheung,Weitao Xu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper introduces SwiftChannel, a framework using hardware-friendly deep learning and accelerator design to address challenges in channel estimation for 5G networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate channel estimation is critical for optimizing 5G MIMO and mmWave communications, but achieving this under strict latency requirements on resource-limited hardware remains a challenge.

Method: The framework employs a CNN with attention mechanisms for accurate reconstruction of channel matrices from LS estimates, combined with model compression techniques like distillation and quantization, and an FPGA-based hardware implementation for efficient processing.

Result: SwiftChannel achieves sub-millisecond latency, a 24x speed-up, and over 33x energy efficiency improvement on FPGA compared to GPUs, as well as robustness across various conditions and unseen channel profiles.

Conclusion: SwiftChannel unifies algorithmic and hardware-aware innovations, offering a scalable and efficient solution for 5G MIMO channel estimation that outperforms existing methods.

Abstract: Channel estimation is crucial in 5G communication networks for optimizing transmission parameters and ensuring reliable, high-speed communication. However, the use of multiple-input and multiple-output (MIMO) and millimeter-wave (mmWave) in 5G networks presents challenges in achieving accurate estimation under strict latency requirements on resource-limited hardware platforms. To address these challenges, we propose SwiftChannel, an algorithm-hardware co-design framework that integrates a hardware-friendly deep learning-based channel estimator with a dedicated accelerator. Our approach employs a convolutional neural network enhanced with a parameter-free attention mechanism, which effectively reconstructs full-resolution spatial-frequency domain channel matrices from low-resolution least squares (LS) estimates. We further develop a multi-stage model compression pipeline combining knowledge distillation, convolution re-parameterization, and quantization-aware training, resulting in substantial model size reduction with negligible accuracy loss. The hardware accelerator, implementing the compressed model and the LS estimator on FPGA platforms using High-level Synthesis (HLS), features a fine-grained pipeline architecture and optimized dataflow strategies. Tested on a Zynq UltraScale+ RFSoC, the accelerator achieves sub-millisecond latency, providing up to 24x speed-up and over 33x improvement in energy efficiency compared to GPU-based solutions. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that the proposed design generalizes not only across various noise levels and user mobilities, but also to a variety of unseen channel profiles, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. By unifying algorithmic innovation with hardware-aware design, our work presents a future-proof channel estimation solution for 5G MIMO systems.

</details>


### [833] [Evolving Token Communication with Parametric Memory Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01869)
*Weixuan Chen,Qianqian Yang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes an evolving semantic token communication system using a parametric memory network to efficiently handle wireless transmission over MIMO fading channels. The system transmits truncated tokens, reconstructing the missing information at the receiver to reduce overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the communication overhead in semantic token-based transmission by efficiently handling missing token information while maintaining communication performance.

Method: The method involves transmitting truncated tokens and reconstructing them at the receiver using a parametric memory network. A fine-tuned GPT-2-based recovery module is used, supported by a codebook and kNN-based teacher distributions.

Result: The approach achieves up to 1.09 dB PSNR improvement compared to benchmarks under varying channel conditions and channel bandwidth ratios.

Conclusion: The proposed system effectively reduces transmission costs and adapts to distribution shifts, providing consistent improvements over existing methods while preserving communication efficiency.

Abstract: Token communication has emerged as a promising framework for efficient wireless transmission by representing source data as compact semantic tokens. However, transmitting full semantic tokens still incurs considerable communication overhead. In this paper, we propose an evolving semantic token communication system with a parametric memory network over MIMO fading channels. Specifically, only an equal-length prefix of each semantic token is transmitted, which reduces transmission cost while preserving a consistent token structure for receiver-side recovery. At the receiver, a parametric memory network is introduced to reconstruct the missing suffix information from the received token prefixes, where semantic memory is stored implicitly in the network parameters. To realize this design, full semantic tokens are first organized into a codebook, and truncated tokens are paired with the codeword labels of their corresponding full tokens. Based on these token-label pairs, kNN-based teacher distributions are constructed to fine-tune a pretrained GPT-2-based recovery module, which learns to infer the codeword distribution of each incomplete token and recover the corresponding complete semantic token. In addition, an online evolution strategy is developed to periodically update the parametric memory network and the entire system using newly observed test samples, thereby improving adaptability under distribution shifts. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms the existing evolving memory benchmark under different channel conditions and channel bandwidth ratios, with up to 1.09 dB PSNR improvement.

</details>


### [834] [Remote Action Generation: Remote Control with Minimal Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01833)
*Szymon Kobus,Deniz Gündüz*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper introduces "Guided Remote Action Sampling Policy (GRASP)," a method for efficient remote control communication by leveraging minimal information transmission to guide actors' actions, achieving significant data reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle the problem of communication constraints in remote control, particularly in large or continuous action spaces, where transmitting full action specifications is challenging.

Method: The framework uses remote generation, where the controller sends minimal guidance information. Actors use this guidance to locally sample actions, facilitated by importance sampling. Additionally, actors learn the controller's policy over time using supervised learning, further reducing communication needs.

Result: The proposed GRASP approach reduces communication by an average of 12-fold across experiments, with up to 50-fold reduction in continuous action spaces, and 41-fold reduction compared to reward transmission.

Conclusion: GRASP effectively addresses communication constraints in remote control setups, offering efficient and scalable solutions for minimal information transmission by combining guided sampling and policy learning.

Abstract: We address the challenge of remote control where one or more actors, lacking direct reward access, are steered by a controller over a communication-constrained channel. The controller learns an optimal policy from observed rewards and communicates action guidance to the actors, which becomes demanding for large or continuous action spaces. To achieve rate-efficient communication throughout this interactive learning and control process, we introduce a novel framework leveraging remote generation. Instead of transmitting full action specifications, the controller sends minimal information, enabling the actors to locally generate actions by sampling from the controller's evolving target policy. This guided sampling is facilitated by an importance sampling approach. Concurrently, the actors use the received guidance as supervised learning data to learn the controller's policy. This actor-side learning improves their local sampling capabilities, progressively reducing future communication needs. Our solution, Guided Remote Action Sampling Policy (GRASP), demonstrates significant communication reduction, achieving an average 12-fold data reduction across all experiments (50-fold for continuous action spaces) compared to direct action transmission, and a 41-fold reduction compared to reward transmission.

</details>


### [835] [Real-Time Text Transmission via LLM-Based Entropy Coding over Fixed-Rate Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01991)
*Vishnu Teja Kunde,Jean-Francois Chamberland,Krishna R. Narayanan,Jamison Ebert*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper explores the balance between data compression and transmission delays using predictive language models for real-time text streaming. It evaluates several coding schemes for their performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the tradeoff between compression efficiency and delay when using causal language models for real-time text transmission across fixed-rate channels.

Method: Several coding schemes (Shannon, Huffman, arithmetic coding, rANS, gzip) are analyzed, factoring in algorithmic and computational delays. The study uses two language models (GPT-2, Llama~3.2) for empirical validation.

Result: Huffman coding is suitable for over-provisioned channels due to zero algorithmic delay, while arithmetic coding achieves better compression with higher decodability delay. Scaling models reduces bits per character by approximately 38%.

Conclusion: The compression-delay tradeoff hinges on hardware provisioning and coder selection. Larger models improve compression, altering the optimal choice of coder.

Abstract: Learning, prediction, and compression are intimately connected: a model that accurately predicts the next symbol in a sequence can be coupled with a source coder to compress that sequence near its information-theoretic limit. When tokenized characters arriving at a fixed reading pace are encoded into variable-length codewords and streamed over a fixed-rate channel, a queue forms whose per-token delay depends on the mean and variance of the bit lengths and on the coder's algorithmic latency. This paper investigates the compression--delay tradeoff that arises when a causal language model serves as the sequential predictor within a predict-then-code architecture for real-time text transmission. Several coding schemes are compared: Shannon (ideal), Huffman, arithmetic coding, rANS at various block sizes, and gzip. The analysis separates algorithmic delay, inherent to the coder, from computational delay, which shrinks as hardware improves. Huffman is the practical choice for over-provisioned channels, with zero algorithmic delay and modest compression overhead. Arithmetic coding achieves near-optimal compression at the cost of decodability delay. Findings are validated across two scales: GPT-2 (124M) and Llama~3.2 (3B), a twenty-five-fold parameter range. This scaling yields an approximately 38\% reduction in bits per character, effectively over-provisioning the channel and thereby changing which coder is optimal.

</details>


### [836] [Optimization of CV-QKD Under Practical Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02045)
*Svitlana Matsenko,Amirhossein Ghazisaeidi,Marcin Jarzyna,Konrad Banaszek,Darko Zibar*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper leverages reinforcement learning to optimize hardware constraints and enhance performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address practical problems caused by limited hardware capabilities like FIR filter taps, mean photon numbers, and DAC/ADC resolution.

Method: Reinforcement learning is applied for performance optimization under constrained hardware conditions.

Result: The approach achieves significant performance improvements within realistic operational constraints.

Conclusion: The use of reinforcement learning effectively improves system performance despite hardware limitations, showcasing its practicality.

Abstract: Using reinforcement learning, we optimize for practical hardware constraints, including limited FIR filter taps at the transmitter and receiver, mean photon number and finite DAC/ADC resolution. Under these realistic conditions, the proposed approach achieves significant performance improvements.

</details>


### [837] [Dueling DDQN-Based Adaptive Multi-Objective Handover Optimization for LEO Satellite Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02416)
*Po-Heng Chou,Chiapin Wang,Chung-Chi Huang,Kuan-Hao Chen*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper introduces a DDQN-based adaptive multi-objective handover framework for LEO satellite networks, focusing on optimizing throughput, reducing blocking probability, and minimizing switching costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of dynamic trade-offs in handover decisions within LEO satellite networks under time-varying conditions.

Method: Utilizes a dueling double deep Q-network (DDQN) for adaptive learning and decision-making, optimizing multiple handover objectives.

Result: Simulation results show improved performance with up to 10.3% higher throughput and near-zero blocking probability compared to traditional methods.

Conclusion: The framework achieves superior adaptability and optimization over conventional baselines for LEO satellite network handovers.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a dueling double deep Q-network (DDQN)-based adaptive multi-objective handover framework for LEO satellite networks. The proposed method enables dynamic trade-off learning among throughput, blocking probability, and switching cost under time-varying network conditions. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently outperforms conventional baselines, achieving up to 10.3% throughput improvement and near-zero blocking under typical operating conditions.

</details>


<div id='cs.DL'></div>

# cs.DL [[Back]](#toc)

### [838] [ARA: Agentic Reproducibility Assessment For Scalable Support Of Scientific Peer-Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02651)
*Kevin Riehl,Andres L. Marin,Nikofors Zacharof,Fan Wu,Patrick Langer,Robert Jakob,Anastasios Kouvelas,Georgios Fontaras,Michail A. Makridis*

Main category: cs.DL

TL;DR: Agentic Reproducibility Assessment (ARA) proposes a structured method to evaluate reproducibility of scientific work using workflow graphs and scoring systems. It demonstrated superior accuracy on key benchmarks compared to prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific peer review struggles with evaluating reproducibility in complex research, necessitating tools to formalize and enhance assessment processes.

Method: ARA extracts workflow graphs from scientific documents, linking methods, experiments, and results, then scores their reconstructability using both structural and content-based measures.

Result: On tests with 213 ReScience C articles, ARA achieved ~61% accuracy and outperformed existing methods on benchmarks like ReproBench and GoldStandardDB.

Conclusion: ARA shows promise in complementing human peer reviews, improving assessment scalability, and standardizing reproducibility evaluations. Resources are publicly available for broader usage.

Abstract: Scientific peer review increasingly struggles to assess reproducibility at the scale and complexity of modern research output. Evaluating reproducibility requires reconstructing experimental dependencies, methodological choices, data flows, and result-generating procedures, which often exceeds what human reviewers can provide. Agentic Reproducibility Assessment (ARA) formalizes reproducibility assessment as a structured reasoning task over scientific documents. Given a paper, ARA extracts a directed workflow graph linking sources, methods, experiments, and outputs, then evaluates its reconstructability using structural and content-based scores for reproducibility assessments. Experiments on 213 ReScience C articles - the largest cross-domain benchmark of human-validated computational reproducibility studies considered to date - demonstrate ARA's generalizability and consistent workflow reconstruction and assessment across LLMs, model temperatures, and scientific domains. ARA achieves ~61% accuracy on three benchmarks, and the highest accuracy reported on ReproBench (60.71% vs. 36.84%) and GoldStandardDB (61.68% vs. 43.56%), highlighting its potential to complement human review at scale and enabling next-generation peer review. Code and Data available: https://github.com/AndresLaverdeMarin/agentic_reproducibility_assessment.

</details>


<div id='physics.comp-ph'></div>

# physics.comp-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [839] [Multi-fidelity surrogates for mechanics of composites: from co-kriging to multi-fidelity neural networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02871)
*Haizhou Wen,Elham Kiyani,Gang Li,Srikanth Pilla,George Em Karniadakis,Zhen Li*

Main category: physics.comp-ph

TL;DR: The paper reviews multi-fidelity surrogate modeling techniques for composites to address challenges in predictive modeling due to complexity and high costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predictive modeling for composites is expensive due to their complex hierarchical and anisotropic properties, requiring large design spaces to be analyzed with high-fidelity simulations and experiments.

Method: The study organizes multi-fidelity surrogate modeling approaches, focusing on Gaussian-process-based methods and multi-fidelity neural networks, detailing their application in predicting and optimizing composite behaviors.

Result: Applications to composites demonstrate how these models assist in rapid material design exploration, parameter optimization, and integrating workflows to manage heterogeneous data.

Conclusion: Multi-fidelity surrogates are promising for addressing modeling challenges in composites, though unresolved issues such as fidelity gaps and mismatches hinder their full utility.

Abstract: Composite materials exhibit strongly hierarchical and anisotropic properties governed by coupled mechanisms spanning constituents, plies, laminates, structures, and manufacturing history. This intrinsic complexity makes predictive modeling of composites expensive, because repeated experiments and high-fidelity simulations are needed to cover large design spaces of material, structure, and manufacturing. Multi-fidelity surrogate modeling addresses this challenge by combining abundant, less expensive data with limited high-accuracy data to recover reliable high-fidelity predictions. This review presents a structured overview of multi-fidelity modeling for composite mechanics, covering Gaussian-process or Kriging-based methods, including co-Kriging, coregionalization models, autoregressive formulations, nonlinear autoregressive Gaussian processes, multi-fidelity deep Gaussian processes, and multi-fidelity neural networks. Their distinctions are examined in terms of cross-fidelity correlation, discrepancy representation, uncertainty quantification, and scalability. Selected examples of their applications to composites are introduced according to the roles that multi-fidelity surrogates play in engineering problems, including forward prediction for rapid exploration of material design spaces, inverse optimization for composite parameter identification and design search under limited high-fidelity access, and workflow integration, where heterogeneous data sources, constraints, and validation requirements determine model utility. Open question discussions highlight recurring challenges specific to composites, such as regime-dependent fidelity gaps associated with nonlinear damage and manufacturing history, mismatches between simulations and experiments, and uncertainty propagation across multi-fidelity models.

</details>


<div id='math.PR'></div>

# math.PR [[Back]](#toc)

### [840] [Universality in Deep Neural Networks: An approach via the Lindeberg exchange principle](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02771)
*Filippo Giovagnini,Sotirios Kotitsas,Marco Romito*

Main category: math.PR

TL;DR: This paper explores the infinite-width limit of deep neural networks and develops a quantitative bound on its $2$-Wasserstein distance to the Gaussian limit.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To better understand the behavior of deep neural networks in their infinite-width limit and provide quantitative tools for studying their approximation to Gaussian models.

Method: Employs a Lindeberg principle to iteratively replace weights in each layer of a deep neural network with Gaussian random variables, under regularity assumptions on activation functions.

Result: Derived general bounds on the $2$-Wasserstein distance between finite networks and their Gaussian limit, along with a theoretical framework employing the Lindeberg principle.

Conclusion: The paper provides foundational insights and quantitative bounds for analyzing deep neural networks in the infinite-width regime, advancing theoretical understanding in this domain.

Abstract: We consider the infinite-width limit of a fully connected deep neural network with general weights, and we prove quantitative general bounds on the $2$-Wasserstein distance between the network and its infinite-width Gaussian limit, under appropriate regularity assumptions on the activation function. Our main tool is a Lindeberg principle for Deep Neural Networks, which we use to successively replace the weights on each layer by Gaussian random variables.

</details>


<div id='cs.NI'></div>

# cs.NI [[Back]](#toc)

### [841] [A Protocol-Independent Transport Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02210)
*Kimiya Mohammadtaheri,David Gao,Samuel Zhang,Matthew Chen,Eric Su,Pengyu Ji,Saad Syed,Chris Neely,Mario Baldi,Nachiket Kapre,Mina Tahmasbi Arashloo*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: PITA introduces a transport architecture supporting protocol-independent, programmable data paths with high performance and flexibility, overcoming challenges in fixed protocol logic in NIC hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern workloads demand high-performance transport layers integrated into NIC hardware, complicating the deployment of new or evolving transport protocols due to fixed or protocol-specific logic.

Method: PITA creates a protocol-agnostic architecture structured around event, state, and instruction abstractions, redesigning core components like scheduling and packet handling to support diverse protocols.

Result: PITA reliably implements distinct behaviors of transport protocols such as TCP and RoCE on the same data path, and demonstrates high performance with minimal hardware costs using FPGA synthesis.

Conclusion: PITA successfully achieves flexibility and programmability for modern transport protocols, sustaining performance demands at 250MHz and providing a viable pathway for newer protocol developments.

Abstract: The network transport layer is increasingly implemented in the NIC hardware to meet the performance demands of modern workloads, but this has made it difficult to evolve or deploy new transport protocols. Existing approaches either fix protocol logic in the data-path or build protocol-specific assumptions into the architecture that limit the range of protocols that can be supported on a single hardware substrate.
  We present PITA, a protocol-independent transport architecture that enables full data-path programmability while sustaining line-rate performance. PITA eliminates protocol-specific assumptions by structuring the data-path around a uniform abstraction over events, state, and instructions, and rethinks core components, including scheduling, packet generation, and data reassembly, to operate on this abstraction. We evaluate PITA along key dimensions reflecting the goals of its protocol-agnostic datapath design. Specifically, we show that PITA supports diverse protocol semantics by showing it can implement TCP and \roce on the same data path and preserve their distinct end-to-end behavior. Through targeted microbenchmarks and synthesis on Alveo U250 cards, we show that PITA's redesigned components sustain high performance under demanding conditions, with modest hardware overhead and meeting timing at 250MHz.

</details>


### [842] [Adaptive Alarm Threshold Prediction in 4G Mobile Networks: A Percentile-Guided Deep Learning Framework with Interpretable Outputs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00838)
*Ayon Roy,Sadman Sharif,Shiva Prasad Sarkar*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a machine learning framework to dynamically and accurately predict alarm thresholds in mobile telecom systems, improving on fixed manual limits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current fixed, manual alarm thresholds in mobile networks are ineffective. They fail to adapt to varying traffic levels and network conditions, causing unnecessary issues or missed faults.

Method: A machine learning framework predicts four specific alarm thresholds based on live network data using models like Gradient Boosted Trees, CNN-BiLSTM with attention, PCTN, and iTransformer. Percentile-guided label derivation is used due to lack of ground truth labels.

Result: The proposed PCTN model outperformed alternatives in three out of four targets, offering accurate and interpretable thresholds while using fewer parameters. The results were statistically significant.

Conclusion: The dynamic threshold framework enhances alarm management, adjusts automatically to real-time network changes, and allows operators to adjust policies manually as needed without retraining.

Abstract: In mobile telecommunications, alarms act as early warning signals. They are triggered when a cell, the basic unit of radio coverage, shuts down or behaves abnormally. This signals a degradation in service quality, which directly affects the customer experience. To fix the issue, operators rely on preset thresholds to decide when an engineer should be sent out. In practice, these thresholds are set manually and remain fixed regardless of the time of day, traffic levels, or overall network conditions. This often leads to serious faults slipping through during busy hours, while minor issues can cause unnecessary callouts when the network is quiet. This paper presents a machine learning framework that automatically predicts four alarm thresholds, audit window duration, inactive time limit, total fluctuation count, and per hour fluctuation limit, from live network behavior. Since no ground truth labels exist for thresholds, we introduce a percentile guided label derivation strategy and evaluate four models on an anonymized dataset of 10,648 cells across three vendors and nine regions from a real 4G network, comprising a Gradient Boosted Trees baseline, a CNN-BiLSTM with attention, the proposed PCTN, and an iTransformer. PCTN performs the best overall with respect to three of the four targets, outperforming a state-of-the-art iTransformer while using 83 percent fewer parameters. Its mixed output heads and dynamic alpha mechanism produce thresholds that are both accurate and interpretable, allowing operators to inspect and adjust the learned policy without retraining. All comparisons are statistically significant at p < 0.001. The framework undergoes daily retraining using new data, which enables the thresholds to constantly adjust to changes in the network.

</details>


### [843] [6G Needs Agents: Toward Agentic AI-Native Networks for Autonomous Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01546)
*Mohamed Amine Ferrag,Abderrahmane Lakas,Merouane Debbah*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: This paper advocates for using LLM-based agents in 6G networks, proposing a four-layer architecture for reasoning and optimization, and highlighting critical challenges and findings through empirical studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the limitations of existing AI approaches in 6G networks that rely on optimization without reasoning capabilities, by introducing a more intelligent and autonomous framework.

Method: The paper proposes a four-layer architecture for Agentic AI-Native 6G that integrates deterministic infrastructure, semantic intent abstraction, hierarchical reasoning, and a multi-agent system across devices, edges, and cores. It includes a proof-of-concept and empirical study using a specialized 6G benchmark.

Result: Results indicate a fundamental tradeoff between reasoning capabilities and system efficiency, showing the need for heterogeneous LLM agent deployment and system-level optimization rather than isolated model compression.

Conclusion: Agentic intelligence presents a promising direction for 6G architecture but achieving scalability, trustworthiness, and effective self-reasoning poses significant challenges requiring further research and optimization.

Abstract: Sixth-generation (6G) networks are increasingly envisioned as AI-native infrastructures integrating communication, sensing, and computing into a unified fabric. However, existing approaches remain largely optimization-centric, relying on closed-loop control with limited reasoning capability. In this paper, we argue for a paradigm shift toward Agentic AI-Native 6G, in which Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents operate as bounded, policy-governed reasoning entities within a semantic control plane layered above deterministic 3GPP infrastructure. We propose a four-layer architecture that integrates deterministic network infrastructure, semantic abstraction of intent and context, hierarchical reasoning, and a distributed multi-agent fabric spanning device, edge, and core domains. To assess feasibility, we develop a proof-of-concept agentic reasoning and orchestration framework and conduct an extensive empirical study using a domain-specific 6G benchmark under realistic deployment constraints. Our results reveal a fundamental tradeoff between reasoning capability and system efficiency, showing that no single model simultaneously satisfies latency, throughput, and accuracy requirements. Instead, heterogeneous deployment of LLM agents across the device--edge--core continuum is necessary to balance these constraints. We further demonstrate that quantization introduces non-uniform effects across models, reinforcing the need for system-level optimization rather than model-level compression alone. These findings establish agentic intelligence as a viable architectural direction for 6G and highlight key challenges in achieving scalable, trustworthy, and self-reasoning networks. All experimental results and evaluation scripts are publicly available to support reproducibility.

</details>


### [844] [Toward Resilient 5G Networks: Comparative Analysis of Federated and Centralized Learning for RF Jamming Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01705)
*Samhita Kuili,Mohammadreza Amini,Burak Kantarci*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: This paper presents a federated learning-based framework for jamming attack detection in 5G and beyond networks, achieving high accuracy and preserving user privacy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Jamming attacks threaten the security of 5G networks, and conventional detection methods compromise user privacy by requiring centralized data collection.

Method: The paper utilizes a federated learning framework with Federated Averaging (FedAvg) to train a 1D convolutional neural network (1DCNN) using over-the-air IQ samples from synchronization signal blocks, avoiding raw data sharing.

Result: The proposed framework achieved 97% accuracy and 97% F1-score, outperforming centralized models like MLP, 1DCNN, SVM, and logistic regression, while ensuring privacy.

Conclusion: The federated learning-based jamming detection framework is effective and privacy-preserving, making it suitable for enhancing the security of 5G and beyond networks.

Abstract: Jamming attacks are proliferating and pose a significant threat to the security of 5G and beyond networks. These attacks target 5G radio frequency (RF) domain and can disrupt the communication in wireless networks. While conventional machine learning and deep learning approaches demonstrate its potential for jamming detection, they typically require centralized data collection, compromising the privacy of user equipment (UEs). This work proposes a federated learning (FL)-based jamming detection framework that operates on over-the-air In-phase and Quadrature (IQ) samples extracted from Synchronization Signal Blocks (SSBs) in the RF domain. The framework enables collaborative model training across multiple UEs without sharing raw RF signal data. We adopt Federated Averaging (FedAvg) algorithm to train a 1D convolutional neural network (1DCNN) for effective detection of attacks. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed FL framework achieves 97% accuracy and 97% F1-score, outperforming centralized baselines including MLP, 1DCNN, SVM, and logistic regression, while preserving the data privacy of all participating UEs

</details>


### [845] [Spatial-Temporal Learning-Based Distributed Routing for Dynamic LEO Satellite Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02413)
*Po-Heng Chou,Chiapin Wang,Shou-Yu Chen,Hsiang-Ming Wang*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: The paper presents a distributed routing framework for LEO satellite networks using GAT, LSTM, and DQN, achieving superior performance and eco-efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address routing challenges in dynamic LEO satellite networks with changing topology and traffic patterns in a sustainable manner.

Method: The authors use a POMDP framework combining GAT, LSTM, and DQN-based architecture for distributed and adaptive routing.

Result: The proposed method outperforms traditional routing schemes in throughput, packet loss, queue length, end-to-end delay, and achieves up to 23.26% queue reduction.

Conclusion: The framework is both effective in improving network performance and efficient from a Green AI perspective, maintaining low computational overhead and carbon emissions.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a spatial-temporal learning-based distributed routing framework for dynamic Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite networks, where graph attention networks (GAT) and long short-term memory (LSTM) are integrated within a deep Q-network (DQN)-based architecture to enable distributed and adaptive routing decisions based on local observations. The routing problem is formulated as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) to address partial observability under dynamic topology and time-varying traffic. Simulation results show that the proposed method significantly outperforms conventional and learning-based routing schemes in terms of throughput, packet loss, queue length, and end-to-end delay, while achieving proactive congestion avoidance with up to 23.26% queue reduction. In addition, the proposed approach maintains low computational overhead with negligible carbon emissions, demonstrating its efficiency from a Green AI perspective.

</details>


### [846] [Beyond State Machines: Executing Network Procedures with Agentic Tool-Calling Sequences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02584)
*Purna Sai Garigipati,Onur Ayan,Kishor Chandra Joshi,Xueli An*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: The study explores how Large Language Model (LLM)-based network AI agents can handle multi-step tasks in mobile communication systems, analyzing their efficiency and reliability in executing network operations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The goal is to leverage agentic AI, using LLMs, to enable flexible, customized services and automate complex tasks in mobile communication systems for improved efficiency and decision-making.

Method: The study examines four approaches for executing network procedures with LLM-based agents, using a UE IP allocation task as a case study. It measures latency, correctness, and conducts stress tests on long procedures.

Result: Results highlight that relying on iterative agent-side reasoning increases latency and errors, while encapsulating the procedure within a tool reduces both. Advanced tool-calling capabilities sustain longer procedures but exhibit limits in execution reliability.

Conclusion: LLM agents can execute network procedures effectively but face challenges with reliability in longer workflows. Future efforts should address these execution limits to improve multi-step procedural capabilities.

Abstract: Agentic AI will be an essential enabling technology for designing future mobile communication systems, which could provide flexible and customized services, automate complex network operations, and drive autonomous decision-making across the network. This work studies how Large Language Model (LLM)-based network AI agents can be utilized to execute network procedures expressed as sequences of tool invocations. We investigate four approaches, which differ in how the agent obtains the procedure and in how execution is distributed between the agent and the underlying tools. We evaluated the latency and execution correctness across these approaches using a User Equipment (UE) IP allocation procedure as a case study. Furthermore, we conduct a stress test to examine how many sequential procedural steps an LLM agent can reliably execute before failure. Our results show that approaches relying on iterative agent-side reasoning incur higher latency and are more prone to execution errors, while approaches where the procedure is encapsulated within a single tool, which internally orchestrates the required steps by invoking other tools, reduce latency by limiting repeated reasoning. The stress-test results further show that the model with advanced tool-calling capability maintains reliable execution over longer procedures than the other evaluated models; however, all models exhibit reliability degradation as procedure length increases, revealing clear execution limits in multi-step tool-based workflows. To systematically analyze failures in procedure execution, we introduce a procedure-specific error taxonomy that categorizes deviations in multi-step procedural execution.

</details>


<div id='cs.SD'></div>

# cs.SD [[Back]](#toc)

### [847] [MedMosaic: A Challenging Large Scale Benchmark of Diverse Medical Audio](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00969)
*Harshit Rajgarhia,Shuubham Ojha,Asif Shaik,Akhil Pothanapalli,Rachuri Lokesh,Abhishek Mukherji,Prasanna Desikan*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: MedMosaic is a novel medical audio question-answering dataset that enables systematic evaluation of language and audio reasoning models. It highlights challenges in medical audio reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks do not adequately represent complex medical audio scenarios due to difficulties in collecting and annotating medical audio data caused by privacy and domain expertise constraints.

Method: MedMosaic compiles diverse medical audio, including physiological sounds, synthetic voices, and real clinical conversations. It features 46,701 question-answer pairs in multiple question types for evaluating reasoning and answer generation capabilities.

Result: Evaluation of 13 models reveals that even state-of-the-art systems struggle with medical reasoning, showing only 68.1% accuracy for Gemini-2.5-pro, and substantial variation across question types.

Conclusion: There is a pressing need for more robust and domain-specific multimodal reasoning models to overcome limitations in medical audio reasoning.

Abstract: We present MedMosaic, a medical audio question-answering dataset designed to benchmark language and audio reasoning models under realistic clinical constraints. Medical audio data is difficult to collect due to privacy regulations and high annotation costs arising from domain expertise. Thus, existing benchmarks tend to underrepresent complex medical audio scenarios. To address these challenges, MedMosaic features a diverse range of medical audio types, including condition-related physiological sounds, carefully constructed synthetic voices to mimic speech with artifacts as well as real short and long length clinical conversations to model varying context lengths. The dataset also features a total of 46,701 question-answer pairs, spanning categories such as multiple-choice, sequential multi-turn, and open-ended question-answers, enabling systematic evaluation of multi-hop reasoning and answer generation capabilities. Benchmarking 13 audio and multimodal reasoning models reveals that reasoning remains challenging for all evaluated systems, with substantial performance variation across question types. In particular, even state-of-the-art model like Gemini-2.5-pro can only achieve 68.1% accuracy approximately. These findings underscore persistent limitations in medical reasoning and highlight the need for more robust, domain-specific multimodal reasoning models.

</details>


### [848] [Spoken Language Identification with Pre-trained Models and Margin Loss](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01905)
*Zhihua Fang,Liang He,Weiwu Jiang*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: This paper addresses speaker-controlled language identification using pre-trained ECAPA-TDNN models with margin-based losses, achieving substantial improvements in accuracy and error rate metrics over the baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to improve speaker-controlled language identification by addressing challenges posed by non-linguistic factors, such as speaker characteristics, through enhanced feature representation.

Method: The method employs a pre-trained ECAPA-TDNN as the feature encoder combined with margin-based losses to maximize the discriminative power and inter-class separability of language representations.

Result: The proposed approach achieves 85.95% macro accuracy, 90.96% micro accuracy, and a 17.08% equal error rate (EER), surpassing the baseline significantly in all measures.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that the integration of pre-trained models and margin-based losses improves language identification accuracy and reduces error, validating the proposed method's efficacy.

Abstract: For the speaker-controlled spoken language identification task proposed in the TidyLang Challenge 2026, this paper proposes a language identification method based on pre-trained models and margin-based losses. The proposed method adopts a pre-trained ECAPA-TDNN as the feature encoder and incorporates margin-based losses to enhance the discriminative ability of language representations, thereby improving inter-class separability and reducing the interference of non-linguistic factors such as speaker characteristics. Experimental results on the Tidy-X dataset show that the proposed method achieves 85.95% macro accuracy and 90.96% micro accuracy on the language identification task and 17.08% equal error rate (EER) on the verification task. Compared with the official baseline, the macro accuracy improves by 45.7%, the micro accuracy improves by 15.2%, and the EER is reduced by approximately 50.8%, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method. The code will be released at https://github.com/PunkMale/TidyLang2026.

</details>


### [849] [Tibetan-TTS:Low-Resource Tibetan Speech Synthesis with Large Model Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02496)
*Jiaxu He,Chao Wang,Jie Lian,Yuqing Cai,Yongxiang Li,Renzeg Duojie,Jie Li*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper introduces a Tibetan TTS system using a large speech synthesis model to address low resources, dialectal variability, and complex phonetic-text mapping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to tackle challenges in Tibetan TTS, including limited speech data, significant dialect variations, and the intricate alignment between text and spoken forms.

Method: The system integrates large-model-based speech synthesis, data quality enhancement, Tibetan-specific text representation and tokenizer adaptation, and cross-lingual adaptive training.

Result: The TTS system achieves high MOS scores (4.28 for syllable-based and 4.35 for BPE-based) with pronunciation accuracies of 97.6% and 96.6%, surpassing an external commercial Tibetan TTS interface.

Conclusion: The integration of large-model backbones with Tibetan-specific adaptations enables natural and intelligible Tibetan speech synthesis and lays the groundwork for future multi-dialect modeling.

Abstract: Tibetan text-to-speech (TTS) has long been challenged by scarce speech resources, significant dialectal variation, and the complex mapping between written text and spoken pronunciation. To address these issues, this work presents, to the best of our knowledge, the first large-model-based Tibetan TTS system in the industry, built upon a large speech synthesis model developed by Xingchen AGI Lab. The proposed system integrates data quality enhancement, Tibetan-oriented text representation and tokenizer adaptation, and cross-lingual adaptive training for low-resource Tibetan speech synthesis. Experimental results show that the system can generate stable, natural, and intelligible Tibetan speech under low-resource conditions. In subjective evaluation, the MOS scores of the syllable-level and BPE-based systems reach 4.28 and 4.35, while their pronunciation accuracies reach 97.6% and 96.6%, respectively, outperforming an external commercial Tibetan TTS interface. These results demonstrate that combining a large-model backbone with Tibetan-oriented text representation adaptation and cross-lingual adaptive training enables highly usable low-resource Tibetan speech synthesis, and also provides a technical foundation for future unified multi-dialect Tibetan speech synthesis.

</details>


### [850] [MindMelody: A Closed-Loop EEG-Driven System for Personalized Music Intervention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01235)
*Yimeng Zhang,Yueru Sun,Haoyu Gu*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: MindMelody is a real-time system using EEG signals for personalized music-based emotion regulation, addressing challenges in adaptation and data scarcity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to improve emotion regulation and stress relief through adaptive, personalized music interventions, addressing limitations of current music services based on static preferences.

Method: MindMelody uses a hybrid Transformer-GNN model to decode EEG signals, RAG-equipped LLM for intervention structuring, and a Hierarchical EEG Controller for music synthesis guided by user dynamics.

Result: Experiments show MindMelody enhances control adherence, emotional alignment, and perceived helpfulness in short-term usage.

Conclusion: MindMelody demonstrates promise as an adaptive, EEG-driven music generation system for personalized emotion regulation.

Abstract: Driven by the escalating global burden of mental health conditions, music-based interventions have attracted significant attention as a non-invasive, cost-effective modality for emotion regulation and psychological stress relief. However, current digital music services rely on static preferences and fail to adapt to users' instantaneous psychological states. Furthermore, directly mapping electroencephalography (EEG) to music generation remains challenging due to severe paired-data scarcity and a lack of interpretability. To address these limitations, we propose MindMelody, a fully functional, closed-loop real-time system for EEG-driven personalized music intervention. MindMelody introduces an emotion-mediated semantic bridge. Specifically, a hybrid Transformer-GNN first decodes real-time EEG signals into global Valence-Arousal states and local temporal affect trajectories. These states are then fed into a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)-equipped Large Language Model (LLM) to formulate structured intervention plans. Subsequently, a novel Hierarchical EEG Controller injects global affect prefixes and local temporal guidance into a pretrained music backbone, enabling fine-grained controllable audio synthesis. Crucially, the system incorporates a continuous feedback loop that updates generation parameters on the fly based on the user's evolving EEG dynamics. Extensive experiments show that MindMelody improves control adherence and emotional alignment, and receives higher perceived helpfulness in a short-term listening setting, suggesting its promise as an adaptive affect-aware music generation framework.

</details>


### [851] [Toward Fine-Grained Speech Inpainting Forensics:A Dataset, Method, and Metric for Multi-Region Tampering Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02223)
*Tung Vu,Yen Nguyen,Hai Nguyen,Cuong Pham,Cong Tran*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper targets the problem of detecting localized tampering in speech (2-7% of content), introduces a new dataset (MIST), and proposes a novel detection framework (ISA) which outperforms existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a critical need to detect localized speech tampering as advancing technology in voice cloning makes it harder to identify small, malicious manipulations.

Method: The authors introduce MIST, a multilingual dataset, and propose ISA, a framework utilizing iterative segment analysis with sliding-window classification for precise tampered-region detection.

Result: The ISA framework outperforms existing detectors, highlighting their inefficiency in identifying partially manipulated speech at a granular level, particularly in zero-shot scenarios.

Conclusion: Localized speech tampering, especially involving small percentage manipulations, remains unsolved by current methods, but ISA offers a superior detection framework. The tools and dataset are open for public use.

Abstract: Recent advances in voice cloning and text-to-speech synthesis have made partial speech manipulation - where an adversary replaces a few words within an utterance to alter its meaning while preserving the speaker's identity - an increasingly realistic threat. Existing audio deepfake detection benchmarks focus on utterance-level binary classification or single-region tampering, leaving a critical gap in detecting and localizing multiple inpainted segments whose count is unknown a priori. We address this gap with three contributions. First, we introduce MIST (Multiregion Inpainting Speech Tampering), a large-scale multilingual dataset spanning 6 languages with 1-3 independently inpainted word-level segments per utterance, generated via LLM-guided semantic replacement and neural voice cloning, with fake content constituting only 2-7% of each utterance. Second, we propose ISA (Iterative Segment Analysis), a backbone-agnostic framework that performs coarse-to-fine sliding-window classification with gap-tolerant region proposal and boundary refinement to recover all tampered regions without prior knowledge of their count. Third, we define SF1@tau, a segment-level F1 metric based on temporal IoU matching that jointly evaluates region count accuracy and localization precision. Zero-shot evaluation reveals that partial inpainting at word granularity remains unsolved by existing deepfake detectors: utterance-level classifiers trained on fully synthesized speech assign near zero fake probability to MIST utterances where only 2-7% of content is manipulated. ISA consistently outperforms non-iterative baselines in this challenging setting, and the dataset, code, and evaluation toolkit are publicly released.

</details>


### [852] [Khala: Scaling Acoustic Token Language Models Toward High-Fidelity Music Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01790)
*Jiafeng Liu,Yuanliang Dong,Hongjia Liu,Yuqing Cheng,Zhancheng Guo,Huijing Liang,Wenbo Zhan,Yuming Sun,Xiaobing Li,Feng Yu,Maosong Sun*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper proposes an innovative approach to music generation by unifying structure and fidelity modeling within a single acoustic-token hierarchy, avoiding the need for separate representation spaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To simplify and improve high-quality music generation processes by modeling both structure and fidelity in a unified framework instead of using separate representation spaces.

Method: The authors develop a 64-layer residual vector quantization (RVQ) acoustic representation and implement a two-stage coarse-to-fine generation framework. They also introduce hybrid-attention training for better lyric alignment and fidelity.

Result: Both text-vocal alignment and audio refinement are achieved within the acoustic-token language modeling framework. The super-resolution model initialized from the backbone model shows improved convergence and overall quality.

Conclusion: Music generation can be effectively accomplished without separating structure and fidelity into different representation spaces, offering a unified approach for enhanced simplicity and quality.

Abstract: A common design pattern in high-quality music generation is to handle structure and fidelity in different representation spaces: a generator first models high-level structure, followed by diffusion-based or neural decoding stages that reconstruct fine details. In this work, we explore an alternative view: both may be progressively modeled within a single deep acoustic-token hierarchy. To study this, we build a 64-layer residual vector quantization (RVQ) acoustic representation and propose a two-stage coarse-to-fine generation framework. A backbone model first generates coarse acoustic tokens for the full track, and a super-resolution model then completes finer tokens within the same acoustic token space. The super-resolution stage works at full-track scale and refines tokens layer by layer while running in parallel over time, leading to a fixed 62-step inference process. To jointly improve lyric alignment and fine-detail reconstruction, we further introduce hybrid-attention training: the alignment objective uses causal attention, while layer-wise refinement uses full attention. A key finding is that text--vocal alignment can emerge within pure acoustic-token language modeling, without requiring a separate semantic token stage. Moreover, initializing the super-resolution model from the trained backbone significantly improves convergence and final quality. Taken together, our results suggest that high-quality music generation can be effectively pursued without separating structure and fidelity into heterogeneous representation spaces. Instead, both can be progressively modeled within a unified acoustic-token hierarchy, pointing toward a simpler and more unified path to high-quality music generation.

</details>


### [853] [TMD-Bench: A Multi-Level Evaluation Paradigm for Music-Dance Co-Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01809)
*Xiaoda Yang,Majun Zhang,Changhao Pan,Nick Huang,Yang Yuguang,Fan Zhuo,Pengfei Zhou,Jin Zhou,Sizhe Shan,Shan Yang,Miles Yang,Yang You,Zhou Zhao*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper introduces TMD-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating text-driven music-dance co-generation systems focusing on rhythmic alignment, instruction adherence, and unimodal quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The task of music-dance co-generation requires fine temporal resolution for rhythmic coupling, which is not adequately captured by existing metrics for audio-visual synthesis.

Method: Developed TMD-Bench with computable physical metrics and perceptual multimodal evaluations, supported by a rhythm-aligned dataset and a structured music captioning tool.

Result: Modern models achieve high-quality unimodal outputs but lack optimal rhythmic coupling. The proposed RhyJAM baseline improves beat-level synchronization while maintaining unimodal quality.

Conclusion: TMD-Bench highlights challenges and opportunities for creating next-generation models that achieve rhythmic and kinetic coherence.

Abstract: Unified audio-visual generation is rapidly gaining industrial and creative relevance, enabling applications in virtual production and interactive media. However, when moving from general audio-video synthesis to music-dance co-generation, the task becomes substantially harder: musical rhythm, phrasing, and accents must drive choreographic motion at fine temporal resolution, and such rhythmic coupling is not captured by unimodal metrics or generic audiovisual consistency scores used in current evaluation practice. We introduce TMD-Bench, a benchmark for text-driven music-dance co-generation that assesses systems across unimodal generation quality, instruction adherence, and cross-modal rhythmic alignment. The benchmark integrates computable physical metrics with perceptual multimodal judgments, and is supported by a curated rhythm-aligned music-dance dataset and a fine-grained Music Captioner for structured music semantics. TMD-Bench further reveals that (i) modern commercial audio-visual models, such as Veo 3 and Sora 2, produce high-quality music and video, while rhythmic coupling remains less consistently optimized and leaves room for improvement, and (ii) our unified baseline RhyJAM trained on rhythm-aligned data achieves competitive beat-level synchronization while maintaining competitive unimodal fidelity. This presents prospects for building next-generation music-dance models that explicitly optimize rhythmic and kinetic coherence.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.QM'></div>

# q-bio.QM [[Back]](#toc)

### [854] [Co-Generative De Novo Functional Protein Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00948)
*Xinrui Chen,Yizhen Luo,Siqi Fan,Zaiqing Nie*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: CodeFP is a new protein language model for designing functional proteins de novo, addressing issues in functionality and foldability by simultaneously decoding sequence and structure tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: De novo protein design struggles to meet both functionality and foldability due to limitations in current direct and decoupled generation strategies.

Method: CodeFP co-generatively decodes sequence and structure tokens while utilizing functional local structures for enriched encodings and adding auxiliary functional supervision during training.

Result: CodeFP outperforms benchmarks with a 6.1% improvement in functional consistency and a 3.2% increase in foldability.

Conclusion: Simultaneous decoding of sequence and structure improves the design of functional and foldable proteins, making CodeFP a promising tool for biotechnology and medicine.

Abstract: De novo functional protein design aims to generate protein sequences that realize specified biochemical functions without relying on evolutionary templates, enabling broad applications in biotechnology and medicine. Existing approaches adopt either direct function-to-sequence mapping or decoupled structure-sequence generation strategies but often fail to achieve functionality and foldability simultaneously. To address this, we propose CodeFP, a Co-generative protein language model for de novo Functional Protein design that simultaneously decodes sequence and structure tokens, thereby enabling superior simultaneous realization of functionality and foldability. CodeFP utilizes functional local structures to enrich functional semantic encodings, overcoming the suboptimal translation of flat encodings into structure tokens, while introducing auxiliary functional supervision to alleviate training ambiguity stemming from the one-to-many structure-to-token mapping. Extensive experiments show that CodeFP consistently achieves average improvements of 6.1% in functional consistency and 3.2% in foldability over the strongest baseline.

</details>


<div id='cs.LO'></div>

# cs.LO [[Back]](#toc)

### [855] [A uniform characterisation of the (a)synchronous must-preorder](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02362)
*Giovanni Bernardi,Hugo Férée,Gaëtan Lopez*

Main category: cs.LO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a unifying axiomatic approach to characterizing De Nicola and Hennessy's must-preorder, applicable across synchronous/asynchronous and value/non-value-passing communication settings, addressing inconsistencies in prior approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify and simplify the theoretical framework around De Nicola and Hennessy's must-preorder and address inconsistencies caused by its varied definitions.

Method: The paper employs a calculus-independent axiomatic approach, emphasizing blocking and non-blocking actions and introducing the notion of label abstraction for reasoning about safe substitutivity.

Result: The authors present a single alternative characterization of the must-preorder, with soundness and completeness proofs applicable across all four communication settings, mechanized in Rocq.

Conclusion: The proposed framework generalizes and simplifies the theory, consolidating existing results into a uniform approach and aiding in better reasoning and tool development for must-preorder analysis.

Abstract: In the setting of message passing software, De Nicola and Hennessy must-preorder defines when a program improves on another one. Since this preorder does not come equipped with a viable proof method, using it requires an alternative relation that characterises it.  The literature presents at least four different definitions of such alternative preorders, depending on whether communication is synchronous or asynchronous and on whether there is value-passing or not. The existence of these different definitions complicates the overall theory, hinders the development of tools, and, upon the whole, suggests a lack of understanding of the properties necessary and sufficient to reason on the must-preorder.  This paper presents the first alternative characterisation that works at least in all the four settings mentioned above. We achieve this result thanks to an axiomatic approach that is calculus independent, by highlighting the role of blocking and non-blocking actions, and by introducing the novel notion of label abstraction. Label abstractions capture the essence of safe substitutivity w.r.t. interactions, and they let us obtain a unique proof of soundness and a unique proof of completeness that work in all the mentioned settings. We believe this generalises and simplifies the overall theory, while letting us present the existing results in a uniform way.  Our proofs are constructive and our result is entirely mechanised in Rocq.

</details>


### [856] [ABox Abduction for Inconsistent Knowledge Bases under Repair Semantics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01341)
*Anselm Haak,Patrick Koopmann,Yasir Mahmood,Anni-Yasmin Turhan*

Main category: cs.LO

TL;DR: The paper tackles the ABox abduction problem in the context of inconsistent knowledge bases (KBs), introducing repair semantics and analyzing complexity for DL-Lite and EL_bot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ABox abduction has seen limited exploration in the context of inconsistent KBs despite its importance in diagnosis, explainability, and data repair.

Method: Introduces repair semantics to enable reasoning with inconsistent KBs and proposes abduction criteria, analyzing its complexity for lightweight description logics.

Result: Defined abduction notions under repair semantics, presented abduction criteria, and mapped out the problem's complexity for DL-Lite and EL_bot.

Conclusion: ABox abduction under repair semantics provides a meaningful framework for reasoning in inconsistent KBs, with complexity thoroughly analyzed for lightweight description logics.

Abstract: Given a knowledge base (KB) with a non-entailed fact, the ABox abduction problem asks for possible extensions of the KB that would entail this fact. This problem has many applications, ranging from diagnosis to explainability and repair. ABox abduction has been well-investigated for consistent KBs and classical semantics, but little is known for the case of inconsistent KBs, which can be caused by erroneous data. In this paper we define suitable notions of abduction in this setting and propose criteria that guide abduction towards "useful" hypotheses. To regain meaningful reasoning in the presence of inconsistencies, we use well-established repair semantics. We provide a comprehensive landscape of the complexity of ABox abduction under repair semantics, treating different variants of the abduction problem for the light-weight description logics DL-Lite and EL_bot.

</details>


### [857] [Static Analysis of Recursive SHACL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02787)
*Anouk Oudshoorn,Magdalena Ortiz,Mantas Simkus*

Main category: cs.LO

TL;DR: The paper addresses the problem of determining whether all RDF data graphs that validate one SHACL document also validate another, discovering undecidability under stable and supported model semantics but decidability under well-founded semantics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is currently no static analysis approach to compare SHACL documents, despite their widespread use for validating RDF data.

Method: The study extends previous work by analyzing SHACL document containment, introducing a translation from SHACL under well-founded semantics to a hybrid mu-calculus framework. The analysis then employs automata-based decision procedures for evaluation.

Result: The research determines that document containment is undecidable under stable and supported semantics, but decidable in single exponential time under well-founded semantics.

Conclusion: This work establishes computational boundaries for SHACL document comparison and bridges SHACL semantics with modal fixed-point logics. It offers a concrete, decidable method for document analysis under well-founded semantics.

Abstract: SHACL (Shapes Constraint Language) expresses constraints on RDF data by means of so-called shapes. Its central service is validation: verifying whether a data graph complies with a SHACL document. But so far, there are no static analysis services to compare documents. In this paper, we study the following problem: decide whether all graphs that validate one SHACL document also validate another. Unlike previous works that have considered the implication of shape expressions only, we consider documents comprising (recursive) shape definitions and targets. We show that implication (a.k.a. containment) is undecidable under the supported and the stable model semantics, even for the fragment that uses the description logic ALCIO for shape expressions. Under the well-founded semantics, in surprising contrast, it is decidable in single exponential time. Our key technical contribution is a translation of SHACL under the well-founded semantics into the full hybrid mu-calculus, revealing a novel link between well-founded models and a fixed point modal logic, and a worst-case optimal automata-based decision procedure.

</details>


<div id='math.CA'></div>

# math.CA [[Back]](#toc)

### [858] [Exact Loop Controllers for ReLU Realization of Homogeneous Curve Refinements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.01655)
*Boldsaikhan Bolorkhuu,Tsogtgerel Gantumur*

Main category: math.CA

TL;DR: The paper studies homogeneous refinement operators that act on compactly supported continuous piecewise linear curves, proving that these operators admit exact ReLU realizations with fixed width and linear depth. It introduces a novel loop controller architecture for handling residual dynamics geometrically.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The aim is to develop exact ReLU realizations for iterates of homogeneous refinement operators, responding to challenges in scalar and vector-valued refinable function constructions, with applications in geometry and network architectures.

Method: Homogeneous refinement operators are analyzed using a novel geometric approach based on loop controllers to handle residual dynamics. The technique transports residual orbits on polygonal loops, incorporating CPwL readouts and scalar selector mechanisms for precise modeling.

Result: Exact ReLU realizations of fixed width and depth proportional to iteration steps are achieved. Additionally, exponential bounds on network weights are provided, and affine forcing terms are decomposed into homogeneous iterates, achieving compact realizations.

Conclusion: The paper provides a robust geometric control framework for exact ReLU realization of homogeneous refinement operators, offering new insights into scalar and vector-valued constructions with broader geometric and network architecture implications.

Abstract: We study homogeneous refinement operators \((Vγ)(t)=\sum_{j\in\mathbb Z}A_jγ(Mt-j)\), acting on compactly supported continuous piecewise linear curves \(γ:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R^p\), where \(M\ge2\) and only finitely many matrices \(A_j\in\mathbb R^{p\times p}\) are nonzero. We prove that the iterates \(V^nγ\) admit exact ReLU realizations of fixed width and depth \(O(n)\).
  The main new ingredient is an exact loop controller for the residual dynamics. Instead of propagating scalar residual surrogates, the construction transports the residual orbit by a forward-exact state on a polygonal loop. Scalar factors and digit selectors are then recovered from this loop state by complementary CPwL readouts. The loop seam is not removed, but its remaining ambiguity is confined to the final readout/selector stage, where it is harmless because the scalar atom is supported away from the seam. This gives a homogeneous \(M\)-ary vector-valued extension of the scalar binary refinable-function construction with a more geometric controller architecture.
  We also record crude exponential bounds on the network weights and biases. Affine forcing terms are handled by expanding affine iterates into finite sums of homogeneous iterates, giving exact fixed-width realizations with depth \(O(n^2)\), and anchored open curves reduce to compactly supported defects with affine anchor mismatch. We also describe homogeneous polygonal generators, including dragon-type examples and a self-intersecting Hilbert-type prototype in arbitrary dimension. The extended version includes stage-dependent forcing, finite-state stacking reductions, and further geometric constructions such as Koch-, Gosper-, Morton-, and connector-based Hilbert-type variants.

</details>


<div id='eess.AS'></div>

# eess.AS [[Back]](#toc)

### [859] [Voice Mapping of Text-to-Speech Systems: A Metric-Based Approach for Voice Quality Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.00861)
*Huanchen Cai,Sten Ternström*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: The paper evaluates six TTS models using metrics like crest factor, spectrum balance, and CPPs, showing VITS has the best voice range and Glow-TTS excels in soft phonation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess and compare TTS models' performance in terms of voice dynamics and expressiveness using a new evaluation framework called voice mapping.

Method: The paper analyzed six TTS models using crest factor, spectrum balance, and cepstral peak prominence (CPPs) as metrics for voice quality and expressive performance.

Result: VITS demonstrated the largest voice range among tested models, while Glow-TTS showed better performance in softer phonation. Additionally, CPPs values were found to be linked to natural or robotic voice perception.

Conclusion: Voice mapping serves as an effective framework for evaluating TTS model quality in terms of vocal effort, dynamics, and expressiveness, emphasizing its importance for future TTS development.

Abstract: This study investigates voice mapping as an evaluation framework for text-to-speech (TTS) synthesis quality. The study analyzes six TTS models, including historical and recent ones. The metrics are crest factor, spectrum balance, and cepstral peak prominence (CPPs). We investigated 6 influential TTS models: Merlin, Tacotron 2, Transformer TTS, FastSpeech 2, Glow-TTS, and VITS. The results demonstrate that voice range serves as a primary indicator of model capability, with VITS showing the largest range among tested models. Glow-TTS exhibited superior performance in soft phonation, indicated by higher spectrum balance, despite limited voice range. The results showed that the CPPs values between 7-8 dB indicate natural voice quality, while with CPPs exceeding 10 dB, the speech tends to sound robotic.
